                                                        Chapter 3
                The Transformation of
                Healthcare Marketing in
                 the New Digital Era:
                          A Comprehensive Analysis
                           With Reference to China

                                                         Mohamad Zreik
                                               https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6812-6529
                                                  Sun Yat-sen University, China


ABSTRACT
Focusing on China’s distinct market, this chapter delves into how healthcare marketing has evolved in
the digital age. It analyzes how developments in AI, big data analytics, augmented reality, blockchain,
and mobile technologies are fueling a transition toward a consumer-centric health culture. This chapter
explores the changing demands and preferences of the Chinese healthcare market, the importance of
value-driven marketing tactics, and the function of customer relationship management. Ethical concerns,
pricing concerns, and success metrics are also discussed. This chapter is a must-read for anybody with
even a passing interest in the rapidly growing field of digital healthcare, since it provides a thorough
overview of the history, current applications, and future directions of digital healthcare in China.



1. INTRODUCTION

Marketing in the healthcare business is a multifaceted profession that draws from a wide range of disci-
plines to advertise and sell goods and services. Since the middle of the twentieth century, this field has
expanded at an exponential rate, paralleling the rise of healthcare consumerism. Promoting medical items
and drugs was an early focus of healthcare marketing, with doctors frequently serving as the intended
audience (Gunjan et al., 2015).
    As the industry developed, however, attention turned from marketing products to marketing services
like those offered by hospitals, clinics, and wellness centers. Marketing strategies were implemented
DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch003



       Copyright © 2024, IGI Global. Copying or distributing in print or electronic forms without written permission of IGI Global is prohibited.
﻿
The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




by hospitals in an effort to improve their standing in the eyes of patients, healthcare professionals, and
insurance companies. The key goals were to increase the number of new patients, keep the current pa-
tients happy, and boost the hospital’s standing in the community.
    The digital revolution has brought about even more radical changes in healthcare advertising. While
there is still a place for more traditional types of advertising, digital channels have begun to supplement
and even supplant them. In order to connect with patients, healthcare organizations now need to imple-
ment digital marketing methods such as search engine optimization (SEO), social media marketing,
content marketing, email marketing, and online reputation management (Desai & Vidyapeeth, 2019).
    In addition, the widespread availability of the internet and cellphones has given consumers greater
access to healthcare information, allowing them to become more involved, knowledgeable, and engaged in
their own healthcare decisions (Carrera & Dalton, 2014). Patient-centric marketing, which puts patients’
wants, needs, and values first, emerged as a result of the rise in consumer activism (Hernandez, 2009).
Therefore, current healthcare marketing takes a more comprehensive strategy, emphasizing not just the
promotion of healthcare products and services but also consumer education and awareness.
    As a result, healthcare marketing’s history is one of constant change, as new technologies and prefer-
ences have shaped the industry. Engaging patients, educating them, and promoting health products and
services ethically are now at the center of the business, which once focused on the product.
    The introduction of the digital age has significantly modified the promotion and distribution of
healthcare services. Thanks to globalization and technological progress, there is now a generation of
healthcare customers that are well-versed in their options and take an active role in making decisions
about their care.
    The widespread adoption of the Internet and smartphones has forced a change from conventional
to digital healthcare marketing strategies. The term “digital marketing” encompasses a wide range of
techniques, such as search engine optimization (SEO), content marketing, social media engagement,
email marketing, and online reputation management (Bala & Verma, 2018). Improved patient satisfac-
tion can result from the use of these methods because healthcare professionals can communicate with a
wider audience, interact with customers on a more human level, and offer timely information or support.
    Information is the driving force behind this shift. The use of artificial intelligence (AI) and big data
analytics has helped healthcare professionals learn more about their patients’ habits, preferences, and
requirements (Chattu, 2021). These findings can be used to build individualized health promotion cam-
paigns that better meet the needs of each patient.
    Healthcare services are now more accessible than ever thanks to innovations like telehealth and
mobile health (mHealth) apps. Patients are able to schedule appointments, monitor their health, and
learn more about their problems through the use of these online systems. Telehealth’s ability to reach
underprivileged areas has been crucial to expanding access to quality healthcare (Haleem et al., 2021).
    In addition, AR and VR have broken through in the healthcare marketing sphere by presenting fresh
methods of showcasing products and services and of educating healthcare professionals. Blockchain
technology also has great potential to protect patient information and strengthen the credibility of health-
care advertising (Chakraborty, 2022).
    Another major shift brought on by the information age is the expansion of value-based healthcare (Da
Silva Etges et al., 2020). It has transformed healthcare marketing techniques to place greater emphasis
on quality of care and the patient experience rather than service volume.
    There are many reasons why China is a focal point in the international conversation on how health-
care marketing will evolve in the digital age. As the world’s most populous country, it offers a massive


                                                                                                         43
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                       The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




opportunity for medical tourism. High-quality, conveniently accessible healthcare is in high demand due
to rising middle-class populations and an aging population.
    China has made great achievements in the field of digital healthcare during the past several years.
Due to technological developments and government initiatives, its healthcare market is becoming in-
creasingly digitized. As part of its “Healthy China 2030” goal, the Chinese government has promoted
digital innovation in the healthcare sector to increase healthcare’s availability and affordability (Tan,
Liu, & Shao, 2017).
    Tech giants like Alibaba and Tencent in China are making significant investments in digital health-
care by building platforms that link millions of people with healthcare providers. Millions of people use
sites like Tencent’s WeDoctor and Alibaba’s AliHealth, which allow them to consult with doctors, get
prescriptions, and schedule appointments online (Harrison, 2021).
    China also actively employs cutting-edge technologies in healthcare, including artificial intelli-
gence, big data, and mobile technology. Disease diagnosis, medication research, patient monitoring,
and predictive analytics are just some of the many uses of artificial intelligence and big data in China
(Chattu, 2021). A large number of people in the country now own smartphones, which has stimulated
the development of mobile health apps.
    In addition, China’s hybrid public-private healthcare system can teach us a lot about how to advertise
healthcare services in other countries. The country’s healthcare market is as varied as its population,
ranging from huge urban hospitals to rural clinics, providing valuable insight into the ways in which
healthcare marketing strategies can be adapted to serve a wide variety of populations and settings (Reyn-
olds & McKee, 2011).
    As such, the development and trajectory of healthcare marketing in the digital age can be studied
effectively by looking at China as a case study. Other countries attempting to digitally modernize their
healthcare systems should learn from its experiences and strategies.


2. A BRIEF HISTORY OF HEALTHCARE MARKETING IN CHINA

2.1. Overview of the Evolution

Over the past few decades, healthcare advertising in China has seen significant changes in response to the
country’s rapid economic and social development. Before the late 20th century, the Chinese government
had a significant role in providing and overseeing the country’s healthcare system (Yu et al., 2010). As
a result, conventional healthcare marketing was essentially nonexistent at the time.
    Market liberalization and economic reforms ushered in a new era in the 1980s. As the private sector
became more involved in healthcare, competition grew (Wagstaff, 2009). Because of this, marketing
tactics were required to win over clients and set apart from competitors. However, early healthcare mar-
keting efforts were limited to more conceptually straightforward forms of promotion, and healthcare
consumerism was still in its infancy (Wang, 2010).
    The arrival of the digital age in the latter part of the 2000s marked a watershed moment. Consum-
ers’ access to information and services was revolutionized by the widespread use of the internet and the
smartphone. The promise of digital platforms to connect with and engage consumers has slowly dawned
on tech businesses, startups, and traditional healthcare providers. There was a tipping point in health-




44
﻿
The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




care marketing when the popularity of mobile health apps, online consultations, and health information
platforms skyrocketed.
   There has been a dramatic increase in the number of digital healthcare projects in China during
the past decade. Alibaba and Tencent, two major tech businesses, have launched healthcare portals,
AliHealth and WeDoctor, respectively. Millions of people across the country benefit from the services
offered by these websites, which range from virtual doctor visits to the distribution of pharmaceuticals
(Harrison, 2021). At the same time, healthcare facilities have become avid users of digital marketing
tools like websites, social media, and online advertising to draw in new customers and keep existing
ones coming back.
   Healthcare marketing using cutting-edge tools such as AI and big data has also been pioneered in
China. By analyzing patient behavior, predicting health trends, and tailoring marketing strategies, these
technologies improve the involvement and pleasure of patients.

2.2. Current Status and Role of Technology

At the present time, China is a world leader in the digital health sector, having made extensive use of
technology in healthcare advertising (Wang et al., 2021). A vibrant digital health ecosystem has emerged
thanks to the growing use of internet services and mobile device usage. The convergence of shifting
demographics, rising healthcare expenditures, and innovative technologies has created a fertile environ-
ment for growth in China’s digital health business.
    China’s healthcare advertising is being revolutionized by tech giants like Alibaba, Tencent, and
Baidu. They’ve developed websites that offer a wide range of digital medical services like virtual doctor
visits, patient records storage, appointment scheduling, and medication management (Yip & McKern,
2016). These sites double as advertising avenues, connecting healthcare professionals with vast pools
of prospective patients.
    Healthcare advertising in China now relies heavily on AI and big data. In order to better understand
patient habits, preferences, and industry health trends, healthcare providers and tech firms use AI algo-
rithms and big data analytics (Thomason, 2021). Insights like this are priceless for tailoring marketing
strategies to each individual client and for using in healthcare to boost efficiency and effectiveness.
    Healthcare marketers are finding uses for not only AI and big data, but also augmented reality (AR),
virtual reality (VR), and blockchain. By providing patients with realistic simulations of medical procedures
or the inner workings of a medicine, AR and VR can empower them to make more educated healthcare
decisions (Haleem et al., 2022). However, blockchain technology can improve healthcare transaction
security and transparency, which will increase patient and customer confidence.
    The COVID-19 pandemic has contributed to China’s already rapid expansion of telemedicine (Zreik,
2023). It’s a great way to get the word out about your practice while also helping patients, especially
those in outlying locations, get the care they need more easily and quickly. Healthcare professionals
and patients can market their services and develop trusting connections through the use of telemedicine
platforms that allow for direct communication.
    There is a plethora of fitness tracking and chronic disease management apps available on mobile
devices. These apps amass huge troves of user information, which can be used for personalized advertis-
ing and in-app purchases.




                                                                                                         45
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                       The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




3. THE EVOLUTION OF NEEDS, WANTS, AND DEMANDS
IN CHINA’S HEALTHCARE MARKET

3.1. Understanding the Changing Healthcare Consumer

Successful healthcare marketing requires an in-depth familiarity with the ever-evolving requirements of
healthcare consumers. Rapid economic development, population shifts, urbanization, increasing digital
access, and a shifting illness burden are all factors that have affected these aspects in China.
    The growing middle class in China is a direct result of the country’s rapid economic development
over the past few decades. The rising middle class in China has brought with it higher standards for
medical care. In addition to cost-effective treatment, they prioritize individualized attention (Azam et
al., 2019). Consumers in China’s modern healthcare market value speed, price transparency, and ease
of access above everything else.
    The rising aging of China’s population is another big shift. An estimated 24% of the Chinese popula-
tion will be 60 or older by the year 2025 (Tan et al., 2020). Because of this change in the population’s
make-up, more people are in need of geriatric care services such long-term care, rehabilitation, and
management of chronic diseases.
    Consumers in China have become increasingly active participants in their healthcare decisions as
internet availability and smartphone usage have increased. The wealth of resources available to them
online in the realm of health care allows people to make well-informed decisions about their treatment
(Thapa et al., 2021). Due to this change, digital health services including telemedicine, online pharma-
cies, and mobile health apps have become increasingly popular (Wang et al., 2022).
    As Chinese society has changed, so too has the composition of its diseases. Urbanization, sedentary
lifestyles, and bad diets have all contributed to the rise in the prevalence of non-communicable diseases
(NCDs) like diabetes, heart disease, and cancer (Reubi et al., 2016). The importance of preventative
treatment, early diagnosis, and ongoing management of many illnesses has grown as a result of this trend.
    Healthcare marketers in China, in response to these shifting demands, have adjusted their approaches.
Value-based, patient-centered care has become a priority for them. Personalized, convenient, and effec-
tive healthcare delivery has become possible thanks in large part to the widespread adoption of digital
technologies.

3.2. Implications for Healthcare Marketing

For healthcare providers, governments, IT companies, and other players in the healthcare sector, the
digital transformation of healthcare marketing in the era of China has far-reaching ramifications.
   To begin, it is essential for healthcare providers to have a strong online presence in light of the grow-
ing importance of digital platforms. For this reason, it’s important to have a search engine optimization
(SEO) plan, an active social media presence, and a user-friendly website. To expand their customer
base, service providers could also promote their offerings through internet ads and email campaigns.
Given the proliferation of mobile devices, it is essential that content and services be optimized for them.
   Second, there is a great potential for more targeted advertising thanks to the availability of big data
and AI. Healthcare providers can increase consumer participation and happiness by conducting in-depth
analyses of patients’ habits, interests, and needs in order to create segmented marketing campaigns and
individualized services. Proactive healthcare marketing is made possible by the ability to foresee and


46
﻿
The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




respond to emerging health trends, made possible by the predictive analytics made possible by these
technologies (Wang et al., 2018).
    Third, new strategies for advertising healthcare services are needed to accommodate the growth
of value-based care. Advertising should stress the excellent quality of care and the positive outcomes
achieved for patients rather than the sheer number of services offered. Examples of such marketing meth-
ods could include highlighting positive feedback from patients, high success rates, and the knowledge
and experience of doctors.
    Fourthly, the shift toward patient-centered treatment means that consumers must be the focus of
healthcare marketing. Transparency in healthcare delivery and pricing, as well as consideration of patient
preferences and input, are all part of this process.
    Healthcare providers will need to adjust their services to match the growing demand for digital health
options like telemedicine and mobile health apps. For instance, telemedicine not only increases access
to care but also presents new possibilities for healthcare advertising because doctors and other medical
professionals can talk to consumers face-to-face and pitch their services.
    Finally, as digital health expands, concerns about patient privacy and data security emerge as crucial
ethical issues. In order to keep their patients’ confidence, healthcare professionals must ensure that their
advertising strategies are compliant with all applicable laws and codes of ethics.


4. DIGITAL HEALTHCARE LANDSCAPE IN CHINA

4.1. Past Development

Recognizing the significant progress made over the past couple of decades is crucial to comprehending
China’s digital healthcare sector. China’s digital healthcare business has grown tremendously in recent
years, thanks to the country’s booming economy, substantial healthcare investments, and widespread
acceptance of digital technologies.
    Information technology was singled out by the Chinese government as a primary factor in health-
care reform in the early 2000s (Mei, 2020). As a result, they started a program to create a digital health
infrastructure across the country. Electronic health records, better hospital information systems, and the
expansion of telemedicine to remote areas were the initial targets of these initiatives. During this time
period, many Chinese hospitals began using electronic systems for patient intake, appointment schedul-
ing, and billing (Song et al., 2022).
    The late 2000s was a watershed moment for China’s digital healthcare when the country launched
its 3G network. Online health platforms providing health information, online doctor consultations, and
digital health services have proliferated in tandem with the expansion of high-speed, widely available
internet (Keane et al., 2020).
    Mobile health apps and wearable health gadgets have emerged during the past decade, significantly
improving patient participation and individualized treatment. Fitness monitoring, chronic disease man-
agement, mental health, and women’s health are just a few of the many areas that have thousands of
specialized applications in China today.
    Furthermore, AI applications in healthcare, ranging from predictive analytics to intelligent medical
imaging, have proliferated in China as a result of the government’s encouraging policies and substantial
investment in artificial intelligence research (Arenal et al., 2020).


                                                                                                         47
                                                                                                        ﻿
                                        The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




    Disease prediction, data security, patient education, and immersive health experiences are just some
of the areas that have benefited from the combination of big data analytics, blockchain, and augmented
reality in healthcare.

4.2. Current Applications

Telemedicine, AI-driven diagnostics, and individualized health management are only some of the existing
digital healthcare uses in China. Telemedicine is one of the most well-known uses, and it’s been increas-
ingly important since the COVID-19 pandemic (Rush et al., 2021). Healthcare is now more accessible
than ever in China, even for patients in remote areas or who are isolated, thanks to the proliferation of
digital platforms that provide virtual consultations, remote patient monitoring, and digital health records.
    The use of mobile health apps is now commonplace. Apps that keep tabs on a person’s exercise,
food intake, sleep patterns, and mental wellness give people the tools they need to take charge of their
health. Patient convenience is further increased by the fact that certain apps provide services like as
online medical consultations, medication reminders, and appointment booking.
    Disease prediction, individualized treatment, medication development, and medical image analysis
are just some of the applications of AI and big data analytics that are seeing widespread use in China
(Bhattacharya et al., 2021). By analyzing massive amounts of medical data, AI algorithms can, for
instance, foretell the likelihood of disease, provide individualized treatment regimens, and speed up
the discovery of new pharmaceuticals. AI can help doctors better understand medical images like CAT
scans, MRIs, and X-rays (Siddiq, 2020).
    The medical field is increasingly making use of AR and VR systems for teaching, informing, and
treating patients. For instance, VR can be used to treat diseases like phobias and post-traumatic stress
disorder (PTSD), while AR can assist medical students perceive complex anatomical systems (Zreik &
Abunamous, 2020).
    Secure data interchange, patient consent management, and the elimination of fake pharmaceuticals
are all possible thanks to blockchain technology. Blockchain has the potential to boost data security
and patient trust in digital health services by providing a transparent, immutable record of transactions.

4.3. Future Research Directions

China’s digital healthcare landscape is constantly changing, opening up a wealth of new research oppor-
tunities. The expansion of digital healthcare’s accessibility and equity is a priority. Despite great strides,
there is still a huge gap between urban and rural communities in terms of access to digital healthcare.
Research in the future could examine how to develop and evaluate novel approaches to increasing the
availability of digital healthcare for underserved communities.
    Data security and privacy is another important field for study. Concerns regarding data security and
privacy are growing in tandem with the adoption of digital health devices. Research in the future should
look into ways to deal with these issues, such as the creation and evaluation of reliable data encryption
technologies, data anonymization techniques, and user consent management systems. Regulatory and
policy considerations, such as how China’s legal system might be improved to better protect patient
data, should also be investigated.
    The role of the patient is another vital one that needs more research. The need for successful methods
of patient engagement with digital health technologies is growing in tandem with the field itself. Strategies


48
﻿
The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




for improving digital health literacy among patients and healthcare professionals can be developed after
we learn more about the factors that affect patient uptake and utilization of digital health technologies.
    Furthermore, there is still a lot of room for exploration into the possibilities of emerging technology in
healthcare. The medical field requires research into the benefits of emerging technologies including AI,
blockchain, AR, and VR. It’s not just the technical details that matter; the effects on patients, healthcare
expenditures, and the industry as a whole must be considered, too.
    Finally, studies need to investigate how well various digital health solutions can communicate with
one another. With so many digital health platforms and apps out now, it can be difficult to ensure com-
patibility between them. A more unified and effective digital healthcare environment may result from
this line of inquiry.


5. CUSTOMER RELATIONSHIP MANAGEMENT IN HEALTHCARE

5.1. Understanding Patient Loyalty in China

Chinese customer relationship management (CRM) in healthcare focuses on patient loyalty and re-
tention through better communication and relationship management. Loyal patients are an important
part of CRM in healthcare, and in China’s specific setting, doing so requires an in-depth grasp of both
cultural and market-driven elements. Due to cultural beliefs emphasizing respect for authority, Chinese
patients have historically shown their doctors great deference. However, the democratization of medical
information via digital media is changing the nature of this respect. Patients are becoming more active
participants in their healthcare, learning about their options, and advocating for the care they receive
(Bridges et al., 2018).
    The level of satisfaction with the care provided is another factor that can affect patient loyalty in
China. Chinese patients now have access to more healthcare options, such as digital platforms providing
remote consultations and AI-powered health advice, as a result of the rise of digital healthcare (Guo &
Li, 2022). Therefore, in order to keep their patients satisfied, healthcare professionals must guarantee
that both their digital and traditional services adhere to the highest standards of care.
    CRM would not be possible without technology. As digital health apps and platforms gain popular-
ity, they will soon give healthcare providers with a wealth of information about their patients’ habits,
preferences, and health. With the use of AI, CRM systems may evaluate this information to spot trends,
anticipate patient requirements, and tailor care to each individual.
    It’s also important to talk to each other. Keeping patients informed, engaged, and connected through
tailored communication on a regular basis has been shown to boost loyalty. In China, this usually means
using social media like WeChat to disseminate health information, remind people of appointments, and
educate the public (Liu, 2021).

5.2. Tools and Strategies

When considering the digital healthcare environment in China, a wide variety of CRM solutions and
tactics have proven particularly beneficial.
   Data Analytics: Modern customer relationship management relies heavily on gathering and analyzing
patient information. Advanced data analytics techniques are being used by the healthcare industry to


                                                                                                           49
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                       The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




better understand patient habits, requirements, and preferences. This can help with strategic planning,
tailor care to each individual patient. Patients at risk of health issues or non-compliance can be identi-
fied, for example, with the aid of predictive analytics, allowing for timely treatments.
    Personalized Communications: The success of customer relationship management hinges on the ability
to personalize interactions. Using customer relationship management solutions, medical professionals can
routinely send out tailored messages to their patient populations. Personalized health advice, prescription
or appointment reminders, and niche advertising are all examples. In China, this usually means combin-
ing customer relationship management software with social media apps like WeChat (Hong et al., 2021).
    Telemedicine Platforms: Due to the rapid development of digital healthcare in China, telemedicine
systems are now integral components of healthcare customer relationship management. Patients place
a premium on the accessibility and ease of use that can be provided by these platforms. Additionally,
they facilitate continuous monitoring and serve as community hubs for patient mentoring and guidance.
    AI-Driven CRM Systems: Artificial intelligence is used by modern customer relationship management
systems to enhance operational efficiency and deliver individualized patient experiences. To allow for
greater attention to be paid to the more difficult aspects of patient care, AI can be utilized to do things
like predict patient requirements, personalize marketing communications, and automate routine tasks.
    Patient Portals: Healthcare customer relationship management relies heavily on patient portals. Pa-
tients can use these to get in touch with their doctors, view their medical records, request prescription
refills, and schedule appointments. User-friendliness and mobile optimization are particularly important
for patient portals in China’s digital healthcare setting, as the vast majority of Chinese consumers utilize
smartphones to access digital services (Tan et al., 2022).
    Feedback Systems: Including patient feedback in regular system updates is an important CRM tactic.
It aids healthcare providers in learning from patients’ perspectives, figuring out where they can make
changes, and addressing issues as soon as possible. This could be as straightforward as a survey emailed
after a hospital visit or as intricate as a system that allows for constant communication and participation.
    Patient Education and Engagement: Another useful customer relationship management tactic is
encouraging patient participation by giving them access to up-to-date health information. This could
include providing health education programs, holding patient support groups, or writing useful content
for a healthcare provider’s website.
    Loyalty Programs: Patient retention can be boosted by loyalty schemes. Some examples of such perks
are price reductions, referral bonuses, and early access to new offerings (Zineldin, 2006).


6. B2B HEALTH MARKETING IN CHINA

When doing business in the health care industry in China, it’s important to understand the many players
and factors at play, from government agencies to regulators to financiers. These groups have consider-
able sway on healthcare policy and marketing methods.
   Healthcare policies, standards, and regulations are heavily influenced by state institutions like the
National Health Commission. They are in charge of establishing norms, enacting changes, and keeping
an eye on the healthcare system as a whole (Wang et al., 2023). In order to remain compliant and adapt
their marketing tactics accordingly, business-to-business (B2B) health marketers must keep up with the
norms and regulations imposed by these bodies.




50
﻿
The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




    Compliance with healthcare norms and standards relies heavily on the work of regulators. They are in
charge of issuing licenses to medical professionals, keeping tabs on the standard of treatment provided,
and upholding ethical standards. B2B health marketers need the guidance of authorities to ensure their
practices are legal and ethical.
    B2B health marketing in China is significantly impacted by both public and private financiers. They
have an effect on the accessibility and affordability of healthcare services because of the money and
resources they give to healthcare institutions. Insurance firms and other healthcare financiers set the
reimbursement policies and pricing structures for medical supplies and services (Li & Wang, 2022).
In order to create successful pricing and marketing strategies that fit into the financial ecosystem, B2B
health marketers must first understand their impact.
    B2B health marketing involves a wide variety of stakeholders, including governmental agencies,
regulators, and financiers, as well as medical societies and professional organizations. These organiza-
tions speak for the concerns of the medical community. B2B health marketing campaigns can benefit
from the alliances, endorsements, and networking opportunities made possible by collaboration with
medical groups.


7. PRICING OF HEALTH SERVICES IN THE DIGITAL ERA

7.1. Understanding the Chinese Context

Considerations specific to China must be taken into account when setting prices for digital health ser-
vices. The public and private sectors, insurance programs, and government restrictions all play a role in
setting prices in China’s healthcare system.
    The Chinese government has a major impact on the cost of healthcare, especially for basic medical
procedures (Li et al., 2017). To make healthcare more accessible and affordable, governments have in-
stituted price limits and reimbursement systems. To strike a fair balance between the price of healthcare
and the demand for high-quality care, the government routinely revises pricing legislation and publishes
recommendations (Long et al., 2013). To guarantee their pricing plans are in compliance with govern-
ment guidelines and regulatory obligations, healthcare marketers need to manage these regulations.
    The cost of medical care in China is affected by both the government and private sectors. Price set-
ting may be affected by reimbursement rates and policies as a result of the proliferation of insurance
programs including government-sponsored basic medical insurance and commercial insurance options.
When setting prices for their services, healthcare providers must account for the rates at which insurers
compensate them.
    Pricing dynamics in the digital era are further influenced by the Chinese consumer market and patient
expectations. Consumers in China are becoming picky and demanding when it comes to the quality of
their healthcare. They look for affordable treatment, clear pricing, and easy accessibility (Miao, 2016).
Thanks to the clear pricing information and comparison features on digital health platforms, consumers
now have a greater ability to make informed choices (Huang et., 2017). As a result, healthcare marketers
need to think carefully about customer expectations before settling on a pricing strategy.
    Pricing healthcare in the digital age presents both opportunities and challenges. Providers can now
offer services at a wider range of prices and experiment with new pricing structures because to the




                                                                                                      51
                                                                                                         ﻿
                                         The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




proliferation of telemedicine and digital health platforms. Concerns about data privacy, security, and
quality assurance are legitimate in digital healthcare, and marketers in this space must address them.

7.2. Implications for Marketing Management

Marketing managers in China need to give serious thought to how they set prices for digital health ser-
vices. In order to maximize their marketing strategies and successfully compete in today’s healthcare
market, companies in the healthcare industry must take into account a number of critical factors.
    First, it’s important to have a firm grasp of healthcare costs and availability. Pricing strategies in China
need to take into account the purchasing power of the intended consumer base. Healthcare providers can
expand their customer base and increase their market share by providing affordable services. To reach
budget-conscious customers, marketers must stress the benefits and low price of their services.
    Second, dynamic pricing methods can be implemented with the help of digital technologies and
platforms. Healthcare marketers can adapt their pricing strategies to meet the needs of their customers
by utilizing real-time data and market insights. Individual health histories, service consumption patterns,
and bundled service packages can all play a role in this type of tailor-made pricing. In order to adapt to
market shifts and maximize revenue, healthcare businesses should implement digital technologies for
price optimization (Elton & O’Riordan, 2016).
    Third, it is critical that pricing strategies be communicated clearly. Trust can be established and
patient expectations can be managed by open discussion of costs. Marketers in the healthcare industry
need to make sure that all of their digital channels, including their websites, make it simple for customers
to find and understand price details. Justifying prices and assuaging customer worries can be aided by
providing specific examples of the value customers would receive from the services.
    Marketers must also ensure that their pricing strategies are consistent with other marketing initiatives
(Davidson & Simonetto, 2005). The pricing plan should align with the company’s values, its target mar-
ket, and the perceived worth of its offerings. This congruence enables a unified strategy for advertising
the healthcare services’ advantages over the competition.
    Furthermore, pricing methods must be constantly monitored and evaluated. Marketers in the healthcare
industry must regularly evaluate their pricing strategies, determine how they are performing in terms of
market share and profitability, and make data-driven adjustments as necessary. By conducting regular
market research and analyzing the strategies of competitors, marketers can gain insight into price trends,
consumer preferences, and market dynamics and adjust their plans accordingly.


8. ASSESSING THE SUCCESS OF HEALTHCARE MARKETING IN CHINA

8.1. Metrics and Measures

Metrics and criteria that accurately assess the impact and efficacy of marketing tactics are essential
for gauging the success of healthcare marketing operations in China (Marzuki et al., 2023). Healthcare
marketers can evaluate their campaigns’ efficacy using a number of different measures.
    Patient acquisition is a key indicator since it quantifies the number of new patients attained as a
direct result of advertising efforts. Measuring the success of marketing efforts to acquire new patients
is made possible through tracking patient acquisition. Successful acquisition methods can be uncovered


52
﻿
The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




by segmenting this number further by demographics, referral sources, or individual marketing channels
(Berkowitz, 2021).
    Patient loyalty and retention rates are also important indicators of a campaign’s success. Patients’
loyalty and pleasure can be gauged by their frequency of return visits. Effective marketing methods for
establishing and maintaining positive relationships with and inspiring loyalty from patients can be as-
sessed by tracking retention rates and assessing patient satisfaction through surveys or feedback systems.
    The success of a healthcare brand relies heavily on the strength of its name recognition and reputation.
Metrics like brand recognition, brand recall, and brand sentiment tracking can give healthcare providers
valuable insight into how their brands are perceived by their target audiences. The efficacy of campaigns
to cultivate a favorable impression of a brand can be measured, in part, by keeping tabs on brand men-
tions, sentiment, and engagement using online reputation management tools and social media analytics.
    Success of online marketing efforts may be measured with digital marketing indicators including
website traffic, click-through rates, and conversion rates. These measures are useful for gauging the
ROI of digital advertising, SEO, and other forms of online promotion (Bala & Verma, 2018). Marketers
may improve their plans and budget allocation with the help of data collected through digital campaign
analytics.
    Return on investment (ROI) and cost per acquisition (CPA) are two of the most important financial
measures for gauging the success of marketing campaigns (Weideman & Kritzinger, 2017). Healthcare
marketers can evaluate the efficacy and profitability of their marketing efforts by comparing the costs
paid in marketing activities to the income earned.
    Finally, qualitative metrics like patient feedback, online reviews, and testimonials can shed light on
the whole scope of patients’ experiences and satisfaction. These intangibles are useful for gauging how
people feel about healthcare advertising and how much they value it.

8.2. Evaluation of Existing Initiatives

In order to establish the efficacy of current healthcare marketing campaigns in China, to locate areas for
improvement, and to make data-driven decisions for future plans, evaluation is essential. The success of
advertising campaigns in China’s healthcare sector can be measured in a number of ways.
    Key performance indicator (KPI) analysis is a typical way of assessment. These metrics may change
based on the nature of the marketing effort being undertaken. KPIs may include the number of planned
appointments, conversion rates, or shifts in patient volume if the objective is to increase patient appoint-
ments (Woodcock et al., 2017). Healthcare marketers can gauge the efficacy of their efforts by comparing
actual results to arbitrary targets.
    Patient happiness and experience can be better understood with the help of surveys and feedback
systems. It is possible to evaluate the full scope of the effect on patients’ attitudes and actions by col-
lecting feedback from those who have participated in the marketing efforts. Patient feedback surveys can
also be used to examine how well various channels of communication are performing. Online reviews
and patient testimonials are two examples of feedback systems that provide timely qualitative feedback
to supplement quantitative data (Schlesinger et al., 2015).
    The success of a marketing campaign can be gauged in large part by conducting market research and
analyzing the performance of competitors. Healthcare marketers can evaluate their efforts in relation to
industry standards by looking at market developments, consumer preferences, and rival strategies. To




                                                                                                         53
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                       The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




fully grasp the dynamics of a market, market researchers may use qualitative interviews, focus groups,
or quantitative data analysis.
    A financial perspective on the success of marketing campaigns can be gained through calculating
their return on investment (ROI). Healthcare marketers can evaluate the efficacy and profitability of their
efforts by estimating the ROI based on the expenditures paid and the revenue earned from the market-
ing activity. The results of a return on investment analysis can be used to better plan future marketing
campaigns (Farris et al., 2015).
    The success of a digital marketing campaign can be better understood with the use of data analytics
and tracking tools. Healthcare marketers can monitor their websites’ traffic, engagement levels, click-
through rates, and conversion rates with the help of web analytics, social media analytics, and other
digital tracking technologies. This information can be used to fine-tune digital advertising approaches
and boost the effectiveness of campaigns.


9. FUTURE TRENDS IN HEALTHCARE MARKETING

9.1. Emerging Developments

Several new trends are emerging in the Chinese healthcare marketing sector that will significantly alter
the way healthcare businesses interact with their intended audience in the near future. An important new
trend is the widespread adoption of AI and ML tools in the healthcare industry’s advertising efforts.
Chatbots and virtual assistants driven by artificial intelligence are being utilized to improve customer
service and provide more one-on-one encounters between doctors and patients (Ahuja et al., 2023).
Massive volumes of patient data are analyzed using ML algorithms to facilitate predictive analytics
and individualized healthcare suggestions. Healthcare marketers may take advantage of AI and ML as
these technologies evolve to reach specific audiences with relevant messages and better patients’ overall
experiences.
    The widespread implementation of telehealth and remote patient monitoring is yet another developing
pattern. The need for online medical treatment has increased since the COVID-19 pandemic, and this
pattern is likely to continue (Pandey & Pal, 2020). Consultations, tracking of chronic illnesses, and prompt
interventions are all made possible by telehealth technologies. Marketers in the healthcare industry can
reach tech-savvy customers who prefer the privacy and comfort of their own homes by emphasizing the
many advantages of telehealth services.
    Marketing in the healthcare industry will also increasingly emphasize individualization. Healthcare
marketers may learn more about their patients’ individual tastes, habits, and requirements thanks to the
abundance of data and sophisticated analytics at their disposal. Because of this, relevant content, sug-
gestions, and marketing efforts may be sent to specific patients. Improved patient engagement, loyalty,
and good brand perception can result from personalized messaging and experiences.
    Another emerging direction in healthcare advertising is the use of social media and influencer market-
ing. Healthcare conversations, patient support groups, and information sharing have all found a powerful
new home on social media. Marketers in the healthcare industry may use social media to reach out to
patients, disseminate helpful information, foster two-way dialogue, and team up with other experts in
the field to boost their visibility and credibility (Park et al., 2016).




54
﻿
The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




   Concerns about data privacy and ethical implications will remain high priorities for healthcare
marketers. Transparency, data security, and compliance with data protection legislation are all duties
of healthcare businesses. The development and maintenance of patient confidence will rely heavily on
privacy-enhancing technology, consent management systems, and strong data security measures.

9.2. Case Study of China

China’s distinctive healthcare environment and rapid digital development make the country an intriguing
case study in the field of healthcare marketing. Amazing developments in digital health technologies
have taken place in the country, as have novel methods to advertising and patient interaction.
    The widespread use of digital platforms and mobile technology is a standout feature of the Chinese
healthcare market. Despite having the world’s largest population of smartphone users, China has been
an early adopter of telemedicine, health apps, and wearable medical technology (Zreik, 2022). With so
many people using digital media, healthcare marketers may more effectively reach and interact with a
large audience through specific campaigns and tailored communications.
    In addition, the Chinese government has been instrumental in developing the country’s healthcare
system. The government of China has made it a top priority to use data-driven decision making and
provide patient-centered care through policies and programs such as the Healthy China 2030 policy (Tan
et al., 2017). Healthcare marketers now have access to a very dynamic environment as a result of these
programs’ stimulation of innovation and investment in digital health solutions.
    The sheer size and variety of the Chinese people creates both distinct marketing opportunities and
challenges. Marketers in the healthcare industry need to be sensitive to geographical and demographic
variances as well as cultural norms and preferences. It is essential to employ localization and segmentation
tactics when catering to the requirements, interests, and habits of geographically dispersed populations.
    There has also been a shift toward value-based care and patient agency in China’s healthcare market.
More and more people want healthcare that is better, easier, and more tailored to their own needs. To
keep up with the changing needs and preferences of Chinese patients, healthcare marketers must em-
ploy customer-centric tactics including patient education, collaborative decision-making, and enhanced
patient engagement.


10. CONCLUSION

Significant shifts and new openings have resulted from the digital era’s impact on healthcare marketing
in China. We’ve covered a lot of ground in this chapter, from the history of healthcare marketing to its
current state, from the changing nature of the industry’s goals and needs to the concept of a value-driven
healthcare marketing strategy, from the current digital healthcare landscape to projected future develop-
ments. Let us now review what has been covered, consider how different groups may be affected, and
propose some avenues for future study.
   Rapid change can be seen in China’s healthcare marketing as a direct result of the advent of the digital
era. Patients now demand more individualized and convenient healthcare than ever before, signaling a
move toward putting the consumer first. Enhancing patient involvement, bettering care delivery, and
optimizing marketing tactics are all possible because to technological advancements like AI, big data
analytics, AR, blockchain, and mobile technologies. The delivery of high-quality and patient-centered


                                                                                                         55
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                       The Transformation of Healthcare Marketing in the New Digital Era




care has become more dependent on value-driven healthcare marketing strategies that put an emphasis
on patient-centricity, co-creation of healthcare, and partnerships between stakeholders. New avenues for
patient participation and healthcare delivery have opened up thanks to the proliferation of digital health
platforms, telehealth services, and health applications in China. Metrics such as patient acquisition,
retention, brand awareness, digital marketing analytics, financial indicators, and qualitative feedback
are essential for gauging the performance of healthcare marketing in China. Integrating AI and ML
technologies, growing telehealth and remote patient monitoring, emphasizing personalization, leveraging
social media and influencer marketing, and stressing the importance of ethical considerations and data
privacy are all predicted to become more prominent in the near future in China’s healthcare marketing.
    The digital era’s impact on healthcare marketing has far-reaching consequences for many parties
involved. To keep up with patients’ ever-shifting wants and demands, healthcare providers must put
patients first, adopt digital technology, and personalize their marketing efforts. Healthcare policymak-
ers and regulators have a significant impact on the industry and should promote the use of creative
marketing approaches while protecting patients’ personal information and adhering to ethical standards.
Reimbursement policies from insurers and investors should reflect the shifting landscape of healthcare
marketing and encourage the adoption of digital tools proven to enhance both patient care and satisfac-
tion. Patient care that is more tailored to each individual, easily accessible, and focused on their needs
is what healthcare marketers strive for. They should have access to up-to-date information, chances for
meaningful participation, and the autonomy to make their own healthcare choices.
    Several crucial aspects need to be explored further in future studies on healthcare marketing in China.
First, healthcare practitioners and policymakers would benefit greatly from research into the efficacy and
influence of digital health technologies like AI, big data analytics, and telemedicine on patient involve-
ment, care results, and marketing tactics. Optimizing marketing tactics and enhancing patient-centered
care requires an in-depth understanding of patients’ experiences and happiness in the digital era, as well
as the elements that influence patients’ loyalty and engagement. It would also be helpful to investigate the
effects of social media and influencer marketing on patient attitudes, knowledge, and choices regarding
medical treatment. Effective pricing models that strike a balance between cost, accessibility, and quality
of care can be developed with the help of research on pricing strategies in the digital era, including the
effects of government laws, insurance coverage, and patient expectations. More research is needed into
the ethical considerations of healthcare marketing, such as patient privacy, data security, and informed
consent. Patient co-creation and public-private partnerships are two examples of value-driven healthcare
marketing techniques that may be analyzed for their effectiveness in improving healthcare delivery and
patient satisfaction.

                                                        Chapter 4
       Marketing of the Healthcare
         System in the Light of
        Sources of Its Financing
        and Tools for Building a
        Competitive Advantage
                                          Agnieszka Jadwiga Wójcik-Czerniawska

                                          SGH-Warsaw School of Economics, Poland

                                                  Zbigniew Grzymała
                                          SGH-Warsaw School of Economics, Poland


ABSTRACT
The main purpose of the chapter is to review medical systems and their financing, and to popularize
these systems through various marketing tools. Within the different approaches to healthcare financ-
ing, particular attention has been given to the method of individual medical accounts. According to the
authors, this method is conducive to the development of various other methods of treatment due to the
availability of funds from one’s own therapeutic account and the patient’s personal choice of the center
and treatment method. In this way, it allows the strengthening of a specific supply of medical services,
including alternative treatment methods from the point of view of classical medicine.



These physicists dealt not only with healing but also with education and broadly understood prophylaxis
for the subjects, the feudal family and the army. Next, the institution of physicists developed in royal
and private towns, which marked the beginning of public health care. (Public health). The financing of
such health care was either the responsibility of the rulers or the private payment of medical services.
Healthcare funding has always been balanced between a private and a public fee. For example, in the
eighteenth-century Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (now Poland, Lithuania, Belarus and Ukraine),

DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch004



       Copyright © 2024, IGI Global. Copying or distributing in print or electronic forms without written permission of IGI Global is prohibited.
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                              Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




the Hospital Constitution was adopted in 1775 by the Warsaw Extraordinary Sejm, in which provincial
physicists were appointed whose task was to supervise all medics in the state, and then in The Polish-
Lithuanian Police Commission assumed the creation of the institution of district and district doctors
.(Public health) These doctors were to provide free medical services to the poor and paid to the wealthy.
This meant partially cross-financing the treatment of the poor by the wealthy and by public institutions.
Balancing between the commercial and public model of providing medical services continues to this
day. Between these two separate approaches, the possibilities of financing health care through voluntary
or compulsory health insurance entered.
    The basic research method is a literature review on the financing of health care systems in the world,
especially in those countries where individual medical account solutions are used. The Polish proposal
of individual medical accounts proposed by the former Minister of Health, Dr. Zygmunt Hortmanowicz,
with whom one of the authors interviewed during his lifetime, is also described.


LITERATURE REVIEW

Health Care Financing Models

In practice, financing for healthcare around the world relies on several sources. A particular source may
dominate in the treatment financing structure, for example, public health care facilities financed from
the state budget or from health insurance, etc. Nevertheless, several basic models of financing medical
services are distinguished in the literature:

1)   Bismarck Model (Germany) – health insurance,
2)   Semashko Model (Soviet Model) - centrally planned system,
3)   Beveridge (British) - the system of the national health service,
4)   free market (American) - treating health as a free market commodity.

   The literature also distinguishes four other normative models of health care corresponding to the
above division:

1)   a moral model based on the principle of the sanctity of life and health, absolutizing the rights of
     the patient and disregarding economic conditions, in which medical professions are missionary
     professions,
2)   bureaucratic, paternalistic model, centrally managed and supervised according to established
     procedures,
3)   a free-market model in which the patient is responsible for his own health, and the scope of medical
     services is governed solely by the principle of their supply and demand,
4)   an insurance model in which the scope of health services is the result of agreements between three
     parties: the patient, the doctor and the insurer.

   The Bismarck model developed at the turn of the 19th and 20th centuries. Its creation was largely a
political movement aimed at improving the living conditions of workers and nipping in the bud the grow-
ing socialist movements. The system later became a role model for other countries, including France,


62
﻿
Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




Belgium, Austria, the Netherlands, Japan and Poland. Most Central and Eastern European countries
introduced systems based on the German model before World War I. The Bismarck Model was charac-
terized by elements such as:

•    establishment of insurance institutions - sickness funds;
•    creating funds from insurance premiums to finance health services;
•    co-payment of contributions partly by the employee and partly by the employer;
•    management of funds independent of the state administration;
•    financing consisting in concluding contracts with service providers;
•    development of subjective entitlements (ie covering also the family or dependents of the insured
     person with the insurance) and objective entitlements (ie gradual increase in the scope of guaran-
     teed health services) (Karłowska, 2017).

    Funds financing this model were also supplemented from the state budget. In essence, the Bismarck
Model referred to solutions also inscribed in the bureaucratic model and, to some extent, in the moral
model.
    In the Soviet Semashko Model which in its assumptions also corresponds to the moral model, health
care is financed through the state budget, which thus assumes full control of a given state over health
care, including the policy of employing employees. This, therefore, determines the rather centralized
management and financing of this model. In practice, this means that marketing tools promoting given
solutions are basically not needed. A patient who has full access to health care, of course, uses what is
offered to him. This also applies to the adopted methods of treatment, the waiting period for an appoint-
ment, etc. In general, the patient’s influence on the choice of treatment method is limited.
    In the Beveridge system, it was assumed that health care would be financed also through the state
budget from general taxes, similarly to the Soviet Model. In this way, the government or field agencies
would exercise control over the system. However, this system allowed the participation of patients in
financing the costs and the participation of private services. In the part financed by the state budget and
patients, there was full access to medical services. Health care workers received a salary depending,
among other things, on the number of patients. In this system, you can see elements of the bureaucratic,
insurance and moral model. Some solutions of Beveridge’s system are similar to those of Semashko or
Bismarck’s models.
    In the free market model, which is most effective in the United States, it is assumed that a health
service is a kind of commercial transaction. Attention is also paid to the financial efficiency of this sys-
tem. Healthcare financing in this model is mainly based on insurance packages or individual payment
for the service if someone is not insured. In the United States alone, the health care financing system is
a complex institutional system dominated by the private sector - one of its elements are medical savings
accounts (MKO), which are a supplementary mechanism protecting against excessive financial burden
during illness and old age.
    In the USA in 2020, over 66 percent. of the insured had private insurance plans, while 35 percent.
was covered by public plans (Medicare, Medicaid and VHA). In addition, about 9 percent. (28 million
people) had no health insurance, despite the efforts of recent years to popularize this type of protection.
Most working Americans and their families are insured by their employers, some pay contributions
themselves (Kozierkiewicz, Gilewski, 2023).




                                                                                                         63
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                              Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




Individual Medical Accounts

Individual medical accounts function, among others, in the USA, as already mentioned, Singapore and
China. In the USA, there is also a solution consisting in setting up Health Savings Accounts - HSA.
These accounts were created under the Medicare Prescription Drug, Improvement and Modernization
Act (Public Law No: 108-173 (12/08/2003)) signed by President George W. Bush on December 8, 2003,
replacing existing medical accounts savings account (Medical Savings Account (MSA). Currently, no
financial institution in the US is opening new MSA accounts, although there are still accounts launched
in the past (Archer MSA. investopedia.com. 2008-08-25. Retrieved 2016-09-03). Of course, the discus-
sion about individual medical accounts in the US has been going on for a long time. This concept was,
for example, combined with catastrophic health insurance established in conjunction with individual
health accounts (IHAs). This was to ensure, on the one hand, the reduction of health care costs, and, on
the other hand, to encourage saving. Under this plan, the employer establishes both a high-deductible
health insurance plan and an IHA. Annual contributions to the IHA are equal to a substantial fraction
of the deductible. Employee health care costs below the deductible are then paid out of the IHA; Costs
above the deductible are paid by the insurance plan. Assets remaining in the account when the employee
retires, or becomes Medicare eligible, are then available for other purposes. The motivation for the par-
allel saving and insurance plans, of course, is that each employee is spending his or her “own” money
for medical care, except in the event of serious illness. The plan thus combines the desirable features of
catastrophic coverage for reducing medical expenditures, with a mechanism that creates a reserve from
which individual expenses can be paid (Eichner, McClellan, Wise 1998; Feldstein 1971, Feldstein and
Gruber 1994).
    This discussion was intertwined with the concept of an acceptable national health plan. According
to (Stano, 1981) the plan should meet the following objectives:

•    Provide all with access to care;
•    Eliminate financial hardships;
•    Control or limit increases in costs;
•    Be administratively simple; and
•    Be widely accepted by the public and providers of care.

   At first glance, it appears impossible to achieve the first two goals without extensive insurance cov-
erage for all regardless of ability to pay. This in turn would aggravate the cost and efficiency problems
unless substantially more cost sharing is included. Such measures, though, would be politically unpopular
and meet strong public opposition.
   However, the apparent need for insurance, and for first-dollar coverage if out-of-pocket costs are to
be avoided, rests on the false premise that insurance (defined narrowly as third-party coverage) is the
only device which can reduce the kinds of financial risk which most individuals clearly wish to avoid.
Under current methods of insurance, no relationship exists between an individual’s consumption of health
services and premiums received by the insurer from that individual. Only for the group of insureds will
premiums be sufficient to cover expected claims and other costs. This is an inevitable outcome of un-
certainty and the pooling of risks in the conventional way. Consequently, there are large redistributions
from healthy to sick persons, with no special financial rewards for avoiding the use of services, as well




64
﻿
Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




as the major faults of overconsumption of health services and inefficiencies arising from a minimum of
cost participation by patients.
    It is possible, though, to provide the security associated with insurance, but with substantial reductions
in insurance as measured by third-party payments while, at the same time, creating significant rewards
for efficient and cost saving behavior by both patients and providers. To achieve these conditions, our
proposal would abandon current methods of financing health care in favor of a system with the following
three basic elements (specific details will be discussed later in this paper) (Stano, 1981).
    This is where the concept of individual health accounts came in. Rather than contributing premiums
into an insurance pool, employers and individuals would be required to contribute, subject to both mini-
mum and maximum amounts, into individual (interpreted as an appropriately defined family unit) health
accounts handled by a qualified administrative organization. The functions of the administrator are: to
credit the individual’s account for contributions received; to invest the balance in suitable assets; and to
debit the account for claims for services received by the individual at terms agreed to by the individual
and the provider (Stano, 1981).
    Currently, HSA funds are basically a savings account where funds are pooled for medical purposes. In
this way, they can be used to pay for medical services and products specified in the regulations without
being burdened with federal tax liabilities. Withdrawals other than for medical services and products are
treated similarly to Individual Retirement Account (IRA) withdrawals, which may be tax-exempt if used
after retirement age . The accounts listed above are part of Consumer-Driven Healthcare. HSA funds can
be used to pay for regulated medical services and products without being subject to federal tax liability.
Withdrawals other than for medical services and products are treated similarly to Individual Retirement
Account (IRA) withdrawals, which may be tax-exempt if used after retirement age. The accounts listed
above are part of Consumer-Driven Healthcare. (Kozierkiewicz, Gilewski, 2023).
    Insured persons do not need the consent of their account manager or their insurer for the withdrawal
of accumulated funds, which are not subject to income tax if they are intended for qualified medical
expenses. These expenses include the costs of services and products covered by health insurance, which
are co-financed by the insured person (co-pay), as well as many other expenses not covered by the health
plan, such as dental, eye and chiropractic care ; medical equipment such as glasses and hearing aids; in
addition, transport costs related to healthcare (IRS outlines changes to health care spending available
under CARES Act. www.irs.gov).
    The country whose healthcare system is generally viewed positively and which has implemented the
concept of medical savings accounts is Singapore. This system has been specifically described. Singa-
pore’s medical system is part of the social security system . It was built on three principles: the principle
of supporting health protection and promoting a healthy lifestyle, the principle of private responsibility
(implemented by the 3M system - Medisave, Medishield and Medifund), and the principle of controlling
the supply of medical services in order to ensure an appropriate level of prices. The system is co-created
by three institutions:

-Ministry of Health, which is responsible for the regulation of the medical services sector, the distribu-
     tion of budget funds and all health programs conducted at the central level, etc.;

     ◦◦    the Central Supply Fund (CPF), which is the central financing institution;
     ◦◦    Central Bank responsible for regulating the entire insurance sector (Karłowska, 2017 p.
           37-38).


                                                                                                           65
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                              Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




    MediSave is a savings account that’s part of a set of CPF accounts. It is compulsory for you and your
employer to contribute to your MediSave account. For employees, this money is set aside automatically
from your monthly salary. All Singaporeans and PRs residing and working in Singapore are required
to make MediSave contributions. Singaporeans residing and working overseas do not have to make
compulsory MediSave contributions, but they are free to make voluntary contributions. Money in your
MediSave account can be used for your healthcare needs such as medical treatments, dental care, Me-
diShield Life, Integrated Shield Plans and ElderShield or CareShield Life with certain limits. MediSave
can be used to pay for your personal or approved dependents’ healthcare needs. It is meant to help you
save for future medical expenses in your later years. It can only be used for certain healthcare and medi-
cal expenses like: (1) Hospitalization and surgery (including wisdom tooth surgery), (2) Palliative care,
(3) Outpatient treatments, (4) Chronic disease management, (5) Health screenings, (6) Vaccinations, (7)
Having a baby, (8) Health insurance (MediShield + Integrated Shield) premiums, (9) Disability insur-
ance (CareShield + ElderShield) premiums. However, there are limits to how much you can withdraw.
Here is a quick snapshot of what you can withdraw for different purposes:

•    inpatient care:
     ◦◦   Minimum 8-hour admission - up to $550 for first 2 days, $400 per day from third day onwards
     ◦◦   Psychiatric episodes - up to $150 per day, maximum of $5,000 per year
     ◦◦   Day surgeries - up to $300 per day including surgical limit ($250-$7,550)
     ◦◦   Approved community hospital – up to $250 per day, maximum of $5,000 per year
     ◦◦   MediSave Maternity Package – up to $550 for first 2 days and $400 per day from third day
          onwards + $900 for pre-delivery medical expenses
•    outpatient care:
     ◦◦   MediSave500/MediSave700 Scheme – simple chronic conditions: up to $500; complex
          chronic conditions: up to $700 per patient
     ◦◦   Selected childhood and adult vaccinations
     ◦◦   Health screenings – mammograms for women over 50 years old and newborn screening tests
          in outpatient setting
     ◦◦   Patients above 60 years old + spouse - $300 per patient per year
     ◦◦   Renal dialysis treatment - $450 per month
     ◦◦   Radiotherapy – $80-$2,800 per treatment
     ◦◦   Radiosurgery treatment – Up to $7,500 per course
     ◦◦   Chemotherapy – Up to $1,200 per month with MediShield Life claim limit above $5,400. Up
          to $600 per month for other treatments
     ◦◦   Anti-retroviral treatment for HIV – up to $550 per month per patient
     ◦◦   Immuno-suppressants after organ transplants - $300 per month per patient
     ◦◦   Autologous bone marrow transplant – up to $2,800 per year per patient
•    long-term care:
     ◦◦   Inpatient hospice palliative care services – up to $250 per day (general palliative care) and
          up to $350 per day (specialized palliative care)
     ◦◦   Home palliative and day hospice care – combined lifetime withdrawal limit of $2,500 per
          patient
     ◦◦   MediSave Care – withdraw up to $200 per month for long-term care needs (Poh).




66
﻿
Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




    MediSave is a savings account funded by CPF contributions. It helps all Singaporeans and Permanent
Residents (PR) save for their and also their dependents’ future healthcare needs. MediShield Life is a
personal health insurance plan that covers all Singaporeans and PRs from birth. It boosts your MediSave
account’s coverage and helps you pay less for expensive hospital bills in B2/C class wards (6-8 beds in
a room). MediShield Life can also be used to help pay for expensive outpatient treatments like chemo-
therapy or dialysis.The savings in your MediSave account can eventually be depleted while MediShield
Life covers you for life. There’s also no limit on how much you can claim in your lifetime (with annual
limits). MediShield Life provides coverage for pre-existing medical conditions such as cancer, blood
disorders, heart diseases and more. There’s also the potential for coverage to include treatment for at-
tempted suicide, substance abuse and alcoholism in the future - traditional health insurance plans usually
exclude these conditions.You can pay for your MediShield Life premiums with MediSave. MediSave
can also be used to pay for a portion of your Integrated Shield Plans, a type of private hospitalization
insurance that helps to reduce your out-of-pocket healthcare expenses. The MediShield Life portion of
your Integrated Shield Plan is fully payable by MediSave. The additional private insurance portion is
also payable by MediSave and any excess is paid in cash: $300 per year - 40 years and below aged on
their next birthday; $600 per year – aged 41-70 years on their next birthday; $900 per year – aged 71
years and above on their next birthday (Poh).
    MediSave account contributions vary depending on your monthly salary, business or work income,
and whether you’re an employee or self-employed. A percentage of your salary is set aside and deposited
into your CPF accounts – employee CPF contribution (table 1).
    The MediSave account had an interest rate of 4 percent in 2022. Depending on the accumulated amount
and age, the interest rate is increased (Poh). The experiences of the last period in the world concerning
inflation put a question mark over the protection against inflation of these accounts. Inflation in health
care significantly exceeds general inflation. From 2002 to 2022, the CPI All-Items increased 44.7%,
while the CPI-Healthcare rose by 53.6%. During that same period, the average headline inflation rate
was 1.86%, while the average healthcare inflation rate was 2.17% (Lim).
    Moreover, according to the data provided by the Ministry of Health, in the period 2007-2017, the
cost of private hospital care in Singapore increased at an annual rate of 9%, while in the case of public
hospitals it was about 4.5%. This led to attempts to limit the increase in costs, eg by introducing co-
payments to private IP health plans (Jasiński, 2022) described earlier. Another reason for the increase in
government spending on health care is, among others, the aging of the population. The older generation
