                                                                                                       ﻿
                                              Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




forces more spending on medical care. Figure 1 shows the share of government spending on healthcare
in selected countries.
    Chart 1. Domestic general government health expenditure (% of current health expenditure) – Sin-
gapore, Poland, United States, China, Hungary, South Africa
    Among the countries presented, the share of government spending in the period 2000-2022 was
increasing, except for China. In other countries, this increase took place regardless of whether they had
medical savings accounts or not.
    In Singapore, in the first place, the main emphasis was placed on accumulating own savings (Me-
diSave) and insurance, eg from serious illnesses requiring expensive hospitalization (MediShield Plus).
Only in the next stages, when the amount of collected funds becomes insufficient, support by MediFund
is launched. In contrast, other countries are trying to give their citizens access to a range of benefits
“right away”. The only condition is that you pay for government medical “insurance”. This often results
in shortages, queues to doctors and low quality of services provided. In Singapore, citizens’ own sav-
ings are the backbone of the system, not an addition. Secondly, in addition to own savings, an important
element of the Singaporean system are additional direct payments to be made by insured persons under
MediShield Plus: deductible and co-payment, which partially reduces moral hazard. Thanks to this, the
basis is direct payments, and not universal health insurance “for everything”. What’s more, MediShield
Plus finances the most expensive services, and the less expensive or routine ones are financed from
MediSave, which well illustrates the purpose and meaning of health insurance. What’s more, the funds
collected on MediSave bear interest, and overpayments entitle to tax reliefs. Thirdly, the above solutions
make the structure of financing access to medical services more rational, and the insured/account hold-
ers have a lot of time to adequately protect themselves against future medical expenses. An additional
advantage is the general prosperity of Singaporeans, which is possible thanks to the liberal nature of
their economy (Jasiński, 2022).
    Another country that introduced medical savings accounts was China, where the MKO project was
launched in two cities, Jiujiang and Zhenjiang. In 1994, the existing universal insurance was replaced
by individual medical accounts supplemented by an insurance policy with a franchise. Since 1998, the
changes have affected all Chinese cities. During this period, there was a change in the use of inpatient
care in favor of outpatient care, which also contributed to a decrease in total expenditure by 8%. There
was also a reduction in the number of hospitalizations and their length. Between 1993 and 1998, expen-
diture growth slowed down. This is attributed to a reduction in the use of expensive diagnostic services
and medications. On the other hand, research indicated a deterioration in access to care (this concerned
the poor, unemployed - mainly for financial reasons) and an increase in health expenditure among people
without MKO (Jurkiewicz-Świętek, 2014).
    Based on almost twenty years of Chinese experience, the most important problems related to their
functioning have been identified in MKO:

•    small savings accumulated on MKO (mainly among patients with chronic diseases), which results
     in poor implementation of the accumulation function;
•    the complicated method of capitalization of the funds accumulated on MKO does not sufficiently
     protect them against inflation and the increase in the cost of medical services over time. Therefore,
     MKO holders see no point in keeping funds in the account (and spend them on an ongoing basis).
     In addition, the sooner the funds for MKO run out, the sooner they will be able to benefit from
     financing by the insurance policy;


68
﻿
Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




•    IOCs do not sufficiently limit moral hazard. A huge asymmetry of information between the patient
     and the doctor has been noticed, where the other party is not sufficiently controlled, which results
     in abuse/inappropriate use of services;
•    setting the same amount of the franchise (after which the insurance can be used) for all MKO
     holders seems to be unfair. People with high incomes can reach this limit very quickly, while
     those with less income will have difficulties (resulting from the simple fact of limited financial
     resources). This means remaining in the phase of direct financing of medical services and a neg-
     ligible chance to take advantage of the insurance;
•    incorrect/misuse of funds collected for the MKO (eg medical supplies, foodstuffs) resulting from
     the lack of faith that the MKO will actually cover the costs of benefits in old age. In addition, there
     was a lack of understanding, especially among young people, of the purpose of accumulating
     funds in an individual account (Kong, Yang, Gong, Zhao).

    Medical savings accounts have also been introduced in South Africa. This took place in 1994. These
accounts became part of private health insurance. As a result, there has been a reduction in health spend-
ing in this country. Of the European countries, the only country that decided to apply the idea of MKO
was Hungary. There, together with the private health insurance policy, the patient accumulates funds in
a separate account in order to cover the costs of co-payment or purchase medical services on the private
market. Unfortunately (Jurkiewicz-Świętek, 2014). Unfortunately, the authors failed to collect more
information about the MKO in Hungary.
    In Poland, the concept of introducing MKO was promoted by Zygmunt Hortmanowicz 1. This doc-
tor, together with the team (Jarosław Góra, Bogusław Dembiński, Waldemar Flisiński, Anna Bielawka-
Hortmanowicz, Sebastian Kamiński, Sławomir Krystosiak, Włodzimierz Kucharski, Krzysztof Słoński,
Krzysztof Teodorowicz and consultant Janusz Kołodziejczyk) promoted the idea of such accounts . This
resulted in the creation of assumptions for the draft act on financing medical care in Poland by the sena-
tor of the 2nd term of office of the Republic of Poland and the main author, Zygmunt Hortmanowicz,
together with the aforementioned team, dated December 17, 1992.
    The preamble to this bill mentions that healthcare financing reform should be based on four pillars:

− liberal profession of a doctor,
− free choice of doctor,
− free market of medical services,
− free use of means of payment by every citizen who has an account in the so-called Health Protection
     Bank (BOZ).

    This model was moving towards a market approach, even more so than the Singapore model. In any
case, it was an innovative approach to financing healthcare in Poland. The aforementioned Bank Ochrony
Zdrowia would replace the National Health Protection Fund and take over its assets and organizational
structure. In this bank, funds would be accumulated on family (individual) bank accounts of working
people, pensioners, and would only be used for health purposes, as in Singapore or even the United
States. From such an account, transfers for medical services would be made. It would also be possible
to provide subsidies and loans to people in the event of severe and chronic illnesses and to save lives.
These loans would be redeemable in 50-80%. This bank would also co-finance the activities of scientific
and research institutes and medical education from its own developed funds.


                                                                                                         69
                                                                                                         ﻿
                                                Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




     Such family accounts in BOZ would consist of amounts from various sources, including:

− 7% of the health insurance premium,
− 5% from Treasury bonds, stock exchange operations, investment income, etc.,
− 5-10% from insurance companies (reimbursement of road accident costs), from KRUS (10% on ag-
     ricultural contributions),
− 10% on funds accumulated in the National and Provincial Funds for Environmental Protection,
− 10% on funds obtained from the privatization of State Medical Care Institutions,
− 10% on the income of plants with harmful workplaces,
− 20% of the funds awarded for medical malpractice,
− 3% on the sale of drugs to finance independent research on their effectiveness,
− 1% on the sale of alcoholic products, high-proof alcohol products (except for spirit for medical purposes),
     tobacco products, non-organic fuels, chemical plant protection products and chemical detergents
     and cosmetics, food produced using non-organic methods (containing synthetic ingredients that
     have a harmful effect on health),
− from the part of the state budget allocated to health care (1% of GDP),
− from possible subsidies from the EU,
− from funds allocated for revaluation due to inflation,
− from voluntary contributions from citizens (Assumptions).

   The funds would be administered by the account owner or a family member authorized by him. Funds
accumulated in these family individual accounts after the death of the owner would be transferred to
the account of the heirs or the person indicated in the last will of the deceased. The funds accumulated
on the family (individual) account in BOZ would give individuals and their families the opportunity to,
among others:

•      making any choice of the place of medical service - in doctor’s, dental, nursing, midwifery, phys-
       iotherapy and therapeutic massage, outpatient clinics, specialist clinics, laboratories and diagnos-
       tic laboratories, sanatoriums and preventoria (this type of possibility would be conducive to the
       promotion of various treatment methods and the use of marketing tools by companies medical,
•      purchase of medicine, dressings, ophthalmological, orthopedic and dental equipment and hearing
       aids,
•      coverage of account management costs by BOZ, up to 2% per annum of the value of accumulated
       funds,
•      serving up to 15-29% as help to people who have exhausted funds from their account and have
       health needs (so-called mutual assistance),
•      free choice of the place of using the medical service (in doctor’s, dental, nursing, midwifery, phys-
       iotherapy, therapeutic massage, clinics, including specialist clinics, laboratories and diagnostic
       laboratories, sanatoriums and preventoria,
•      purchase of medicines, dressings, ophthalmological, orthopedic and dental equipment and hear-
       ing aids,
•      covering the costs of servicing the BOZ account up to 0.03%,
•      mutual aid to 15-20% for people who have exhausted these funds from their account, and would
       have health needs (Assumptions).


70
﻿
Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




    For people under supervision, for example, the unemployed, homeless and others who are dependent
on social welfare, the costs of medical services provided would be covered by the Local Social Welfare
Facility. Funds accumulated on the family (individual) account (account) in the bank, after the death of
the account owner, would be transferred to the account of the closest family (spouse, children) or to the
account of the person indicated in the last will of the deceased (will).
    The essence of the idea of Z. Hortmanowicz and his team also referred to the promotion of a healthy
lifestyle, which would promote not only physical activity, but also nutrition, animal husbandry and plant
cultivation. There is a view propagated by an increasing number of doctors that many diseases of mod-
ern societies are caused by the conditions of the natural environment in which we live and the quality
of the products we eat. Poisoned air and food chemically stimulated to grow and some chemical plant
protection products cause an increase in the amount of toxins in our bodies. These, in turn, contribute
to many diseases, especially chronic diseases. Improving the condition of the environment in which we
live and the quality of the products we consume should, on the one hand, reduce the number of cases
and, on the other hand, facilitate the recovery of people who are already ill. In this way, Poland as a
country would stand out against the background of Europe and the world and would gain a new valuable
specialization, also of an economic nature. As in Singapore, financial support from the state budget was
envisaged, which would cover in full:

•    costs of treatment and rehabilitation in provincial, clinical (university, academic) and infectious
     diseases hospitals,
•    medical rescue services,
•    activities of Sanitary Inspectors, - activities of blood donation stations,
•    operation of dialysis laboratories, banks of organs for transplantation.

    Payment to individual medical accounts would be made by bank transfer to the accounts of facilities
providing services or a health credit card or in other selected form. The Ministry of Health would be
responsible for the transfer. Substantive supervision over medical care facilities would also be exercised
by the Ministry of Health through the voivodeship and poviat health departments. According to the
originators, this reform does not require additional funds from the state budget and additional taxes.
Pursuant to the constitutional provision, the Polish state does not renounce financing the health care of
its citizens. The Minister of Health will be allowed to deal with the issues of health security of citizens,
and not with the financing of medical facilities, as has been the case so far. It does not divide citizens
into rich and poor. There is no need to maintain special medical facilities for the poor - all subjects are
treated equally. The introduction of this solution should cause a beneficial phenomenon of universal
self-discipline in taking care of one’s own health. In addition, compared to the currently applicable
solutions in Poland in the field of health care, the citizen will stop paying 3 times for the same services.
Implementation of this system as soon as possible will prevent further pathology in medical care. It
will improve working conditions and prevent unnecessary protests of medical staff on a national scale.
Free use by citizens of means of payment for medical services will make citizens independent of the
whims of the rulers. Everyone will benefit from medical services under the 4 x BEST formula (most
effective, best, cheapest, closest). The market mechanism will contribute to improving the motivation
of both the healers and the treated. It will also contribute to the self-education of medical staff. BOZ is
not threatened with bankruptcy due to the large number of sources of financing the accounts. Most of
the medical staff is insured against civil liability in insurance companies. The Insurance Company pays


                                                                                                         71
                                                                                                        ﻿
                                               Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




for errors made by the medical staff, proven in court, which will facilitate the collection of receipts. The
system provides opportunities for mutual assistance in the event of exhaustion of the health account in
BOZ. Funds accumulated on the BOZ account (they are not lost) after the death of its owner. They are
transferred to the account of the person indicated in the last will of the deceased or to the account of the
closest family (spouse, children) (Assumptions).
    This proposal is clearly conducive to the use of various marketing mechanisms and tools of medical
facilities that will seek patients. With this proposal, the originator, Dr. Zygmunt Hortmanowicz, wanted
to support and promote medical centers whose treatment methods go beyond classical medicine. He
himself admitted patients with chronic diseases, including cancer, and treated them, among others, with
the use of an ecological diet, dietary supplements or climate. For many years he ran an outpost in the
mountains. At the same time, he did not give up diagnostic tests provided in the public health service. He
was one of the originators of the so-called integrated medicine. In an interview with one of the authors,
he claimed to have cured the so-called solid tumors in more than 80% of patients with such a disease.
Using these methods, he cured himself of cancer. He applied the principle of experimenting with healing
methods first on himself. However, the costs of staying in the mountains and purchasing supplements
were relatively high for Polish patients. Unfortunately, many of them could not afford the full choice
of the method promoted by Dr. Z. Hortmanowicz. Individual medical accounts (MKO) would facilitate
the financing of treatment stays in the mountains or other places with a favorable climate for treatment.
    Besides, in a system based on the Semashko Model, moral or even Bismarck Model, where the state
predominantly finances health care, the patient is dependent on the solutions adopted in this country.
For example, in Poland, a very effective and minimally invasive method of treating leukemia, the so-
called Car-T is not financed by the state budget for adults. This condemns such patients to accept the
cheaper methods offered by the state. Sometimes the sick are influenced by people without medical
education who offer unconventional methods of treatment. The effects of treatment vary. Individual
medical accounts could help in the selection and promotion of various treatment methods, even the so-
called unconventional. The condition would be that the specialist had a medical education and was able
to equip his center with the necessary tools to provide diagnostic and preventive care, including modern
methods, such as the aforementioned Car-T.
    The authors have also encountered practices of lobbying by pharmaceutical corporations for a specific
drug or treatment method. One representative of the Polish parliament responsible for shaping health
policy in Poland blocked the development of drugs obtained, for example, from medicinal marijuana.
This was probably the result of lobbying activities of certain pharmaceutical corporations. If treatment
is financed from the state budget, patients must accept the treatment methods proposed by the state
health policy. Not every patient can afford to use private medical centers that may offer other treatment
methods. It seems that MKO would make it easier for patients to choose a given treatment method from
the pool proposed by representatives of integrated medicine. The choices made by patients would also
transform public healthcare.


TOOLS FOR BUILDING A COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE IN MEDICINE

The basic tools for building a competitive advantage include, among others, increasing the quality of the
service or medical product offered, reducing the costs of production or service provision, or appropriate
promotion being part of broadly understood marketing. Medical marketing is a set of activities aimed


72
﻿
Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




at the emergence of, for example, a medical facility in the minds of the consumer, ie a potential patient.
Polish law prohibits advertising of medical services, which is also part of marketing. According to Ar-
ticle 14 pt. 1 of the Act of 15 April 2011 on medical activity, an entity performing medical activity may
disclose to the public information about the scope and types of health services provided, provided that
the content and form of this information do not bear the characteristics of advertising. In other words,
doctor’s offices cannot directly persuade patients to use their services or purchase treatments. However,
they can inform potential customers, eg about the advantages of disease prevention, or even educate them
in the field of pro-health behaviors. It is also allowed to provide information about the types, methods
and scope of services provided in the facility. Medical marketing focuses on such activities, and their
goal is to build a positive image of the office, doctor or clinic and increase their visibility on the web
(Rzetecki, 2020).
    Comparing the creativity of the public and private medical market, it should be stated that the market
of private services is the most dynamic. In Poland, the dental services market itself was privatized the
fastest and almost in 100%. In practice, public institutions are marginal, and if they exist, access to them
is very difficult, and the quality of services offered is far from the level of services provided by private
institutions. As a result of this situation, patients almost always choose a private clinic for highly special-
ized services (Olewniczak, 2022). Dental services are not cheap, hence MKO accounts would probably
be conducive to financing the dental service.
    Due to the growing social demand and the inefficiency of the public health sector, the share of
private service providers in the provision of rehabilitation services is also increasing. The aging of the
society is the main reason that generates the demand for, among others, therapy services after spine or
joint surgery: knee, hip, ankle or shoulder. Treatment of postural defects (especially in adolescents) and
broadly understood physiotherapy services are also of interest to private entities. These entities, provid-
ing the above-mentioned services, have modern medical and diagnostic equipment and the current best
medical knowledge (Olewiczak, 2022). The demand for such services will increase. You can even find
information about it on social networks. Demand is limited by the costs of providing this type of service.
If individual medical accounts were introduced in Poland, this demand would transform from potential
to actual and would probably allow for increasing the digitization of medical services.
    Digitization of medical services, such as teleconsultations, consultations via chat, video advice, e-
prescriptions, etc., has gained a permanent place on the medical market, especially during and after the
pandemic. Also, the results of pharmaceutical research conducted by transnational corporations confirm
the introduction of permanent digitization of medical services, although it was not easy.
    Managers of pharmaceutical companies also pointed out to various effects of changing the ways of
providing services. For example, the aforementioned directors and managers of these corporations in
free interviews pointed to the negative effects of remote work, in the form of isolation from teams or
clients, which is particularly difficult for people working in teams that have direct contact with the client.
This aspect was also noticed by the respondents, who indicated that the form of hybrid work would be
the most optimal for them in the future, as it would provide them with the possibility of both conceptual
work at home and direct contact in the office with the team and supervisor (Bartosiak). Attention was
also drawn to the advantages of remote work. The need to further develop methods related to hybrid
work was noticed - based on the research results, it should be concluded that in the post-pandemic period
some tasks will be carried out remotely, taking into account the hitherto achievements related to this
form of work . Due to the advantages associated with the provision of remote work, it is recommended
to further improve the solutions developed by enterprises in the field of tools and methods used to en-


                                                                                                            73
                                                                                                        ﻿
                                               Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




able the indicated form of remote work. Implementation of the above solutions, even to a limited extent,
will allow enterprises to prepare for possible turbulences in the immediate environment and, as a result,
to make them more resistant to the effects of potential crises and turbulences in the future (Bartosiak).
    The competitive advantage of private medical entities is currently also built by the fact that they also
have a developed diagnostic base, ranging from ordinary laboratory analyzes to highly specialized di-
agnostics using the latest medical equipment and devices. In addition, in some areas, aesthetic medicine
services are provided exclusively by private entities. The purpose of these services is to remove health
problems (e.g., excessive adipose tissue) or to make corrections, such as: reduction of cellulite and
stretch marks, removal of wrinkles, etc. A separate phenomenon - going beyond health aspects - is, for
example, lip modeling and enlargement or nose correction. However, here too the private sector meets
social expectations (Olewniczak, 2022).
    Patients look for doctors on the Internet, so information about medical services must also be visible
there. According to Gemius/PBI data from September 2019, as many as 15 million Poles searched the
Internet for information on treating diseases, proper nutrition and a healthy lifestyle. In 2019, an average
of 57% of Polish Internet users searched for health information online (Rzetecki, 2020) . Social media,
in which medical facilities are promoted, have also turned out to be very popular. The profile of a given
medical facility on Facebook or Instagram has now become a necessity. Professional companies have
specialized in building such an image. Social media is a great way to communicate with patients. Today,
marketing and communication in the medical industry is carried out in the omnichannel formula. The
patient asks for advice on his problem via Facebook, asks additional questions via Whatsapp to finally
arrange an appointment by phone. The solutions proposed by such companies are:

•    running a Facebook profile for doctors,
•    paid social media campaign on social networks for the medical industry (eg Facebook Ads,
     Instagram Ads),
•    preparation and maintenance of a Google business card visible in Google Maps,
•    lead generation: acquiring new clients for a medical office in social media,
•    social media posts : static (eg photos, graphics) & video (eg Instagram Stories, animations),
•    social media moderation : building engagement and establishing good relationships with recipi-
     ents for efficient brand activity in social media comments,
•    doctors - copywriters : experts who will take care of the quality and content of publications on
     your profiles instead of interns who will be provided by the competition (Social Media for doctors
     and veterinarians (doctortarget.pl)

   It is likely that the digitization of medical services will develop even more in the future. Perhaps it
will be possible to install an application on a mobile phone that will allow, among other things, to mea-
sure blood pressure, perform a simple blood analysis, etc. Such results can then be sent electronically to
a doctor’s consultation. The medical consultant will then be able to make a preliminary diagnosis and
recommend certain further actions, including, for example, taking a drug.




74
﻿
Marketing of the Healthcare System in the Light of Sources




SUMMARY

Healthcare is one of the most important aspects of our lives. In a changing and turbulent environment,
such as the increase in the incidence of cancer, pandemics, etc., it must keep up with these changes.
An important issue is its financing. Observing the experience of different countries, the most effective
solution is the choice of an individual treatment method by the patient. In the palette of various models
of medical care, ranging from more centralized – Semashko, Beveridge, moral or bureaucratic to free-
market, the most creative solutions include private medical services open to many different treatment
methods supported by the private choice of the patient of a given medical facility. Centralized mod-
els, which in their assumptions allow all citizens on equal terms to public health care, also have their
drawbacks. One of these disadvantages is the constant lack of funds to finance all medical procedures
in a relatively reasonable time, as well as various other treatments, including the use of modern more
expensive drugs. This results in long waiting periods for a doctor’s appointment or hospital treatment
and premature deaths. It also fosters corruption and a certain social passivity in terms of responsibility
for one’s health. The inclusion of private medical services, accompanied by the promotion of certain
medical methods, meets the demand for medical services. In order for patients to be able to use such
solutions more easily, they should be able to decide on the direction of their treatment and financing.
Having individual medical accounts with the possibility of inheritance, etc., they choose the path of
medical care development. In this way, they stimulate service providers to create new ways of treatment
and use tools such as digitization of medical services.
    Accounts also have their drawbacks related to the relatively higher inflation of medical services com-
pared to other goods or services. One of the methods of limiting inflation is the introduction of greater
competition in the broadly understood healthcare market. Centralist models of health care do not favor
the consolidation of a competitive market for medical services. Such a market will be strengthened by
individual medical accounts and the possibility for patients to choose the use and financing of various
treatment methods from these accounts, which in the long run may reduce the inflationary pressure on
medical services.
    The proposed model for Poland was a draft. In a conversation with the authors, Z. Hortmanowicz
maintained that Polish individual medical accounts should not be perceived as instruments diversifying
society. Through the mutual possibility of sharing their accounts in the event of exhaustion of funds
accumulated on them, they were to support the financing of treatment. Hence, they were not connected
with the function of the pension fund. Polish health accounts were also intended to allow patients to
choose which method they wanted to be treated, whether a more traditional one or an alternative one
under the supervision of an educated doctor. Discussions over these accounts have been suspended in
Poland. This article is an attempt to resume this interrupted discussion.

            Big Pharma and Impact of

ABSTRACT
There has been a significant rise in the prices of medicines in the recent past. Major pharmaceutical
companies, ‘Big Pharma’, argue in favor of this development as it takes a lot of research and resources to
bring a drug to market. Once in the market, the primary source of revenue generation for the companies
is through sale of prescription drugs. Companies also have to spend a lot of their income on various
marketing communication programs to convince their prescribers, even if it comes at costs that are dif-
ficult to ignore. This chapter studies the array of marketing strategies that are adopted by Big Pharma
in developing and expanding their businesses. It also places these strategies within the context of the
fast-growing generics market with its consequent changes to the marketing mix of the industry. While
doing so, the chapter also explores and appreciates the various aspects of the pharmaceutical industry,
as it presents features distinct from any other.



1. INTRODUCTION

While India is very distant in terms of having a robust health infrastructure, it certainly stands tall in sup-
plying pharmaceutical products to the entire world. The Indian pharmaceutical market ranks among the
top three markets globally, in terms of volume, and 14th globally in terms of value (Invest India, 2022).
It also accounts for 8% of the global API market, with more than 500 different APIs are manufactured in
the country, while contributing more than half of APIs to prequalified list of the WHO (Department of

DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch005



       Copyright © 2024, IGI Global. Copying or distributing in print or electronic forms without written permission of IGI Global is prohibited.
﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 1.­




Pharmaceuticals, Annual Report 2022-23). Lately, Indian pharmaceutical companies have become the
global leaders in the production of generic medicines and vaccines. Indian pharmaceutical companies
meet more than half of the global vaccine demand, more than 1/3rd of the US’s generic medicine demand
and one-fourth of all of the UK’s medicine demand (Invest India, 2022). As some of the indicators are
changing, these producers need to play more pivotal role on the global stage. Further, with India’s epi-
demiological profile rapidly changing, healthcare is becoming a priority for the government as outlay
for the sector is being progressively increased in every budget. With the change in the lifestyle and rapid
urbanization, a host of diseases are on the rise - cardio-vascular problems, neurological disorders, and
other form of chronic diseases like diabetes, hypertension etc. All these factors together combine to make
India a very promising market for the global pharmaceutical manufacturers.
    The pharmaceutical marketplace is shifting quite dramatically, with enormous implications for the
entire industry. Like every industry in the recent past, the Pharma industry too is experiencing labor
shortages, supply chain snags and higher input costs due to inflationary pressure. Lot of factors are
responsible for the launch of new efficacious drugs in the market, even if companies are burdened with
very high costs of R&D, enhanced competition and growing pressure from various stakeholders to re-
duce their healthcare costs without compromising profitability. The industry has made some structural
changes and found more novel ways to augment their drug discovery potential, reducing time from the
ideation to commercialization, and controlling their cost along the whole value chain. The goal is to
stay profitable and lead the market, all while keeping the social and economic value of treatments for
chronic diseases low and accessible.
    While keeping with this overall goal, deeper penetration of branded generics and growing govern-
ment initiatives for encouraging branded generics have emerged as a definite trend globally, due to
the immense potential that generics hold to bring about substantial financial savings for governmental
healthcare systems and out of pocket saving (OOPS) for individuals; thus, making healthcare more cost
effective and accessible to its peoples.
    Food and Drug Administration (FDA) defines a generic drug to be,

Identical or bioequivalent to a brand-name drug in dosage form, safety, and strength, route of admin-
istration, quality, performance characteristics and intended use.


                                                                                                        79
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 2.­




    Thus, the main point of contention remains to make these generic drugs as effective and safer as
its counterparts, but pricing them much lower competitively. The low costs of generics are majorly on
account of negligible R&D to be undertaken and minimal expenditure on marketing, advertising and
sales promotion. According to Association for Accessible Medicines (AAM), Generics constituted 91%
of drug prescriptions dispensed in the US in 2021, while these prescriptions accounted for only 18.2%
of the total drug spend of the country. In the previous decade, US government healthcare system saved
around $1.67 trillion largely due to better availability of generic medicine (Association of Accessible
Medicines, 2017).
    It has also been found that, generics have played significant role in lowering the prices of off-patent
original drugs and other equivalents through introduction of price competition in the market (Yina et al.,
2023). In the European Union, the entrance of generic drugs has brought down the price of off-patent
medicines by 61% (Cameron et al., 2012). Taking into account the above-mentioned reasons, the generics
market has given them a new lease of life, with industry experts expecting the global generics market
size to expand at a CAGR (Compound annual growth rate) of 8.3% this decade until 2030 (Grand View
Research, 2023).


2. PHARMA PRODUCT DIFFERENT FROM CONSUMER PRODUCTS

Pharmaceutical companies supply drugs or medicinal products that are used for diagnosis, prevention
and treatment when specific medical conditions occur. Consumer products like tooth paste, snacks, sta-
tionery items, etc., are used more routinely. The pharmaceutical products differ from consumer products
of other industries in that it has longer stages in its product life cycle. A new pharmaceutical product’s
development has a high price tag and a number of hazards. On an average only .0002% of the drugs get
into the stage of commercialization among the 5000 molecules in the pipeline and were eventually ap-
proved for the patient use. Studies reported that only 30% of the approved drugs in the US were able to
meet or exceed their average R&D cost, which are being projected at anything in the range of US $500
million to US $2 billion per molecule (DiMasi et al., 2003; Admas et al., 2006; Kraljevic et al., 2013).




80
﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 3.­




    Pharmaceutical industry bank on new patented drugs to exploit its income from a novel pharmaceu-
tical product. Since recent years, the average lead time between receiving marketing authorization and
patenting a new chemical entity has decreased to 6.4 years (Keyhani et al., 2006).
    Pharmaceutical industry is quite distinct from other industries in number of ways. Firstly, the company
uses various communication strategies to make their prescriber aware and these prescribers prescribe
their patients medicines, who are the ultimate customer or consumer in the entire chain. These health care
professionals also take the choice about whether the patient requires a drug to be prescribed or not, and,
if yes, what type of drug and in what dosage and its affordability; the druggist/pharmacist, who might
be actively encouraged to sell the branded drugs by their generic counterparts of what was prescribed
by the health care provider (Generic Substitution).
    Companies adopt rapid penetration strategies in the introduction stage with the launch of a new
pharmaceutical drug and segment the market to target early adopters who will be among the majority
of the prescribers. Pharmaceutical companies use very intense marketing communication strategies like
continuing medical education (CME), drug samples, sales promotional tools etc. to leave mark in the
chamber of the health care prescriber.
    The next phase in the life cycle of drug being the growth phase which is aimed at augmenting the
perceived value and loyalty among its users and targeting new set of prescribers to expand their prescriber
base. In the maturity phase, company might bring some line extension of the drug to target another set




                                                                                                        81
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 4.­




of customers as the competition gets intensified seeing the progress in the size of the market. But the
company still keeps its drug on the active promotion and the new prescribers are called laggards.
    Finally, in all the above-mentioned strategies involving product penetration & market development
supported by intense marketing communication campaigns and personnel selling skills of their sales-
people, the stage comes when the company loses the exclusive marketing rights of the drug as product
patent is no more valid, which results in a significant drop in sales and generic drugs enter the market
and exploit the pre-established market but marketing of drug remains a grave area of concern. In addition
to the product life cycle, some of the major differences between pharmaceutical products and consumer
products have been summarized in Table 1.
    All the pharmaceutical companies’ intent to produce effective and efficient drugs, but the same
needs to be done proficiently by balancing the profitability and the market share. This can also lead
to an anxious situation with the respective governments of countries as they may be otherwise trying
to cover budgets of healthcare, specifically of prescription drugs. This conflict was recently apparent,
during the COVID-19 pandemic, when many life-saving drugs, like albuterol inhalers, azithromycin,
hydroxychloroquine and sedation drugs, were in huge demand and pharma companies made millions
by charging exorbitant prices (Christina, 2021).
    The first reason for high pricing is the unique way the consumer surplus functions in the asymmetric
health care market; it is so odd that we can call it patient surplus. The number of patients tends to infin-
ity as a disease’s severity rises. When one’s health or, worse yet, life is in danger, how much will they
be willing to spend for it or how much pressure can they put on the payer?
    A monopoly is the second. The drug patent is defended in this context as the sole way to generate
revenues to fund the future R&D in the entire pharmaceutical industry. Pharmaceutical drugs are primarily
used to treat acute and chronic conditions. Acute illnesses generally develop suddenly and last for less
than a month, like common cold, influenza, and gastroenteritis. Chronic illnesses are more persistent
health conditions that develop gradually over time and lasts for longer periods of time, sometimes entire
lifetime. Chronic conditions are often hereditary, but can also be linked to age, lifestyle choices and
environmental factors. Examples include, Type 2 diabetes, asthma, epilepsy, hypertension (increased
blood pressure), hyperlipidemia (high level of LDL-cholesterol) and thyroid disease.

3. BIG PHARMA

As per the Merriam-Webster dictionary, the term “Big Pharma” is used quite often to describe large and
successful pharmaceutical businesses that generate literally billions of dollars and exert considerable
economic, political and social influence. These leading pharmaceutical firms, also referred to as “big
pharma,” are mostly of American and European origin. Half of the top 10 pharmaceutical companies
in the world belong to United States, while the other half are headquartered in Europe. Even these busi-
nesses and the entire pharmaceutical sector rely heavily on international supply chains, with China and
India playing crucial roles in the supply of both active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and finished
pharmaceuticals. Figure.2 gives the top 10 pharma companies with sales revenue and % increase in
sales in 2022.
    After the Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS) agreement of the World
Trade Organization (WTO) entered into force in 2005, Big Pharma effectively received exclusive patents
in all markets. In third world countries, the patent regime amounted to merely the granting of ‘private

﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 8. Top 10 global pharma companies (2022)




rights of exclusion’ to foreign patent-holders. India, a nation better renowned for its services sector in
the global economy, developed a distinctive and prosperous manufacturing industry as a result of the
lack of product patents in the pharmaceutical business from 1972 to 2005 and limits on foreign invest-
ment in the 1970s and 1980s.
    India being one of the fastest growing pharmaceutical markets among the “Emerging Markets -7”,
also called ‘EM7’ - (Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa + Mexico, Turkey and Indonesia),
and growing at 13%, is also the third major producer of pharmaceuticals in the world by volume. The
country’s Department of Pharmaceuticals reported that India accounted for one-fifth of the total global
exports of “generic” drugs. India, also has the 2nd highest number of FDA approved facilities outside
the US and it is further estimated that in future this will rise to 40% of its generic formulations supply
to the US markets (Department of Pharmaceuticals, Annual Report 2022-23). Indian companies, led by
the likes of Sun Pharma, Aurobindo, Cipla, Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories Ltd, Emcure, Hetero, Macleods,
Matrix and Strides have played a massive part in supplying numerous life-saving medicines globally.
    From the above chart (Figure 3), it is quite evident that patented / over-the-counter drugs / generic drugs
constitute the combined revenue of all the companies. Lately, off-patented drugs like Lipitor (Pfizer),
Metformin (Eli-lily), etc., have given lot of opportunities for big Indian pharma companies to establish
their footprint of being the most efficient and leading players in entire global pharmaceutical industry.

4. PHARMACEUTICAL MARKETING

The structure of pharmaceutical market is quite complex and is a highly controlled industry. In addi-
tion to the statutory regulations, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) officials consistently assess
medicines (Dyer, 2004 and Grey, 2015) on the aspects of their

(a) efficacy, safety, and conditions of their manufacturing quality as to whether they comply with the
    good manufacturing practices as a condition of market access;
(b) promotion; and
(c) prices of drugs

    The definition of pharmaceutical marketing, according to WHO and Health Action International
(HAI), is the “activities focused on making physicians as well as the general public aware of new and
existing pharmaceutical brands; it can include giveaway samples, detailed product literature, disease
management programs, and support material for patients, and events/meetings for physicians”. There
are several factors that influence the success in marketing efforts, such as – drug affordability, illness
prevalence and severity, new breakthroughs and medical technologies, public attitude, and supply chain
related factors. Marketing strategies have traditionally been built around the four ‘Ps’ i.e., Product, Price,
Place, Promotion, popularly called the ‘Pharmaceutical marketing mix’, first introduced by Borden in
1964 (Hailu, 2021). Figure 4 shows the main elements constituting the 4 Ps of marketing.
    Moreover, James (2004) identified that two new ‘Ps’ are also relevant to pharmaceutical marketing,
thus making it ‘the 6 Ps of Marketing’. The two new Ps are,



86
﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 10. Marketing’s 4Ps




a)   Political relationships with those organizations responsible for payment and
b)   Patients, who have access to information and an increasing economic oversight into their care.

    Patients are the final consumers of the pharmaceutical drugs being prescribed by the doctors and
retailers / chemist / druggist honor these prescriptions which are supported by pharma company ap-
pointed Wholesalers / distributors supplied by carrying and forwarding agents (CFA). Figure 5 shows
the entire pharmaceutical supply chain.
    The pharmaceutical industry has gone under paradigm shift in honoring the prescriptions of the
doctors, with the use of various marketing strategies. Marketing strategies help the companies to build
competitive advantage over its peers, which can be take the form of the below mentioned.

I.   Innovate Strategies
II. Differentiate Strategies
III. Focus strategies


IV. Defense strategies
V. Strategic alliances


4.1. Innovate Strategies

Majority of the drugs being launched in the market by the multi-national corporations belong to the
category of innovative drugs also called as patented drugs. Innovation in the industry involves new drug

drugs with less side effects. But the time taken from the ideation to formally launching the drug take

   Companies bring new drugs or focus on improving the existing product line as patients start develop-

The growth in the business revenue of pharmaceutical companies has also been helped by support from

for the next 10/ 20 years depending on the country. Essentially, the grant of exclusive market rights has
further helped the companies to get a state of temporary monopoly situation so that they can recover the
hefty costs incurred in discovery of drug and its development. The reports indicate that grant of patents
to the companies and their charging of hefty pricing does not guarantee that their entire input cost of
developing new drugs can be recovered in a decade; with about 90% of patented drugs are having direct
competitors in the same therapeutic category (Australian Academy of Science, 1995).
    According to Bain and Company, the competition in the pharmaceutical industry is becoming more
intense:
