4.2. Differentiate Strategies

The consistent ever-growing competition has forced the companies to adopt novel strategies to stay bet-
ter off than their competitors. Pharmaceutical companies themselves or when the drug regular brings
the drugs in the pricing control ensure that the prices of the drugs fall. This also helps the companies to
target the larger market who otherwise might not able to consumer due to its cost. This strategy of reduc-
ing their profit margins by lowering their prices and maximize the reach is called as market penetration.
    But sometimes the companies adopt a strategy of reformulating the innovative patented drug to target
it for different therapeutic indication or for promote the same drug with different dosage regimen, or it
being an improved version that can demonstrate better therapeutic superiority (e.g., tolerability, greater
safety, or improved convenience of administration), an additional 3 years of exclusivity rights may be
granted. But the grant of this type of extension is largely dependent on sanction of a new application by
the competent authority like FDA in U.S, which requires data from new clinical trials aimed to establish
their superiority in new indications, formulation, or dosage.

4.2.1. Evergreening

A primary patent, which completely covers a novel molecular or chemical entity, grants the inventor a
free right to all sales of that entity in the market for 20 years, which being the full life of the patent. But
this looks to a pretty short period in context of their investment and sales.
    The best strategic alternative for these companies would be to get their pre-existing exclusivity extended
in the market or enter in those global markets where they can still reap the fruits of patenting. This will
help the corporations to keep the competition from the generics at a distance. Since, it is practically not
desirable to get the term of their preliminary filed patent extended, but a few of the creative alternatives
can help these corporations. Some innovators instead used other strategic alternatives which included
subsequent filing of patents that were only the improved variations of their parent compound called as


90
﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 14.­




secondary patents. This practice, is defined as evergreening, which allowed an extended monopoly which
denies the public at large an accessibility of drugs at fair and equitable prices unfairly.
    The statute as per Sec 3(d) of the Patents Act, 1970 clearly defined the standard for follow-on patents
on drugs, “as one of therapeutic efficacy, where applicants may need to supply some clinical evidence”.
But this standard was tested and upheld by the Supreme Court in one of its landmark decision in 2013
while hearing for the patent application of Novartis’ anti-cancer drug Gleevec (imatinib mesylate), or
Glivec. Novartis had claimed that the drug gets absorbed more easily into the bloodstream and will help
to fight leukemia, that was enough evidence of an upgrading to warrant patent protection. Enormous
examples are there in the history of India whereby the timely Indian government intervention has curbed
the evergreening, ensuring that the interests of its citizens are safeguarded and the drugs are accessible to
them at economical prices. But India’s trade and industry ministry held it as “absolutely justified under
the law” and reiterated that India’s patent law “does not accept evergreening.”
    According to a 2018 study published in the Journal of Law and the Biosciences, more than two thirds
of new drug patents granted in the previous 10 years were for products that were previously available on




Figure 15.­




                                                                                                          91
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 16.­




the market. The next list of bestselling 100 drugs included with 70% of them got their exclusivity protec-
tions extended at least once, and 50% of them got their patents extended on more than once occasions.
    The second strategic move was “thicketing,” which involves filing numerous patent applications with
the U.S. Patent and Trademark authority which makes it harder for other companies to get patents. These
tactics further aided pharmaceutical companies in maintaining their monopolies.

4.2.2. Line Extensions

Line extensions, which are often based on the original substance, offer patients an improved medication.
Through their communication strategies, brand teams try to convince prescribers to convert their patients
to the newly patented drug before the original drug’s patent expires under the guise of convenience or
lower cost. This strategy helps the companies to minimizes the loss in the market share to its competitors
and makes it less striking for the generic players to enter the market thereby raising the barriers of entry
because now they will be targeting a very a depleted segment of patient population.
    Examples include Alkem Labs putting out the extension for its cefpodoxime-proxetil brand, Swich,
or Intas Pharma launching brand extensions for its anti-infective drug-based drug, Ceftas. Even a well-
known medication like Voveran has seen brand extension. Similar instances have been witnessed by
both Indian and MNC’s. across the therapeutic categories.
    Brand extension strategy helps the pharmaceutical companies to get better mileage in combination
drugs, since they are meticulously in line to the therapeutic areas of the original drug. This methodol-
ogy is being widely adopted in India and changed the entire landscape of global pharmaceutical market.
    Additionally, brand extensions might generate more revenue than the primary brand. As an illustra-
tion, sales of Abbott Lab’s vitamin B complex medicine Betonin have increased following the debut of
its brand extensions.

4.3. Focus Strategies

Companies need to look into those aspects of markets which can be segmented on the basis of product,
therapy, or patient wise, and identified those key elements which influenced treatment options for a


92
﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 17.­




pertinent indication, such as tolerability, safety, efficacy and side effects (Paich et al., 2011). Lately,
pharmaceutical companies have started to practice either a highly focused clinical strategy by showing the
exclusive features of their drugs that provided value to its prescribers, or build a novel set of therapeutic
indications hovering around their brand well supported by promotional tools like continuing medical
education (CME), grant for research aimed at creating brand loyalty (James, 2004). Many pharmaceuti-
cal businesses practice a “weak” marketing strategy, whereby the weak sales pitch is presented to larger
customers, despite the fact that the toughest marketing strategies were intensive on certain client seg-
ments that are most likely provide them better profits. The examples of these kind of strategies include
launching of sustained/extended formulations of their existing drugs.
    When Lilly company confronted the challenge of expiry of product patent of its bestseller antide-
pressant drug Prozac(brand), the company thereby developed and attained patent protection approval
for its once-a-weekly, sustained-release Fluoxetine (generic name). Additionally, Bristol-Myers Squibb
received FDA approval and patent protection for their extended-release version of the diabetes medication
Glucophage (Metformin hydrochloride). This new formulation, sold under the trade name Glucophage
XR, enables type II diabetics to take a dose once daily.

4.4. Defensive Strategies

R&D expenditures in the pharmaceutical business account about 20% of annual revenue. Every time a
new drug is introduced with a cutting-edge chemical or a brand-new method of delivery, an established
player usually has to protect their market share.

4.4.1. Price cut-off

The Ranbaxy corporation sold medications at “pocket-friendly costs” in an effort to appeal to the rural
and small-town populations of the nation. It built up its brands in less expensive, less competitive small
towns before catching rivals off guard in the metropolitan market with pharmaceuticals that were just
slightly more expensive than the market leaders’ offerings. Additionally, it saved money on building
infrastructure and had their pharmaceuticals manufactured through contracts, allowing the business to



                                                                                                          93
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare




lower the price of their goods and surprise rivals. For instance, compared to GSK and Ranbaxy’s brands,
which used to cost Rs 40 each, Mankind Pharma launched its flagship antibiotic brand Moxikind CV
costing for Rs 11 (a six-tablet box of 500 gm). It goes without saying that both business and consumers
have praised this tactic.

4.4.2. Polymorphic Patents

The idea of polymorphism states that a molecule can adopt many crystal forms. The shelf life, solubil-
ity, formulation qualities, and processing features of a medicine can all be significantly impacted by
polymorphism. One drug polymorph may be more potent than another, easier to make or harder to
make, or even harmful. Because a business may decide to patent the molecule’s structure, a particular
crystalline state, or both, polymorphs create patenting issues. Some businesses have benefited from
polymorphism. A business can prolong its window of market exclusivity by patenting a polymorph after
the primary medication has been protected by a patent. Ritonavir, an antiretroviral medication sold by
Abbott Laboratories under the trade name Norvir® and used to treat HIV infections and AIDS, was the
first medicine in which many polymorphic-forms were discovered. The first generic patent for the HMG-
CoA inhibitor class was filed in 1986 for atorvastatin, a medication sold by Pfizer Inc. under the trade
name Lipitor®. This patent expires in 2010. The business also submitted a particular patent in 1991 for
calcium atorvastatin, which gave it an extra year of exclusivity until it expired in 2011. Another patent
was filed to further prolong market rights, exposing the medications’ unique polymorphs (form-I, -II,
and -IV, of which form-I was the most prevalent).

4.4.3. Flanking

Some companies lately have used a strategy of launching a generic version of their patented drug which
are on the verge of losing patent either themselves or in exchange of royalty, the “flanking” or “approved”
generics strategy enables branded drug companies to manufacture at capacity. Due to the industry’s
generally low profit margins, it is far less enticing for other generics producers to try to compete when
one generic form of the drug gets established in the market before the patent expiration. This method
ensured that there existed only one generic form of the drug and preventing loss in market share which
could have been there by the introduction of multiple generic drug introductions. An approved generic
contains the exact same active and inactive components as the brand-name medication. It is frequently
produced in the same location and using the same machinery as the brand-name drug, but is usually
offered at a lower cost. Branded generics and authorized generics are frequently confused, but they are
not the same. After the patents on branded drugs expire, branded generics go through the same FDA
approval procedure as other generics.
    However, authorized generics are produced by manufacturers of branded pharmaceuticals and are
allowed to be sold prior to the expiration of drug patents under the same NDA (New Drug Approval)
permission as the original branded drug. Authorized generics and branded generics typically cost more
than regular generics, but less than branded. Only innovator businesses are authorized to sell gener-
ics, but both innovator and generic companies may sell branded generics. They both differ from one
another and have varied effects on customers, generic medication manufacturers, and brand-name drug
manufacturers. However, a generic drug may differ slightly from the name-brand product in some small
ways, such as using alternative inactive components.


94
﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare




4.4.4. Pay for Delay Deals

A “reverse payment” or “pay-to-delay” arrangement - Manufacturers of branded medications use a variety
of life cycle management techniques to prolong their market exclusivity and postpone the introduction
of generic medications. The sale of branded medicine makers declines as generic medications enter the
market. Therefore, many innovator businesses occasionally use “pay for delay deals,” in which they agree
with generic corporations to postpone the release of their generic version on the market, to address this
issue and lessen the impact of generic debuts on market share. Consumers pay a price for these pay for
delay agreements because they are only given the choice of branded or legal generic medications to
purchase. According to Section 3 of the Competition Act of 2002 in India, “pay for delay” behavior can
be investigated as an anti-competitive agreement or as a monopoly abuse under Section 4.

4.4.5. Divesting

Also known as ‘divestiture strategy’, Divesting involves organizations cutting down on the range of their
business activities in an effort to obtain higher efficiency, improve financial health and build brand value.
Many Indian and foreign businesses sell off their non-core brands, assets, divisions or subsidiaries to
concentrate and strengthen their primary portfolios. The divestment strategy reduces expenses associated
with inventories, field force, sales, and marketing by liberating resources and freeing up time that can
be reassigned to the important brands. It also helps to maintain a low debt-to equity ratio. This strategy
also helps in enticing investors, as it brings into sharp focus the company’s profitable growth. When
adopted during a lean period, the divesting strategy also results in greater returns for its shareholders.
All these benefits make it an attractive strategy to build sustainable core competencies, thus eventually
leading to gain competitive advantage in the industry. While Pfizer sold its non-core brands to Abbott
and Sun Pharma, Sanofi sold its nutraceutical business to Universal Nutriscience. In addition to selling
its orthopedic portfolio to Integrace earlier, Glenmark Pharmaceuticals also sold its gynecology division
to Integrace Pvt. Ltd.

4.5. Strategic Alliances

Rusu et al. (2011) told that mergers and acquisitions could be used to build the pipeline management well
supported by prioritization of R&D projects. Some of the forms of strategic alliances a pharmaceutical
company can adopt are given below:

4.5.1. Licensing Arrangements

Aside from expanding the portfolio of new products, in-licensing agreements are a less expensive and
hazardous option to secure items than buying businesses or investing in R&D, which is far more expen-
sive. The in-licensing model aids Indian businesses in introducing innovative drugs to the nation at fair
costs. Additionally, because these drugs have already received commercial approval in other nations, the
regulatory processes are also simpler and quicker because they move straight through a bio-equivalence
study and phase-III trials.
   Eli Lilly and Zydus Cadila have signed a new medication discovery and development agreement to
work on potential new treatments for cardiovascular disease. India is likewise becoming as a centre for


                                                                                                          95
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare




advanced research. In a recent announcement, Johnson & Johnson (J&J) stated that it would use India as
a global hub for the development of its newest medications. As a result of this strategy, J&J will conduct
final pre-production testing in India for all upcoming novel medications and chemicals. Such cooperative
agreements are becoming more prevalent among domestic enterprises.
    Deals involving the licensing of patented goods fall into three categories.

•    Acquiring exclusive marketing rights (EMR): Territorial rights to the licensee for a predeter-
     mined time. For instance, Boehringer Ingelheim and Lupin announced a contract to co-market
     Boehringer’s diabetes medications Ajaduo and Gibtulio Met in India. Remogliflozin Etabonate,
     an anti-diabetes medication, to be co-marketed in India by Glenmark Pharmaceuticals and Torrent
     Pharmaceuticals.
•    Non-exclusive agreements: In order to address the increased demand for some pharmaceuticals
     as a result of the pandemic, businesses have also made non-exclusive agreements. Examples of
     non-exclusive deals between pharmaceutical corporations include the licensing of Remdesivir by
     Gilead, Molnupiravir by Merck, and Baricitinib by Eli Lilly.
•    Off-patent licensing contracts: Businesses are willing to licence generic, non-patented portfolios
     as well. A contract was negotiated by Abbott to take over the marketing and distribution of MSD’s
     generic product line in India.

4.5.2. Mergers and Acquisitions

Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) are very common in the pharmaceutical industry, as well as the tech
industry. Due to its immediate strategic implications, M&A has been a ‘go to’ strategy for the pharma
companies to capture market share and augment growth. So much so, it has been quite a regular occur-
rence. Healthcare companies in India spent $4.32 billion on mergers and acquisitions from January to
June 2022 compared to $2.02 billion during the same time last year.
   The latest to join the group of Chinese medicine manufacturer Shanghai Fosun Pharmaceutical’s ac-
quisitions in India was Hyderabad-based injectables manufacturer Gland Pharma. Company in its effort
to grow its contract manufacturing business in Europe, it purchased French Contract Development &
Manufacturing Organisation (CDMO), Cenexi Group for a value of about 1,000 crores. The Goa-based
bulk pharmaceutical formulations division of Teva Pharma India had been purchased by local company
Marksans Pharma.


5. GENERICS

The World Health organization (WHO), defined generic as a drug, “that can be interchangeable with the
original one”. So, instead of writing branded drug names like ‘Allegra’ ‘Agimfast’ or ‘Alafree’, health
care prescribers had to prescribe their patients the same medicine to treat allergy indications with its
molecule name, fexofenadine. During the term of the patent, pharmaceutical innovators are allowed to
forbid rival firms from releasing generic copies of their products before their patents expire (s).
    For the medicine Flurazepam Hydrochloride (Brand: Dalmane; Company: Roche), a generic business
called Bolar Pharmaceuticals was barred from developing generic versions by the innovator corporation
Roche years ago. This incident sparked the landmark legal case of Roche Products v. Bolar Pharmaceutical.


96
﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare



Figure 18. Product market matrix




Afterward, the US Congress passed a law allowing the manufacture, use, offering to commercialize, or
sale of patented inventions during the patent’s term if such actions were solely for uses that were reason-
ably associated to the development and submission of evidence under laws that govern the production,
use, or sale of drugs. The “Bolar” principle came to be known as a result of this reform in patent law.
This also goes by the name “early working exception.” Through the 2002 Amendment, the Bolar flex-
ibility was incorporated into Indian patent law. Recently, the Indian Bolar provision was put to the test
in court cases involving two different medications: one covered the cancer treatment Sorafenib Tosylate
(marketed under the Bayer brand name Nexavar), and the other covered the blood-thinning medication
Rivaroxaban (Bayer brand: Xarelto).

5.1. Inhibiting Factors for the Penetration of Generics

Taking a cue from the 6 Ps of marketing described earlier, various key elements for the penetration of
generic drugs can be discussed. The following are the concerns:

1.   Policies - Drugs that are getting off-patented are to be clearly demarcated as ethical drugs or branded
     drugs. Moreover, the off patented drugs should not be allowed to be sold as ethical branded drugs
     to improve the R&D in the industry. Also, the government should make a policy whereby the off
     patented drugs should be notified so that companies are able to augment their presence in those
     therapeutic categories.
2.   Product - The level of acceptance of the generic molecules also needs to improve at the prescriber
     level. Even though the generic drugs are being made available in India through huge chain of hos-



                                                                                                         97
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare




     pitals/ dispensaries/stores, yet still the revenue and the usage of these drugs is dismally low, about
     1%. In addition, the pharmacies need to be given quotas for sales of generics to augment their
     presence. Also, the bioavailability/ equivalence studies performed needs to be discussed among
     the prescribers, in order to improve their level of confidence about the drug.
3.   Price- Accessibility to affordable medicines still remains a significant public health priority for
     most governments’ world over. The government regularly conducts surveys to measure the impact
     of approved generic equivalents that can have on consumer generic drug prices. The major bar-
     rier in the pricing element of generics is that beyond a handful of government stores, namely Jan
     Aushadi Kendras, the benefit of low cost of generics is, by and large, not being passed to the final
     consumer, which makes its usage at dismal low.
4.   Place - Government of India has launched their much-awaited generic pharmacy chain called Jan
     Aushadi Scheme in 2008, which is now renamed as Pradhan Mantri Bharatiya Janaushadi Pariyojana
     (PMBJP), to ensure that generic drugs are largely made accessible to majority of the population with
     bare minimum cost. Also, in one of the recent developments, One India pharmacy, private generic
     pharmacy chain, has opened more than 550 store and are looking to expand further. However, just
     these two chains of stores alone cannot make these generic drugs accessible to 140 billion plus
     people. More pharmacies need to be brought into operation to be able to help the distribution of
     generics in the country. However, reach, geography and cost of distribution concerns will present
     a major bottleneck that they will have to iron out.
5.   Promotion - The pharmaceutical industry uses number of marketing communication channels to
     ensure that their prescribers stay updated to the latest developments in the medicine field. But this
     is true in the case of ethical branded drugs where the cost of drugs is low and they are being sold
     at high prices, thus providing better profitability. However, pharma companies are hardly spending
     on the promotion of generic drugs as the profitability is bare minimum in comparison to it coun-
     terpart. Notwithstanding, whatsoever being the profit, most of it gets evaporated in the distribution
     cost. All the pharmaceutical companies can push for at least one of their generic offerings to be
     promoted by their sales teams, along with their branded drugs, which can not only improve the level
     of awareness of generics among the populace, but also help to bring about efficiency in operations.
     This could also tweak the interest of the pharmacists and druggists in dispensing generics.
6.   People - Previous studies carried out in the domain have revealed key strategic insights and have
     found that the entire research theme could be grouped into three clusters for the better adoption of
     the generic drugs among the people.
     (i) Improving the attitude towards generic medicine through public awareness programs and
           public policy.
     (ii) Conducting many more bioequivalence studies to address the safety and risk indications of
           generic drugs. These could help build confidence among health professionals in their prescrip-
           tion behaviors.
     (iii) Making dispensing of generics economically viable even for pharmacists, through exploration
           of various business models.

    So, from the above discussion it can be gleaned that the habits of doctors and pharmacists are key
to adoption and are also apparently very difficult to change. Although laws allow a pharmacist to sub-
stitute a generic equivalent for a branded drug (generic substitution), unless the prescription indicates
not to, however such substitution was not that common. Additionally, there aren’t any generic players


98
﻿
Marketing Strategies of Big Pharma and Impact of Generics in Healthcare




in many of the drug segments; for example, the medicine producer Valeant abruptly raised the price of
isoproterenol from $440 to around $2,700 each dose, sending the price of a single drug for inpatient use
soaring from $300,000 to $1.9 million. Isoproterenol is used as heart medication. The drug helps in the
indication of heart attacks and works to keep patient’s blood pressure within range.
    When the price of isoproterenol suddenly increased, the typical response would have been to stop
using it and start looking for alternatives; however, hospitals began stockpiling up in pharmacy backup
boxes so they would always have the essential medication on hand. The medication isoproterenol was
once patented but is no longer. Therefore, in theory, any pharmaceutical company should be able to
produce a generic version and market it at a reasonable price. But the generic version never appeared
as it ought to have.
    The credibility of independent businesses that produce generic drugs was also damaged by a con-
troversy in 1989. Generic drug producers were accused of fabricating laboratory tests and buying FDA
officials to approve their products. This incident made it even more difficult for doctors and pharma-
cists to switch out branded medications for generic ones. By the beginning of the 1990s, a number of
significant pharmaceutical companies stepped up to fill the void by creating their own divisions to sell
generics or by acquiring stakes in businesses that supplied generics. In 2015, more than one recall of a
generic medication product occurred every other day, totaling 267 product recalls. These product recalls
were carried out due to quality problems, such as improper product dissolution, contamination, or even
blatant fake products. In another instance, two generic drug producers are accused of forming a cartel
to set artificially high pricing for ranitidine, the generic version of the ulcer medication Zantac.
    Traditional medicine fills a significant gap in modern medicine in developing nations like India.
Industry estimates indicate that 70% of Indians use traditional medicines as a complement to, and oc-
casionally instead of, pharmaceutical medications. All these factors collectively inhibit the adoption of
generic medicines and realization of its true potential.


6. CONCLUSION

The usage of generic medicines will provide considerable savings to overall healthcare systems. With
the increasing pressure on government budgets and containment of healthcare expenses, the window
of opportunity for generics may stay open for long. The promotion of these drugs among the prescrib-
ers are quite needed for a country like India where no formal social security system exists. The present
business environment, pose the entire generics industry with two opposing forces that complicate their
profitability and future growth. On the one hand, demand for generics continues to rise in India. The
demand for generics shall remain strong, as the next wave of patent expiries will create additional busi-
ness opportunities for those companies who will get the license for selling these off-patented drugs. At
the same time, there are some global barriers like inflation, supply constraints of generic molecules and
deteriorating health of economies. While these macro forces could be beyond the reach of pharmaceutical
companies to control, individual marketing strategies are very well in their grasp to mold and augment
generic medicine growth. Such strategies will have to adopt a wholistic approach, understanding the
concerns of all the stakeholders involved – government agencies, pharma companies, pharmacists and
patients. Only then a sustainable adoption of generics can be achieved and thus furthering the cause of
universal health coverage and health equity in the country.

                                                        Chapter 6
      Sustainability and Healthcare
      Marketing in the Digital Age
                                                     Iza Gigauri
                                        https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6394-6416
                              St. Andrew the First-Called Georgian University, Georgia


ABSTRACT
Healthcare as a dynamic and complex industry requires marketing to achieve business goals and im-
prove the well-being of society. Transformations in the environment, increased competition, changes
in consumer behavior force organizations to adapt to the altered landscape. In this regard, marketing
plays a pivotal role in defining consumers’ attitudes, motivations, and perceptions, building relationships
with stakeholders, developing a strategy, and creating value. Moreover, the aging population, chronic
diseases, prolonged pains and disabilities, poverty and poor health conditions, and lack of reliable medi-
cal services cause an increased burden not only on the healthcare system but also on the economy and
society in all counties. Sustainable Development Goals aim to solve the pressing challenges facing the
world. The objectives of this chapter are to discuss healthcare marketing in light of the twin transition:
sustainability and digitalization. The chapter illustrates sustainable healthcare marketing opportunities
in relation to digital advancements including ChatGPT.



1. INTRODUCTION

Demand for marketing in healthcare organizations is growing to effectively communicate with patients,
suppliers, governments, and providers but also to successfully respond to their needs and wants. The
consumer for healthcare marketing is not only an individual patient or a physician but also companies
purchasing care for their employees as well as insurance companies (Berkowitz, 2021).
   Marketing communication is an essential part of health promotion as health marketing relates to
health promotion programs. According to the definition of WHO (n.d.), “Health promotion is the
process of enabling people to increase control over, and to improve, their health. It moves beyond
a focus on individual behavior towards a wide range of social and environmental interventions”.
Studies demonstrate that health promotion including education prevents diseases, reduces health

DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch006



       Copyright © 2024, IGI Global. Copying or distributing in print or electronic forms without written permission of IGI Global is prohibited.
﻿
Sustainability and Healthcare Marketing in the Digital Age




risks, and stimulates behavior changes (Pham et al., 2020; Basińska-Zych & Springer, 2021). Thus,
the integration of health promotion and social marketing can add value to health improvement and
increase effectiveness.
    Healthcare marketing is characterized by consumers instead of products or services. For this
reason, marketing aims to create a long-term relationship with consumers in order to ensure their
satisfaction and loyalty. Marketing aims at understanding the health needs of the target population
and responding to the demands with accessible products and services. In addition, the reputation
and image of healthcare institutions influence patients’ choices. Moreover, marketing can improve
healthcare quality, gain employee satisfaction, and allocate human, financial, or material resources
efficiently. Furthermore, marketing increases awareness of healthcare issues among the population
and contributes to sustainability.
    A successful marketing strategy should begin with the research of consumers’ needs, desires, and
opinions based on which new or improved services must be offered by a healthcare organization. It
should be taken into account that patients’ behaviors and attitudes are affected by families and friends
and hence, can change across countries (Syed et al., 2021). Only under such conditions, it is possible to
acquire and maintain consumers, earn income, and gain a competitive advantage in the market. Research,
based on a systematic review of relevant papers, confirm the significance of marketing strategies and
technological progress for healthcare organizations (Syed et al., 2021).
    Recently introduced advanced technologies enable healthcare consumers to access various informa-
tion and special offers through the Internet, evaluate healthcare services, and share their experiences
(Berkowitz, 2021). Patients now know their needs and assess alternatives. Consumers are increasingly
seeking values whilst transparency and global competition become paramount (Berkowitz, 2021; Syed
et al., 2021).
    Researchers underline the importance of communication in healthcare marketing which can be
performed through electronic means such as television, outdoor billboards, and especially social media
(Syed et al., 2021). Studies show that a huge number of people worldwide use social media and even
advertisements directing customers to the Facebook page of a company are considered to be reliable
and less annoying than those leading visitors to the brand’s webpage (Pelet & Ettis, 2022). However,
marketing activities promoting health-related issues can be performed with utmost caution given the
powerful effect of social media. For this reason, governments tend to pose regulations on advertise-
ments in order to protect patients since they are unable to distinguish the authenticity of messages
(Syed et al., 2021).
    In order to address the pressing challenges, the world has faced, Sustainable Development Goals
(SDGs) have been initiated by the United Nations. Since then, sustainability has become a buzzword.
Organizations of all types and sizes are expected to contribute to SDGs. The joint efforts of various
agents should ameliorate people’s lives on Earth.
    Against this background, the purpose of this chapter is to examine healthcare marketing in light of
the twin transition - Sustainability and Digitalization. The chapter illustrates sustainable marketing op-
portunities for healthcare organizations and establishes the links between healthcare marketing, digital
transformation, and Sustainability. Social marketing, digital technologies, particularly chat GPT, and
ethical considerations are also discussed.




                                                                                                      105
                                                                                                          ﻿
                                                Sustainability and Healthcare Marketing in the Digital Age




2. SUSTAINABILITY HEALTHCARE MARKETING

2.1. Sustainable Marketing

The importance of sustainable marketing is growing strongly associated with consumer behavior and
preferences. Consumers’ purchase decisions have changed as they become aware of pressing environ-
mental and social issues demanding that brands integrate responsibility and sustainability aspects in
their products and services. Moreover, a new transformative role of marketing has been widely discussed
with regard to consumerism and sustainability (Gigauri & Djakeli, 2021). Traditional marketing is often
referred to as manipulative, unethical, and irresponsible, causing pollution and excessive consumption
problems. For this reason, marketing evolved towards a responsible and ethical direction, adopting a
fair, honest, transparent, and stakeholder-oriented approach.
    The paradigm shift from traditional to sustainable marketing has induced an expansion of the research
agenda in the field. Yet agreement on the definitions and related terms is not reached among scholars.
Martin and Schouten (2014) define sustainable marketing as “the process of creating, communicating,
and delivering value to customers in such a way that both natural and human capital are preserved or
enhanced throughout”. Furthermore, Dahlstrom and Crosno (2022) describe sustainable marketing “as
the study of all efforts to consume, produce, distribute, promote, package, and reclaim products in a
manner to achieve ecological, economic, and social objectives”. In this sense, sustainability-oriented
marketing considers environmental concerns and tries to decrease the negative impacts of marketing
activities on the environment, builds the relationships with all stakeholders of a company, and promotes
social equity and ethical behavior (Belz & Peattie, 2012).
    Kemper and Ballantine (2019) suggest that sustainability marketing unifies both sustainable marketing
and green marketing and hence, sustainability marketing, sustainable marketing, and green marketing are
represented under one term of ‘Sustainability Marketing’. This perspective proposes that sustainability
is incorporated into marketing mix, promotes sustainable consumption, defines the role of marketing in
contributing to sustainable development, takes responsibility for social well-being, and leads to consum-
ers’ healthy choices.
    Thus, sustainable marketing integrates traditional commercial marketing with relationship marketing,
environmental or green marketing, ethical marketing, social marketing, and SDGs. Sustainable market-
ing incorporates sustainability into marketing programs. In this context, sustainable marketing aims to
satisfy consumer needs and consider economic, environmental, and social aspects simultaneously (Belz
& Peattie, 2012; Lloveras et al., 2022).
    The special focus of sustainable marketing on the consumer is a distinctive characteristic of healthcare
marketing centering on patient satisfaction. Consumer needs are studied to develop marketing programs
and the marketing mix is changed in order to better respond to sustainability goals. Although marketing
has put emphasis on customer satisfaction in order to increase profit, the sustainable marketing concept
has also addressed ecological and social factors paving the way to ethical, green, and social marketing
ultimately merging into the idea of sustainable marketing (Martín-Cervantes et al., 2022; Gigauri, Palazzo
& Ferri, 2023). The concept strives to create and maintain long-term relationship instead of short-run
profit. Sustainable marketing enables organizational change in favor of sustainability while recognizing
and using strategic opportunities.




106
﻿
Sustainability and Healthcare Marketing in the Digital Age




2.2. Social Marketing for Healthcare Services

Social marketing comes into play to implement healthcare marketing programmes and to find solu-
tions in terms of inclusiveness, of serving customers with diverse needs, of improving quality of life,
and participating in value co-creation (Gil Guzman et al., 2021). Effective healthcare project requires
stakeholder participation, which can be ensured through social marketing. Successful social marketing
includes appropriate products/ services developed in response to community needs, a proper place for
them to be delivered, affordable prices, and effective promotion strategies, but social marketing also
encompasses financial sustainability, organization sustainability, and market sustainability (Aras, 2011).
    Studies show that social marketing aims at reaching different groups of people to improve their
behavior in favor of public health while also focusing on the sustainability aspects (Aras, 2011). More-
over, social marketing can identify barriers to behavioral change and design appropriate interventions
(Morris & Clarkson, 2009). Since conditions and institutional norms influence individuals’ behavior,
social marketing can lead to healthy habits and create an enabling environment for informed decisions
and better choices.
    Scholars advise planning social marketing campaigns to accomplish health goals based on three sets:
identifying the social problem, describing the benefits of changed behavior, and developing the mar-
keting mix (Da Silva & Mazzon, 2016). Although social marketing resembles commercial marketing,
its objectives are different. Still, social marketing uses commercial marketing tools. Social marketing
aims at changing the behavior of individuals to improve life in societies. Consequently, social market-
ing programmes should be appropriately developed for the country’s context, main actors should act
coordinately, persuasive communication designed to change behavior should be implemented, benefits
should be emphasized, and sustainability should be considered (Donovan, 2011; Marchioli, 2006; Ll-
overas et al., 2022).
    In order to facilitate health and social changes, social marketing can arrange communication and
educational campaigns (Weinreich, 2010). Using various communication strategies and marketing tools
such as media, healthcare providers, promotion, and community outreach, social marketing encourages
healthy behavior.
    According to Henley et al. (2011), social marketing for the healthcare industry involves the follow-
ing aspects:

•    Consumer’s perspectives - as their interests are considered in the planning process;
•    Marketing activities and plans are in agreement with the target audience and management, accom-
     plishment, and assessment are carried out accordingly;
•    Communication messages and channels are directed to reach the target audience;
•    Public satisfaction is increased due to marketing research activities and healthcare campaign re-
     sults are achieved;
•    Individual behavior is changed as market research determines a specific health problem that needs
     to be addressed (Henley et al., 2011).

    Studies analyzed social marketing programmes from 2005-2017 around the world found that social
media including Facebook was mainly used for sharing information, connecting target populations,
raising awareness, and reaching disadvantaged groups (Shawky et al., 2019). Moreover, social media
marketing impacts brand loyalty, trust, and business sustainability (Veseli-Kurtishi, 2018; Ibrahim &


                                                                                                      107
                                                                                                          ﻿
                                                Sustainability and Healthcare Marketing in the Digital Age




Aljarah, 2018). Consequently, companies effectively utilizing social and digital marketing can increase
sales and improve branding strategies (Dwivedi et al., 2021).
   Thus, social marketing using various communication channels can improve public health and soci-
ety’s quality of life. Therefore, healthcare marketing can apply social marketing tools to successfully
reach its goals.

2.3. Healthcare Marketing Addressing Sustainable Development Goals

The term ‘Sustainable Development’ has its roots in the Report of the World Commission on the Envi-
ronment and Development (WCED). In particular, Norwegian Prime Minister Gro Harlem Brundtland
published his report “Our Common Future” in 1987, where he defined the concept as “development
that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their
own needs” (WCED, 1987).
    Since the Brundtland Report, the term has spread around the globe and attracted attention from policy-
makers, governments, scholars, professionals, and educators. Additionally, ‘Sustainable Development’
entered the everyday language (Lloveras et al., 2022) and has become a matter of discussion in almost all
areas of society. The concept is related to Elkington’s Triple Bottom Line (TBL) known for three inter-
connected dimensions of sustainability - economic, social, and environmental facets (Elkington, 1998).
    Later, the United Nations introduced Sustainability Development Goals as a response to Grand Chal-
lenges. The 17 SDGs are the most recent global policy to ensure societal welfare and prosperity (Lloveras
et al., 2022). This development has been also reflected in the marketing efforts of business enterprises.
Marketing has been transforming towards sustainability while taking into consideration all stakeholders
and developing innovative products or services (Bocken, 2021). Since health issues impede the quality
of life, SDG 3 is dedicated to the target “Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages”
(UN, n.d.), nonetheless the rest sixteen goals, directly or indirectly, are related to improving the health
of people and promoting healthy lifestyle. In this respect, marketing has a pivotal role to play to promote
SDGs and tackle healthcare issues.
    Health marketing contains elements from health promotion, commercial marketing, and communica-
tion to provide health-related information to the general public. Health marketing campaigns can raise
public awareness of health issues, health protection, new treatment opportunities, or a healthy lifestyle.
With respect to sustainable development, a healthcare system should develop the infrastructure that
manages and ameliorates people’s health and educates them about healthy behavior, as well as prevents
illnesses (Popa et al., 2022).
    The purpose of healthcare marketing is to attract consumers, accompany them throughout healthcare
service, and maintain them through public relations and communication efforts. Healthcare marketing
strategy encompasses segmentation and targeting activities using both online and offline channels. Ac-
cordingly, healthcare Marketing helps organizations to invite prospective patients by increasingly de-
livering values and advantages as the competition in the industry is high. By the same token, healthcare
marketing activities should be engaged in the relationships with patients through multiple channels to
keep them long-term and enhance their loyalty to the brand and healthcare company. In order to redesign
and strengthen strategy, healthcare marketing also contains performance evaluation and measurement
activities.
    In addition, digitalization can contribute to SDGs by improving the accessibility of disadvantaged
and vulnerable groups of the population to various services they may need (Gigauri et al., 2023). Digital


108
﻿
Sustainability and Healthcare Marketing in the Digital Age




progress stimulates innovation, brings new approaches to processing information, enables digital com-
munication, and facilitates dealing with challenges (Gigauri, Apostu & Popescu, 2023; Pelet, Belda &
Arezki, 2023). Digital tools occupy a prominent place as users of online communication channels are
growing around the world. For example, the majority of patients tend to search healthcare facilities through
search engines (e.g. Google) and book their appointments online. Patients have access to health-related
information through digital technologies and use it. However, overwhelming information can confuse
patients, a solution of which can be a company’s website serving consumers with reliable and accessible
information. Moreover, thanks to numerous health and wellness applications and gadgets, patients have
greater influence over their health.
    Therefore, marketing becomes progressively vital to reach consumers in such a competitive market.
An online presence can enhance branding position and increase revenues. Furthermore, data-driven mar-
keting analysis as well as digitalized data enables healthcare organizations to better predict and observe
diseases, to improve health management and patient care (Moro Visconti & Morea, 2019).
    Currently, there is a considerable lack of studies related to healthcare marketing from the perspec-
tive of sustainable development as the scholars were unable to identify any research themes through the
comprehensive bibliometric analysis (Popa et al., 2022). Consequently, the examination of healthcare
marketing in the context of sustainability is important for the implementation of SDGs but also for so-
ciety’s well-being. Marketing seems to have a part in sustainable healthcare strategies.


3. DIGITAL MARKETING FOR HEALTHCARE AND ETHICAL CONSIDERATIONS

3.1. Digital Technologies, Marketing, and Ethical Concerns

Technologies revolutionized medicine and have shaped the healthcare industry. Trendy technologies
expand marketing efforts in healthcare. Technological advancements such as the Internet of Things
(IoT), Machine Learning (ML), Robots, AI, cloud computing, etc. are used in the healthcare system to
enhance patient satisfaction and assist professionals in medical care and treatment (Mondal et al., 2022;
Popescu et al., 2022). Specifically, ML and IOT are applied for healthcare digital marketing in order to
target consumers, improve search engine optimization, and make better strategic decisions (Mondal et
al., 2022).
    Digital marketing enables healthcare professionals to promote their services and disseminate marketing
messages around the globe while improving the reputation of healthcare companies (Mondal et al., 2022).
    Studies emphasize the positive impact of digital marketing on consumers behaviors concerning
healthcare and social media campaigns becoming an effective tool for health promotion, in particular,
pain management, health education, mental health awareness, as well as for preventing disease, and
adopting healthy attitudes (Rogers et al., 2017; Buchanan et al., 2018; O’Reilly et al., 2019; De Veirman
et al., 2019; Murphy et al., 2020; Günther et al., 2021; Alonzo & Popescu, 2021).
    On the other hand, or exactly due to its powerful influence, ethical considerations need to be taken
into account while applying digital marketing tools. In public health, ethical issues are related to data
privacy and patient protection (Clar et al., 2014). While using digital marketing campaigns to provide
consumers with health education and self-care benefits, misunderstanding health information can lead
to certain risks (Huntsman & Bulaj, 2023). To avoid dangers, scholars indicate that ethical marketing
strategy needs to be integrated into health advertising activities (Huntsman & Bulaj, 2023).


                                                                                                        109
                                                                                                            ﻿
                                                  Sustainability and Healthcare Marketing in the Digital Age




    Moreover, diversity and inclusion should be incorporated into healthcare marketing. Consumers re-
quire diverse products and services and individualized treatment. Inclusive healthcare marketing directs
its efforts towards disadvantageous populations and plans marketing strategy in a way to address their
needs and wants. In this respect, inclusive marketing aims to conquer inequality in the healthcare domain
by embracing inclusiveness and equality and considering underrepresented individuals in marketing
communication. Diversity, equity, and inclusion are parts of marketing efforts in healthcare. As a result,
marketing effectiveness and consumer well-being can be boosted. Marketing objectives comprise studying
target markets, developing tailored products and services, and connecting with target consumers. Through
health marketing activities, it is possible to overcome discrimination, stigma, and stereotypical mindset.
    Since inclusive marketing is essential in healthcare, the World Wide Web Consortium has published
resources for the accessibility of digital marketing platforms (W3C, 2023). As a result, marketers, web
designers, and social media professionals can adopt the main principles of accessible digital marketing
including seen and heard content, user-friendly navigation from different devices, and understandable
content design.

3.2. ChatGPT for Healthcare Marketing

After technological developments comprising digital tools, social media, and Artificial Intelligence
(AI), new applications such as ChatGPT are assessing now for healthcare. GPT - Generative Pretrained
Transformer that can write like a person based on AI that creates a language model ChatGPT, which can
be used in the healthcare system for improving patient care, planning, and evaluating treatment possibili-
ties (Javaid et al., 2023). As a chatbot, it can answer questions, for which it must access to correct and
current medical data and without which ChatGPT cannot provide reliable recommendations, existing
treatment possibilities, or healthcare solutions (Javaid et al., 2023). Therefore, data should be updated
consistently to ensure improved results in ChatGPT responses. Consumer privacy and data security plays
also here an important role (Javaid et al., 2023; Mijwil et al., 2023).
    In addition to physicians, also patients can use it to retrieve information about their disease or symptoms,
support their health education, manage their chronic illnesses or pains, as well as schedule appointments
according to their needs (Javaid et al., 2023). Furthermore, ChatGPT can analyze marketing data and
segment consumers based on their behavior or needs and hence, ensuring a better experience through
targeted marketing campaigns and by responding to questions on various online platforms (Javaid et al.,
2023; Kung et al., 2023). A number of patients can be treated simultaneously and hence, improve health
service and patient satisfaction as ChatGPT delivers information and gives answers on different subjects
(Javaid et al., 2023). It generates text data from various sources rapidly (Alkaissi & McFarlane, 2023),
and therefore, can be utilized for social media content. Figure 1 summarizes how ChatGPT can be used
in healthcare focusing on its application for marketing.
    Yet, ChatGPT lacks emotional support and tone of human responses, is less individualized, and is
unable to react or understand users emotionally, which makes it less suitable for mental health services
(Javaid et al., 2023).
    Thus, digital marketing technologies must be carefully selected for effective healthcare marketing in
order to, on the one hand, reach marketing objectives and, on the other hand, engage in ethical conduct.




110
﻿
Sustainability and Healthcare Marketing in the Digital Age



Figure 1. ChatGPT in healthcare marketing
Source: Author’s elaboration based on Javaid et al., 2023.




4. CONCLUSION

This chapter has been dedicated to the potential connection between healthcare marketing and sustain-
able development agenda with an emphasis on digitalization. The relationship between sustainability
and healthcare marketing is becoming prevalent in the current digital age. Sustainable marketing is a
comprehensive approach to healthcare marketing as it aims to maintain equilibrium in economic, social,
and environmental performance while generating value for all stakeholders.
    Marketing, especially in the healthcare sector needs to pay attention to privacy and other ethical issues
when using digital technologies. Social media can be integrated into social marketing programmes in
the healthcare industry to communicate with the target population and encourage their healthy behavior.
Health promotion and communication marketing campaigns should be planned and implemented care-
fully taking into consideration risks of misinterpretation of disseminated information.
    Eventually, AI cannot replace humans but can be used to serve humans in automated tasks. ChatGPT
is beneficial for healthcare marketing by responding to questions, performing automate processes, and


                                                                                                         111
                                                                                                            ﻿
                                                  Sustainability and Healthcare Marketing in the Digital Age




analyzing data leading to enhanced customer experience and ultimately increased sales. This ground-
breaking innovation tends to boost AI capacities with a simple and convenient interface creating agree-
able customer service expanding patient satisfaction.
    Thus, digital tools and technological progress enhance the healthcare marketing domain for achieving
better results effectively. Further applications of sustainability and digital aspects as well as the influence
of emerging trends of sustainable development and digital transformation are yet to be explored. Since
scholars devoted little attention to healthcare marketing with the relationship to sustainable development,
further research should explore this field theoretically and empirically to create knowledge and provide
insight into this interrelated area.

 Artificial Intelligence in Medical
      and Healthcare Service:
                       Applications and Challenges
