                                                        Chapter 8
     The Relevance of Literacy in
    Digital Health for Success of
    Healthcare and Its Marketing:
Navigating Towards Digital Health Literacy

                                                       Mario Sierra Martin
                                                    Universidad de Málaga, Spain

                                                     Pilar Alarcón Urbistondo
                                                    Universidad de Málaga, Spain


ABSTRACT
This chapter represents a significant effort to understand and present a comprehensive overview of
telemedicine and digital literacy in the context of healthcare in contemporary society. To achieve this, a
thorough review of the most relevant and current studies conducted in the last five years has been carried
out. One of the crucial aspects addressed in this chapter is the identification of barriers that hinder the
adoption of telemedicine. Additionally, it examines the drivers that promote the adoption of telemedicine.
These may include convenience, time and money savings, accessibility to quality healthcare services,
and the ability to continuously monitor medical conditions from the comfort of one’s home.



1. INTRODUCTION

Digital health is seen as a new possibility to interact with the Health System; it has actually entirely
changed the way we communicate with practitioners and handle medical consultations. Internet related
topics have been addressed many times by researchers and so have social network issues; their impact
on the health sector is of interest for the community because of the drastic changes they represent.




DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch008



       Copyright © 2024, IGI Global. Copying or distributing in print or electronic forms without written permission of IGI Global is prohibited.
                                                                                                          ﻿
                                                               The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




    Digitalization has invaded all sectors, and healthcare is no exception to that.
    For over a decade now, the use of new technologies has emerged into societal manners; these play
an increasingly important role and have a deep impact on society itself (Paramio-Pérez et al., 2015).
Today, both patients and professionals have changed the way they behave to handle health matters. The
health sector is experiencing changes to apprehend, for example, a medical consultation, or some in-
tercommunication between practitioner and patient, or even the tools used to search any kind of health
information on the Internet.
    In this context, easy access to the internet and, constant information updates are responsible for
patients to use this tool as their main source on health matters (Serhrouchni & Malmartel, 2021). This
new trend and, the collapse of health systems observed today in many countries, is promoting both the
Internet and social networks as the main advertising sources, when trying to get answers on possible
health doubts or issues (Serhrouchni & Malmartel, 2021).
    This tendency intensified during the COVID-19 pandemic, when the health sector started to make
a more intense use of technologies to provide medical information, while respecting social distancing;
the health sector had been quite suspicious towards its digitalization needs until then with doctors and
patients feeling quite reluctant to attend virtual consultations.
    The convergence and integration of Internet and related technologies into the health sector shows
in new concepts like the “E-Health” or the “Telemedicine”. Today, about 39% of the global audience
is making use of telemedicine. These technological advances progress every day by leaps and bounds.
There is, however, a digital divide which means that with technology advances, digital health literacy
becomes an issue for health equity (Zamora & Clingerman, 2011). It is true that digital health is evolving
significantly and provides benefits for all, most particularly in the way certain medical care challenges
are addressed; it provides new opportunities to improve patient diagnosis.
    Today, we can differentiate seven main large blocks inside the digital health sector:

1.    Telemedicine: With the use of ICT tools, medical consultations are carried out remotely.
2.    Automation of processes and services: This is contributing to take more efficient and effective
      decisions, thanks to the existence of automatic controls, continuous updates and improvements that
      reduce the margin of error in diagnosis.
3.    mHealth: These are all kinds of mobile applications used to facilitate the access of patients and
      practitioners to records, guidelines, or to more secure control means.
4.    Wearables: These are electronic devices used to monitor different indicators on individual health
      like: smart watches, bracelets...
5.    Virtual Reality: Patients have access to surgical simulations to reduce their insecurity levels; they
      get the most precise and updated information with a touch of realism.
6.    Augmented Reality: This is used mainly by health professionals, to enrich virtual reality represen-
      tations with added elements that would remain otherwise hidden. Both the rehabilitation process
      and remote operations can be improved with this technique.
7.    Big Data and Artificial Intelligence: The access to large amounts of real-time and updated infor-
      mation opens new horizons for health researchers. These tools present -altogether or separately- a
      wide potential to advance and improve in digital health.

   Professionals need to carry on improving the level of technological literacy within society and provide
new ways to make these tools more available.


132
﻿
The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




   According to Paramio et al. (2015), digital health literacy is “the ability to search, find, understand
and evaluate, health information derived from electronic sources, and apply the knowledge acquired to
address or solve a health problem”.
   Medical professionals must consider the digital health literacy of their consumers. Developing tele-
medicine also means practitioners must have sufficient digital knowledge. An assessment of the current
digital literacy situation in health and the detection of improvement interventions can ease communication
between medical professionals and patients (Baker et al., 2007; DeWalt, Dilling, Rosenthal, & Pignone,
2007; Schillinger, 2002; Yin et al., 2007).
   Today, more than ever, there is a wide range of opportunities available to transform the health sector
and involve the two fundamental acting parts: doctors and patients. However, the digital divide cannot
be radically addressed. This is why it is important to differentiate the two types of literacies entailed:
digital literacy and digital health literacy.


2. THE DIGITAL TRANSFORMATION OF HEALTHCARE:
LITERACY AND TELEMEDICINE IN TODAY’S SOCIETY

2.1 Digital Literacy

Technology is not inherent to the human being. On the contrary, technology is a transformation pro-
cess that is to be learned progressively. The great challenge for technology within society is for users
to become fully autonomous to then make the most of it. Our modern world has seen Information and
Communication Technologies (ICT) rapidly address all layers of society and, transform the way we com-
municate or share information. Internet is now one of the most popular daily activities for a large part
of the population worldwide; its importance is increasingly evident and so are the progressive changes
experienced in different sectors.
    Currently, the term ICT is defined as the use of digital technologies to improve information and com-
munication in real time (Sarkar, 2012). ICT plays an important role in the daily life of users; it improves
their quality of life and has become an indispensable part of society.
    All these facts lead to a key question before ICTs can carry on its advances and developments into
the community: Is society really prepared to face such rapid digital advances? The use of digital tech-
nologies requires of some specific skills and competencies to perform tasks, adapt, and solve problems
within digital environments (Fu, 2013; Sarkar, 2012; Martin & Grudziecki, 2006).
    The twenty-first century society is multimodal, which means that all contents generated -either
educational or informative- are expressed, produced, and distributed using different supports (paper,
screens) to do so, different technologies are used (computers, mobiles, internet networks ...) (Salcedo
Aparicio et al, 2022). All this is creating a revolution and, for over a decade now, the need to incorporate
new means to help develop a good digital literacy is felt as a necessity.
    Although these technologies offer different possibilities to improve users ‘quality of life, there needs to
be an increasing commitment between all organizations to develop good digital literacy. These skills are
essential to adapt and reduce the existing digital divide that is present in a large part of our global society.
    The ‘digital literacy’ term was first employed by Paul Glister, in 1997, in his “Digital Literacy” volume.
He used elements of the critical literacy by Len Masterman and defined the concept as the ‘the ability to




                                                                                                            133
                                                                                                          ﻿
                                                               The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




understand and use information in multiple formats from a wide range of sources when it is presented
via computers”. Glister’s proposal is based on several skills used to face digitization on the Internet:

•     Ability for the user to generate value judgments through all the information provided by the
      internet.
•     Ability to obtain and evaluate their own opinion, avoiding bias.
•     Creation of a ‘personal information strategy’ based on the different sources of information and
      mechanisms.
•     Caution to judge the veracity of the information obtained through internet links.

    As technologies advances are produced, the concept of digital literacy is expanding; according to
Fu (2013), we can consider digital literacy as a set of skills that is necessary to become competent. It is
focused mainly on getting skills for the use of information and communication. To achieve a high level
of digital literacy, twenty-first century persons need to get the same starting opportunities, which is, an
access to digital tools and to the digital world. Non digitally literate users will therefore confront more
limitations when trying to progress, than the literate ones (Burnett et al., 2014). Progression towards
digitization has started -to a greater or lesser extent-, with digitization set as a final goal.
    The digital revolution, in which we are already immersed, has made the concept of digital literacy
very popular in our society and has aroused the interest of researchers; some of them asking for its inclu-
sion in different educational, political or health… programs (Reddy et al., 2020; Ata & Tilirim, 2019;
Kumar et al., 2019; Nand & Sharma, 2019).
    In short, all people now feel the need to be digitally qualified to face work, service or professional
challenges provided by digital communication environments. The digital divide can not only be produced
by the lack of equipment and supports that prevent any action in digital environments. It can also be
produced by the insufficient capacity to manage the information and communication presented online.
A consensus on the subject is that literacy is a very complex process today due to the exponential growth
of these digital environments. We present here three types of literacy levels, classified from the most
basic to the most complex. Learning how to use technology and then put it into practice or be able to
get the most out of it has become a necessity.
    -Learning to make use: The user is learning how to employ technological devices and handle the
digital tools available. The user acquires practical skills to continue advancing towards good digital
literacy. In this way, the user will experience a greater benefit by making use of 100% of the capabilities
that these technologies offer.
    -Understanding of use: Once the basic knowledge of these tools is acquired, users need to identify
and assess how these tools can affect their lives and facilitate that of others. So that they become part
of the patient’s daily routine.
    -Creation of digital contents: Finally, digital literacy should be a hand towards being connected, im-
prove communication, share experiences or, in our case, share diagnoses or medical results -a helping
tool for us and for others.
    Getting to know the different levels of literacy is important to advance in a more technological so-
ciety. Limiting oneself to a purely practical purpose is not sufficient, it is also important to understand
how technology can improve our quality of life and provide more practical digital solutions in health
environments. Currently, the main growth barrier for these digital healthcare tools is the low literacy
among a significant portion of society regarding the use and adoption of these tools.


134
﻿
The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




2.2 Telemedicine

When technology was gaining momentum during the last decades, new tools and new digital solutions
were created and progressively incorporated into our lives. Technological progress has emerged into
all sectors. The health sector, though later than others, is also being drastically altered by the incursion
of technologies (Matarín, TM., 2015), this is mainly due to the benefits provided by the use of these
technologies (Ekow Arkorful, V. et al.; 2019). As a result, the tendency has boosted and improved health
sector outcomes (Chang et al., 2004; Cline & Haynes, 2001; Kreps, 2006; Lorence, Park, & Fox, 2006;
Neuhauser & Kreps, 2003; Rippen & Yasnoff, 2004). The health sector has not been able yet, to adapt
quickly and develop improvement actions on productivity and patient satisfaction.
    The digital evolution in this sector has had a significant impact on the transformation and improve-
ment on the provision of services. Digital transformation should therefore be in constant evolution to
present novel products for both patients and physicians to help them provide the most accurate diagnosis,
supported by technological progress (Belliger, A., & Krieger, D.J., 2018).
    Some research findings evidence the positive impacts of telemedicine, while others report the un-
derlying complexities of such technological uses: social, cultural, financial and legal barriers, lack of
knowledge, low telemedicine literacy or evidence deficiency (Ekow Arkorful, V. et al.; 2019); too few
government policies regulating a new health system incorporating telemedicine (Ahern, Kreslake, &
Phalen, 2006) and lack of technological culture when providing health services (Lee, 2014).
    Telemedicine is not a new concept; in fact, it has showed to be quite successful in different health
areas. It emerges as an effective fusion of ICTs with health sciences. In this way, digital healthcare tools
are experiencing increasing growth due to the technological evolution of society.
    The concept of telemedicine was first coined in the 70s (Strehle, E.M., Shabde, N., 2006) when its
attractiveness and interest for society lied in the provision of remote health services (Allen, A., Allen,
D., 1995). Though before, in 1959, the Nebraska University already had started to use the first doctor
to patient telemedicine video consultations to transmit neurological tests performed (Marily, J., 1996).
    So, for years, practitioners have offered telemedicine services to their patients to simplify health
processes. Currently, due to the COVID-19 pandemic, we were forced to join that digital revolution,
when the force majeure completely changed the usual channels to provide health services (Gopal et
al., 2018). Physical consultations experienced a complete revolution with the use of telemedicine. And
though telemedicine had been existing for years, it was quite a challenge to adapt to these technologies
because only a small part of the population had previous experience of virtual consultations.
    It is interesting to observe how, in health emergency situations, a greater use of telemedicine technolo-
gies is adopted to combat local, national, and international crises (Smith AC, et al., 2020). Telemedicine
is sometimes used too by people living in geographically disadvantaged regions (Bashshur, 1995).
    In recent years, telemedicine has become a clear alternative to improve healthcare, with doctor / patient
social distancing issues. It reduces travel costs and increases access to high quality care in rural areas.
Such measures are important, but efforts to describe the added value of telemedicine may be different
for the parties involved (Greenhalgh et al., 2017). We can differentiate two types of telemedicine: syn-
chronous telemedicine, that is, both the doctor and the patient perform a consultation in real time (video
call, phone call, chats) or asynchronous telemedicine when the consultation is carried out in deferred
time (email, remote monitoring platforms, notifications by questionnaires) (Montserrat, J.M, et al., 2021).




                                                                                                         135
                                                                                                           ﻿
                                                                The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




2.2.1 Types of Telemedicine

Synchronous: real-time (e.g., video conferencing)
   Asynchronous: delayed results
   E-mails
   Remote monitoring with delayed reception
   Transmission and storage of tests
   Access to data collected from therapy sessions.
   Automated care and self-management systems
   Interactive voice response
   Web platform access
   Notifications via text messages
   Questionnaire notifications. App
Source: Montserrat, J.M, et al. (2021)

    The constant evolution in medical care has been optimized thanks to technological advancements. This
has improved the life expectancy of the population though, to do so, it is fundamental for health centers
to develop a protocol based on cutting-edge technologies and to educate their professionals in their uses.
    Several authors consider the existence of 3 necessary basic characteristics for the use of telemedicine:
1) A necessary easy and personalized use, because complexity might lead to abandonment; 2) Some
gradual implementation in the society to ensure good acceptance, even when not useful in all health
areas; and 3) Telemedicine should simplify and facilitate the management of clinical work. (Montserrat,
J.M, et al.; 2021).
    There are different elements of the mosaic called “Digital Health”. Telemedicine -limited to remote
health care services- is considered as the biggest revolution ever within health services, not only from a
more a technological point of view, but also at cultural and social levels, because it eased the access to
some healthcare evolution in the Twenty-first century.
    After the problems observed during the COVID-19, Telemedicine is presented as a quick and abrupt
solution to expand medical care (Tenforde, AS., et al., 2020).
    The population is already prepared to understand the concept of telemedicine, where technology is
included to improve medical provision; its uses have become much more ubiquitous in daily care.
    With the great acceptance of mobile phones in recent decades, technological applications in health
communication have intensified with the firm objective to perform consultations and patient monitor-
ing (Logan et al., 2007). Currently, telemedicine is used in many medical specialties. Some medical
centers even offer their own mobile application for patients to use teleconsultation or simply interact
with physicians.
    The main reason for medical professionals to increasingly use telemedicine is due to the high volume
of demand issued by our society.

2.2.2 Benefits of Telemedicine

Telemedicine has the necessary potential to respond the many and current challenges faced by our health
systems like rising costs, aging population, increasing loneliness, management of chronic conditions,
unequal access, and fragmentation/lack of continuity in care.


136
﻿
The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




•    Greater comfort: patients receive medical care without leave their homes. Sometimes a simple
     virtual consultation is enough to provide a diagnosis, this is time saving and often means less
     travel hassle.
•    Greater accessibility: Especially for those patients with difficulty of access to the nearest medical
     consultation, because of geographical issues.

2.3 Digital Literacy in Health

Health literacy is broadly defined as the “level of ability one has to obtain, to process, and understand
basic health information and services necessary to then make appropriate health decisions” (Seng et
al., 2023).
    As underlined before, during the last decades, the use of ICTs has intensified progressively, and their
there is now a recurrent activity in almost all sectors. Internet is an endless source of information, with
millions of people consulting health-related topics every single day. Digital health literacy is presented
as a crucial element as far as individual empowering is concerned because it enables them to make
informed decisions about their well-being. That’s why studying and researching to improve in this field
becomes of vital importance so that healthcare technology can continue to evolve and provide increas-
ing benefits to patients.
    People with higher levels of digital competence or literacy are empowered to play a more active role
in healthcare self-management. However, our society is still lacking good literacy and patients some-
times miss the necessary skills to search, evaluate or apply all health-related information available on
the Internet; the management and consultation of medical appointments through mobile devices being
quite difficult for them.
    Inadequate levels of digital health literacy have proved to be a widespread problem worldwide, despite
technological advances in recent decades.
    In our modern world, all the information we need is accessible. However, barriers persist within
society with the corresponding limit of it uses and benefits.
    Different barriers make it complicated for these technologies to be experienced widely. The digital
divide means that, on one hand, some do have access to the TCIs and to the corresponding information,
while others have not yet stepped proactively towards their use. Technological complexity is sometimes
overwhelming for part of our society; some people contemplate that frenzied evolution without being
able to adapt and become familiar with these digital tools. The lack of criteria to verify what type of
information extracted online might be accurate, is another barrier put forward by reluctant users towards
these technologies.
    To confront these barriers, health experts work on the need to increase digital health literacy levels.
As advanced tools keep progressing towards a more technological world where doctors and patients in-
teract digitally, there is an increased need to help users acquire such skills and adopt these technologies.
    Digital health literacy has been discussed, extensively and from different perspectives at the aca-
demic level, after Professor Scott K. Simonds first used the concept in 1974. Since then, it has gained
importance due to technological advances and is now considered as a global strategy - a strategy that
will encourage and improve the health system itself and the coverage of patients ‘needs when providing
more immediate responses.
    The vertiginous diffusion of health information on Internet together with the popular demand for
such information has increased progressively. The number of adults with high levels of digital skills


                                                                                                        137
                                                                                                            ﻿
                                                                 The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




to research, understand and evaluate the health information available on the Internet, is still scarce, as
are the resources and capacities needed to make good use of these digital technologies to handle health
matters. Health literacy is often influenced by other different individual characteristics like for example,
age, education levels, digital literacy skills, degree of motivation when seeking information or health
status. These variables that can alter the understanding and use of such technological tools.
    It has been evidenced that people with higher levels of digital health literacy have better therapeutic
compliance, lower capacity for self-management of their disease. That is the main reason for the existence
of a global strategy on these issues -where the main objective is to involve and educate the population so
as to counteract the existing divide in digital health literacy. To do so, it is fundamental to know more
about citizens’ competences and to raise awareness on the importance of acquiring such skills. When
otherwise, people tend to play a more passive role in the management of their health or that of their
relatives. Some of these competences considered as of importance for citizens to acquire a high level of
digital literacy in health, are as follows:

•     To know the importance of good digital health literacy in society.
•     To be able to identify the existing barriers for technological use to then eliminate or reduce them.
•     To know which are the reliable sources of information when conducting searches on health pre-
      vention or health care.
•     To know how to use the different applications available in each health system to get appointments
      or read diagnosis.

    ICTs used for health issues can potentially reduce medical disparities between citizens, regardless of
their geographical location, because they provide all people with information access, medical consulta-
tions or digital tools that are used for prevention and care.


3. TELEMEDICINE, DIGITAL DIVIDE, AND PATIENTS

The digital transformation of the health sector towards a more technological society should focus, above
all, on improving and facilitating health coverage for all and on accelerating the development and imple-
mentation of digital solutions. The World Health Organization (WHO) recognized digital health as a key
factor to guarantee -the access to universal health systems, -the protection against health emergencies
and, -the improvement of well-being for one billion people around the world. To address the importance
of the digital divide and to reveal the corresponding digital determinants in health is fundamental to
ensure the highest level of digital health literacy.
    In 2005, WHO already announced to member states it was about to “consider developing a long-term
strategic plan to develop and implement eHealth services and, develop information and communication
technology infrastructure for health” (WHO, 2005).
    But this plan was set up only in 2020, due to the COVID-19 pandemic -an event that changed com-
pletely the global health system- where medical assistance was firstly virtual so as to protect all patients.
The COVID-19 served as a driver towards digital transformation in the health sector.
    Although there has been changes, it is still quite common to see individuals with great limitations in
the use of telemedicine. Its implementation is still limited by the existing digital divide.




138
﻿
The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




    The digital divide is defined as the distance between people making daily use of new technologies
and, those with still no access to the Internet or with access to it but no know how.
    New technologies have played an important part in all these drastic societal changes since they have
accelerated this separation (the digital divide). Internet is a powerful binary communication tool that
emerged last century, though its benefits have been felt by a quite small portion of the world. Even so,
the proportion of poor people with access to an internet connection has increased in the past 5 years,
though this is not enough to reduce this digital divide. The accelerated pace of technological innovations
accentuates the digital divide and splits populations.
    The lack of technological infrastructure -especially in rural areas or less developed countries-, the
lack of digital tools and, even the absence or low internet connection levels, in remote areas is reverting
likewise into a lack of technological skills and, therefore, in low levels of digital literacy.
    Given the situation, we propose an analysis of health technology implementation to apprehend its
benefits and limitations for users.


4. METHODOLOGY

The first step to address the digital literacy issue is to identify the barriers blocking or slowing down the
implementation of telemedicine. To detect different variables easing the use of telemedicine services
and those representing a limitation towards the acceptance of these technologies.
    To comply with these objectives, we performed a systematic literature review (RSL), with a qualitative
approach, using an inductive analysis based on a non-experimental and cross-sectional design (Barrios
et al., 2021). For the research to be as truthful as possible and to ensure its validity and traceability, we
focussed on the PRISMA methodology (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-
Analyses), designed by Urrútia and Bonfill (2010). The PRISMA methodology is a statement aiming to
be a guide for the preparation of literature reviews (Yepes-Nuñez et al., 2021).
    During the first phase, we systematically reviewed the literature to locate previous studies on the
subject. We consulted several scientific databases of relevance for our topic with some international
results like - Web of Science (WoS) and Scopus. The choice of articles was guided by descriptors like
digital literacy, telemedicine, barriers and technological drivers; these were considered as alternate
concepts during the analysis.
    Please find in Figure 1, the work diagram representing the different phases our selection of articles
was submitted to, according to the PRISMA criteria.
    During the first RSL phase, a total of 467 articles were gathered as a result of the bibliographic search
in the following databases: WoS and Scopus. These searches were performed with the words mentioned
above. An extraction of 467 articles was made and they were manually incorporated to the Mendely
document manager. Of these documents obtained during the first phase, 84 articles were excluded by
search criteria and, 92 articles for being duplicates; the final list of selected articles included 291 items.
Of these 291 articles, 154 articles were excluded at the title level and, 76 at the abstract level; only 61
articles were considered valid for full-text evaluation. Finally, we extracted data from these 25 articles
that met the chosen inclusion criteria and focused on our object of study.




                                                                                                          139
                                                                                                            ﻿
                                                                 The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health



Figure 1. Article selection flowchart (adaptation PRISMA)




4.1 Telemedicine Drivers

After the bibliographic analysis of the 25 selected articles, we identified 8 factors as drivers that help
facilitate the use of digital tools specialized in health.

-     Level of health insurance coverage
-     Access to internet services
-     Access to technological devices
-     Savings on travel costs
-     Shortage of specialists
-     Work-life balance
-     Age
-     Confidentiality and privacy

    Level of health insurance coverage: The level of health insurance coverage is identified as one of
the facilitating variables for the use of telemedicine (Lattimore, et al., 2021; Eberly, et al., 2020; Whaley,
et al., 2020). Studies show that, a medical coverage including the possibility of a medical consultation
by phone, is helping patients adapt much better to these tools, as compared to those who do not have
any health insurance.
    Access to internet services: The relevance of access to Internet services as a key factor in accessing
telemedicine service is highlighted by researchers (Lattimore, et al., 2021). Internet offers the necessary
infrastructures to provide telemedicine services between practitioners and patients: it favors real-time
communication without interruptions. Internet access creates a digital bridge which urges patients to
make use of these technologies.



140
﻿
The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




    Access to technological devices: The Internet connection is a key factor because it helps patients
adopt digital technologies to respond health demands like telemedicine, though owing technological
devices is another key factor in this online communication with practitioners. The ownership of devices
that provide the opportunity to make a virtual consultation is a propeller for patients because it pushes
them to perform this type of online consultation (Chen, et al., 2022).
    Savings in travel costs: Making use of health technology applications also means saving money;
an average of $ 147 to $ 186 per consultation, according to data published by the JAMA Network Open
journal. But it not only implies financial savings, it also time saving with an average of 2.9 hours (round
trip) and, 1.2 hours in the clinic per consultation. This is particularly important as far as work-life balance
is concerned. A balance between professional and personal life that is often difficult to obtain -for in
person medical appointments for example-. That is when savings and facility of use for patients carrying
out a virtual consultation, are seen progressively as necessary measure.
    Shortage of specialists: Telemedicine allows patients living in remote geographical areas to make
a medical consultation with a wide catalog of available specialists. To attend health problems, many
patients travel around -even outside their city- to get a medical consultation with a specialist, or expert,
in their condition. Making use of telemedicine helps patients connect remotely with doctors from dif-
ferent geographical areas and receive accurate diagnosis.
    Work-life balance: As already mentioned, not only economic savings are important. Time saving
matters are too to adopt these technologies. Sometimes, going to a medical appointment and combining
it with work life can be a problem, especially for those patients with diseases requiring of some kind
of medical routine control. In these cases, telemedicine saves time and travel costs and, helps patients
enjoy their medical consultation with no work rhythm alteration.
    Age: Age is one of the strongest drivers underlined in the studies we have worked with. Some patients,
-especially the young ones-, have grown up with technology are more predisposed to receive a medical
consultation online. On the contrary, patients having always attended physical consultation, feel uneasy
and limited when making an online consultation. That is why age is for many authors a driver for the
implementation of health technologies.
    Confidentiality and privacy: Depending on the problem they are attending with their practitioner;
some patients feel more protected and given more privacy when having a consultation at home. In this
way, the articles studied underline that patients feel less judged in situations that are related to mental
health or sexual health issues, among others. In these cases, telemedicine can provide a more discreet
and less intimidating environment.

4.2 Telemedicine Barriers

Despite the large number of telemedicine initiatives and the interest in promoting its uses within soci-
ety, the reality is that its implementation is still scarce. Telemedicine is a health system nourished by
technology and, without it, the service would not be realistic. This is why patients need to have a high
level of digital literacy to make use of digital health tools.
    After the literature review, we established a group of 6 barriers, perceived by society, to be limitative
for the implementation of telemedicine.

-    Access to technology
-    Digital and technological literacy


                                                                                                           141
                                                                                                             ﻿
                                                                  The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




-     Privacy and data security
-     Lack of face-to-face interaction
-     Limitations in diagnosis and treatment
-     Resistance to changes

    Access to technology: It is undoubtedly one of the main reasons for part of the society to be prevented
from using virtual consultations. This barrier refers to the ability of patients and the availability of access
to mobile devices; having the necessary infrastructures to maintain good communication is fundamental.
Patients with no access to these devices are currently facing great challenges since the actual trend is
to provide a more technological health system. This lack of technology is most particularly present in
more rural areas or in marginalized communities. This barrier against technology prevents patients from
benefiting all the advantages offered by telemedicine; it also leads to health disparities and increases
the digital divide in society. As Greenhalght et al. (2020) points out, the lack of access to technology
increases disparities in medical care, because patients not using telemedicine may miss the opportunity
to receive a diagnosis with other doctors, or get a closer follow up on their condition, etc.
    Digital and technological literacy: Not only is it necessary to access technological devices to get
virtual consultations, it is also fundamental to possess an acceptable level of digital literacy to make a
good use of telemedicine. This barrier is especially relevant in the context of telemedicine, -where it
is necessary for patients to make use of technological applications to attend the medical consultation-;
patients need therefore to manage well these technological tools.
    A study published in the Journal of Medical Internet Research (Bokolo, 2019), analyzed the link
between digital literacy and telemedicine implementation. Results showed that there was a significant
relationship between the two concepts. The lack of knowledge on digital technologies and, the lack of
skills to navigate online applications or platforms, can be a major obstacle for patients to engage in
telemedicine. And this situation is most increasingly noticeable for population groups not having been
exposed to these technologies routinely.
    In short, digital literacy is presented as a barrier that patients feel and see as a limitation to interact
with telemedicine.
    Privacy and data security: The privacy of personal data is a major barrier that limits the use of tele-
medicine, not only for patients but also for health professional; both are concerned about the protection
of medical information, especially those specialties too highly sensitive to interact online. A study in the
journal Health Affairs underscored the importance of data security when adopting these technologies.
(Adler-Milstein et al., 2016)
    Lack of face-to-face interaction: Many people value very positively the personal connection and
face-to-face relationship with their personal practitioner. Telemedicine providers face here a strong bar-
rier that prevents users from adopting the use of telemedicine. Such barrier is characterized mostly by
patients’ perception of online medical consultations lacking key elements that are usually present in a
traditional medical consultation: non-verbal communication, face-to-face physical examination or the
feeling of privacy or individualized medical attention.
    A study published by the Journal of Medical Internet Research (Donaghy et al., 2019) revealed the
concerns of patients when performing virtual consultations. They listed the absence of physical contact
in the consultation and the limitation of nonverbal communication -such as gestures and facial expres-
sions-. All this made it impossible to generate a more personal connection with the doctor and, therefore,
the experience of the online consultation was valued negatively.


142
﻿
The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




    Some patients do prefer to perform physical consultations because they feel safer and more com-
fortable in known settings. Going to a medical center provides patients with a sense of security which
reinforces confidence in the given diagnosis and treatment.
    In conclusion, we can affirm that the lack of face-to-face interaction is a barrier that patients face
when making use of telemedicine. Personal connections and certain tangible aspects in the consultation
itself are highly valued by these patients.
    Limitations in diagnosis and treatment: Remote clinical evaluation sometimes mean that the medi-
cal appointment is not as exhaustive or accurate as a face-to-face medical consultation. These limita-
tions affect patients’ adoption of these technologies and also physicians’ restricted ability to accurately
diagnose diseases or provide appropriate treatment. Some of these limitations may be:

-Limited physical examination: The absence of direct contact with the patient can result in a complex
     physical examination. A remote examination may be insufficient depending on the referred condi-
     tion or pathology.
-Diagnostic tests: Some diagnoses require of tests like the x-rays, ultrasounds, etc. The lack of these tests
     in telemedicine can reduce the ability to diagnose diseases.

    Limited medical intervention: There are situations where medical treatment requires of physical
procedures. This is where telemedicine can be a barrier for both the patient and the physician.
    In conclusion, the limitation in diagnosis and treatment is a barrier that imposes and reduces the use of
telemedicine for patient with a necessity for physical examination. Despite these limitations, telemedicine
is very valuable for many chronic diseases and monitoring situations, follow-ups, etc.
    Resistance to changes: This is a barrier that is quite common and noticeable in both patients and
health professionals. The reluctance to adopt new technologies and methods of health care is increased
when the level of digital literacy of users is low. There are different dimensions preventing changes
towards an online consultation:
    -Familiarity and comfort within the traditional consultation: The habit of a traditional consultation
model means safety for some patients; this safety feeling is stronger than that of change. Switching to
telemedicine may require of some kind of mindset process. According to Adler-Milstein et al. (2016)
familiarity with an existing model can influence resistance to adopting telemedicine.

-    Quality: There is a fear among patients about telemedicine not offering the same quality of service
     as a face-to-face consultation. This may be due to a barrier already mentioned above, that of a lack
     of physical contact and communication.
-    Work processes: Health professionals are sometimes reluctant to making changes in their daily
     consultation routine and in work processes.
-    Negative perceptions: Resistance to change can also be influenced by previous negative experi-
     ences. When patients and practitioners have had previous negative experiences, they tend to turn
     to the alternative face-to-face consultation and, prefer to continue their usual consultation routine.




                                                                                                         143
                                                                                                          ﻿
                                                               The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




5. HEALTH MARKETING AND ITS IMPLICATION IN HEALTH LITERACY

Digital transformation in the health sector is a challenge both economically and socially. The results
of the research highlight the many different barriers undermining the use of telemedicine. Bearing in
mind the barriers studied, there is still work to be done to achieve a greater acceptance and literacy of
digital health uses. This is why we invited 4 health marketing professionals to a focus group, for them
to draw proposals and break those digital health barriers felt by patients so as to improve health literacy
within society.
    Some of these action proposals of marketing professionals to be implemented to improve health
literacy are as follows:

1.    Improvement of awareness: It is necessary to carry out some work to improve the education and
      awareness of both health professionals and patients for them to understand the benefits of tele-
      medicine and its effectiveness. The government of every country is to encourage the use of these
      digital tools and help patients acquire the corresponding knowledge. To do so, they propose to
      include the creation of explanatory and informative materials on telemedicine and its benefits -like
      keeping track of personal diseases, - etc. Conducting workshops or educational seminars detailing
      some uses and applications -like preparing more interactive events to help users interact in real
      time and address their concerns or questions-. The use of social media or awareness campaigns
      launched to encourage patients’ exploration of their uses. Real experiences always generate more
      links. Sharing patients’ stories with a positive experience is fundamental -like the explanation of
      how the use of telemedicine has changed their lives-. These can humanize technology and help
      patients feel strong enough to improve their digital health literacy and experiences.

   Awareness and improved education towards a more digitized health system. Transmitting accurate
and reliable information of large medical institutions or of the government itself helps promoting the
acceptance of telemedicine and improves literacy levels of both patients and health professionals.

2.    Generation of trust: According to the barriers studied, we have observed how, for some patients,
      trust and privacy in the practice are very precious variables. This is why we propose the improve-
      ment of trust through marketing: with the generation of messages and materials to address patients’
      concerns. These could also highlight the security measures and regulations involved for online
      consultations to meet the same requirements as physical ones.
3.    Real experiences: Through marketing, health centers can also launch real messages with patients’
      stories, success stories and testimonies. In this way, patients will feel more identified and less
      reluctant to make use of these digital tools.
4.    Improvement of users ‘experiences: The market trend is changing towards a more technological
      society, so we can help the whole society acquire an acceptable level of literacy to improve in the
      use of such tools. We propose to carry out actions with health providers for them to listen and then
      meet the needs of the population to further facilitate their use of these tools. Providing a more
      intuitive and simple use for example, supported by some patient care system in case of doubts.
5.    Because of the previous action, the use of chatbots is proposed. Messaging applications are more
      than consolidated on the market. This is why they consider the use of chatbots as a digital marketing
      strategy in the health sector. In this way, user service is automated when requesting an appointment


144
﻿
The Relevance of Literacy in Digital Health




     or consulting health information for example. Chatbots help simplify the query process, but they
     must be configured beforehand.
6.   Finally, it is important to achieve inclusive digital health by means of actions. Some populations
     cannot make use of health technologies, whichever of the previous actions are taken. A particular
     attention to these populations with situations of greater social, economic, geographical, or cultural
     vulnerability, is fundamental. ICTs have a great potential to reduce inequalities in health, but actions
     must be developed to: enhance the access to information or prevention tools, to access healthcare
     and provide a closer digital inclusion. Actions such as: (1) The provision of some help –with
     health advisors for example- for the most vulnerable patients to understand and practice the use of
     online medical appointments. (2) The collaboration with local organizations, like associations or
     community centers, to provide them with an access to resources and training programs, to further
     improve their digital health literacy. (3) The access to mobile devices in communities with low
     literacy levels, for them to use digital health apps and resources, providing less use concerns.

    These actions are aimed at increasing and improving digital health literacy equitably in all areas.
While working to reinforce these measures to ensure literacy is not a problem anymore, it is important
to favor research on the real impact of digital literacy levels in patient health.
    The perspective from the point of view of marketing professionals opens up an area that can be
worked on to enhance satisfaction and, above all, the adoption of these technologies by society. Market-
ing professionals emphasize that there is still much work to be done, firstly, to thoroughly understand
the needs that will arouse the desire of these consumers and, secondly, to meet their expectations: ease
of use and user experience.
    Telemedicine presents a revolutionary potential to overcome in the future the barriers it faces. By
removing or reducing these barriers while improving digital health literacy, we aim at a society that is
enabled to benefit from the digital health advantages provided.
