                                                        Chapter 9
         A Review of the Colombian
            Healthcare System:
                    Challenges and Opportunities

                                     Gustavo Adolfo Girón-Restrepo
                 Universitat Jaume I, Spain & Antonio José Camacho University, Colombia

                                                    Sandra Tena-Monferrer
                                               https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3153-8271
                                                    Universitat Jaume I, Spain

                                                      Juan Carlos Fandos-Roig
                                                      Universitat Jaume I, Spain


ABSTRACT
This chapter, of a descriptive nature, aims to highlight the challenges that the Colombian healthcare
system is facing. The widespread discontent and protests of society over the last few years have led to
the proposal that the current government of Colombia presented to the Congress of the Republic for its
reform. Bill 339 of 2023 seeks to improve access conditions for the most vulnerable households while
also reducing the administrative barriers causing delays in payments to hospitals and specialist appoint-
ments. However, it should be stated that the healthcare system is a truly broad and complex ecosystem,
so it is not an easy task to assess the outcomes of the potential reforms. Besides, Colombia brings to-
gether highly relevant heterogeneous factors, almost like no other country in Latin America, a complex
geography, interculturality, unemployment, and social inequality, among many others. Consequently,
this work presents and brings together the social, political, economic, and academic perceptions of this
new scenario that arises in the potential change.




DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch009



       Copyright © 2024, IGI Global. Copying or distributing in print or electronic forms without written permission of IGI Global is prohibited.
﻿
A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




1. INTRODUCTION TO THE COLOMBIAN CONTEXT

It is said that Colombia has always had two ideological tendencies, the “Santanderista” and the “Boli-
varista” and this is due to a historical dispute between the liberator Simón Bolívar and the well-known
lawman Francisco De Paula Santander. Bolívar made Santander understand that Colombia had many
laws and few political subjects to enforce them. As the centuries passed, Colombia continued in this
trend of going deeply into the regulations. This has led to great achievements such as having one of the
most advanced constitutionalism in Latin America.
    More than 200 years have passed, and the division of thought continues. According to the historian
Julio Roberto Galindo (Galindo, 2019), it is the Bolivarian tendency that embodies the thought that al-
ludes to personal freedoms and thought and the Santanderista that marks the legacy of order and legality.
Paradoxically, both trends share a privileged place in the National shield with the expression “Freedom
and order” (Galindo, 2019).
    In 1830 Bolívar resigned his mandate (in 1828 he had been the victim of an attempted coup d’état
at the hands of Santander’s followers) and Colombia was divided. On December 17, 1830, Bolívar died
and in 1832 Santander was called from his exile to be the president of New Granada.
    Citing the historian Galindo (Galindo, 2019), all this directly influences the evolution as a nation,
since before the national front, two parties always governed, either the liberal or the conservative. Each
one with its own tendencies, and although today other political parties have already gained ground,
Colombia continues to be framed in the two ways of conceiving the State. However, the art of good
governance in Colombia will be achieved when someone finds a way to successfully combine these two
trends (Galindo, 2019).
    Many decades passed before health gained a place on the national political agenda in Colombia. The
political power decided to include health as part of the government system with the constitution of 1886
(Colombia, 1886). This constitution had very different characteristics from the current one and governed
the country’s destinies for 105 years. As the highest legal framework of the nation, and in particular,
centralist in nature, it served as the foundation for the organization of public health as a function of the
state. Under its protection, Law 30 of 1886 was enacted, creating the Central Hygiene Board, defining
for the first time a state structure in charge of the health problems of the Colombian population. Among
the main functions of the central Hygiene Board were: 1) control of epidemic diseases, 2) environmental
sanitation, and 3) everything related to the control of ports within the guidelines of the International
Sanitary Conventions and the international market pressures. However, this first attempt is too limited
due to the lack of resources, infrastructure, political will, and the reality of a divided country.
    The government and its leaders identified the need to organize and generate solid health policies,
which is why laws began to be enacted that would allow a restructuring of the model. Law 33 of 1913
stands out, which created the Higher Health Council, making it dependent on the Ministry of Govern-
ment and with direct advice from the National Academy of Medicine. The same Law establishes three
more levels in the organization of health: the Departmental Hygiene Boards, the Health Boards, and the
Health Commissions. Next, law 32 of 1918 that converts the previous Board into the National Hygiene
Directorate, dependent on the Ministry of Public Instruction. In 1925 the Ministry changed its name to
Public Instruction and Health, its main function was focused on educational problems and was focused
on “Hygiene and education, and as such it needs to penetrate deeply into the brain of the communities
so that they can understand it.” assimilate”, clearly there were already some notions of the importance
of the environment and conditions to promote healthy environments (Health Promotion).


                                                                                                        151
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                                           A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




    Health functioned in this way until 1945, managing only health aspects, a health system with very
poor scope. Curative-preventive-specialized care was financed by patients or religious institutions of a
charitable nature, for this reason the model was colloquially called “the charity model.”
    In 1945, the national provident fund was created, in charge of serving public sector employees. And,
at the same time, the Social Security Institute was created by Law 90 of 1946, which served employees
in the private sector. It cannot be denied that a giant leap was made in terms of population insurance.
However, they continued to have very little coverage in the most disadvantaged strata; this model lasted
until 1975.
    By decree 056 of 1975, the National Health System was created under the supply subsidy scheme.
The resources were transferred directly to hospitals, health centers and health posts (Public Network).
It had a basic organization for its direction at the national, sectional, and local levels, as defined by
said decree in “Article 37: As of March 15, 1975, to receive aid or contributions that come directly or
indirectly from the nation. or the budget of the Sectional Health Services, any hospital institution or
health organization attached or linked to the National Health System must comply with the standards
established in this Decree and in the other provisions on the National Health System.
    In 1977, by decree 1650, the Colombian Social Insurance Institute was transformed into the Social
Insurance Institute. The workers became officials with the right to collective bargaining. This period
was characterized by the geographical expansion of social security coverage of the private sector in
Colombia, and this is how we ended up having the three health systems that coexisted before Law 100
of 1993. These three systems according to Tafur & Prada (2019), were the German Bismarck model of
insurance for low-income workers, financed based on wages (former Social Insurance Institute). Be-
veridge’s English model, financed by taxes, aimed at the uninsured population (state-financed Public
Network, National Health System) and the private system based on voluntary insurance (private clinics).
    In 1990, Law 10 was issued, within the framework of very notable internal and global changes. In the
90s, the Washington Consensus was created, a list that included economic policies for Latin America
prepared by different international financial organizations. This became the model applied by several
Latin American countries, including Colombia.
    The 20th century was coming to an end and a new world order was reflected where capitalism
reigned, and neoliberalism was the new economic model. This largely led in some Latin countries to the
privatization of state industries, downsizing in the public sector, and deregulation of business; promoted
by the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank. All this with the objective of reducing the
power, the monopoly of the state and stimulating private initiative. This completely changed the way
the markets and the Colombian economy were managed. Health was not immune to this, which is why
after three years of application of the law of January 10, 1990, which reorganized the National Health
System. Exactly on December 23, 1993, the General System of Comprehensive Social Security Law 100
was born, which contains 4 books, in order they are 1) General Pension System, 2) General System of
Social Security in Health, 3) Professional Risks and 4) Complementary Social Services.
    This law sought to expand health coverage through two regimes, one contributory (population with the
ability to pay) and another subsidized (poor insured population), with a scheme to expand the provision
of health services that covers spouses and children underage. age or adults up to 25 years old students
dependent on parents, but even allowing the inclusion of parents and third-degree blood relatives, this
model continues to be the Colombian health model today (Vélez, 2016).
    Contrary to what many believe, Neoliberalism or new liberalism is not a concept or a term or a new
model, it appeared years after the end of the First World War and in summary its primary function is to


152
﻿
A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




limit the power of the state in economic and social matters. legal. Those who defend this theory con-
stantly promote free trade and the deregulation of markets, instilling the idea that private administration
is much more efficient than public administration. This is colloquially called “Privatization”, in this way
state intervention in spending and investment is reduced, affecting (in some cases) public health, public
education and all sectors that the state must guarantee to civil society.
    Law 100 that creates the Colombian Social Security Health System framed in its second chapter, is an
essentially neoliberal system since it allows the creation of Private Companies that serve as administra-
tors among patients (or users of the health system) and their Health Providing Institutions (IPS). These
Private Companies, called Health Promotion Entities (EPS) or Benefit Plan Administration Companies
(EAPB) are the ones who manage public resources, contract with the Service Providers, pay for the
adequate provision of health services to the Providing Institutions. (IPS) and manage the risk of getting
sick, currently health insurance covers 99.6% of the Colombian population (Vélez, 2016).
    Analyzing the main reforms that have taken place in recent years in the Colombian healthcare system,
we find three main modifications: law 1122 of 2007, law 1438 of 2011 and law 1751 of 2015. Through
the first modification of 2007, changes were made to the general social health security system, with the
priority being to improve the provision of services to users. With this objective, reforms were carried out
in the aspects of management, universalization, and financing. Strengthening the balance between the
actors of the system. This law is commonly known as the “financing law”, since it increased the health
tax in Colombia by 0.5% for the contributory regime, going from 12% to 12.5%.
    Later, in 2011, an important change occurred with the inclusion of provisions to establish the unifica-
tion of the Benefit Plan for all residents of the country. The universality of insurance and the guarantee of
portability or provision of benefits anywhere in the country, within a framework of financial sustainability.
Finally, with Law 1751 of February 16, 2015, health became a fundamental right. Health, understood
as health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, recovery, rehabilitation, and palliative
care. All of this became guaranteed, under the responsibility of the State, to all people, without distinc-
tion of any kind. For the first time, a law made it clear that, in matters of health, people have duties to
themselves and to the health system. Promoting the need for each person to take care of themselves by
adopting, for example, healthy habits.


2. CHALLENGES OF THE COLOMBIAN HEALTHCARE SYSTEM

According to Agudelo et al. (2011), the progress of the General Social Security Health System is optimis-
tic, but it has not managed to achieve universality and has stagnated in access to services and equity. We
do not agree with the statement regarding universality, since having such a high percentage of coverage
puts Colombia among the healthcare systems with the best coverage globally. However, accessibility to
health services and equity represent two of the biggest weaknesses of the healthcare system.
    It is evident that substantial improvements have been made to the health system in Colombia and
that Law 100 allowed all citizens, regardless of their social status, to have access to the health system.
However, it is also important to understand that there are very complex structural problems that make
people, especially those who live in deep Colombia, who have been excluded for some social reason or
who do not have the economic means, see how a crusade to be able to have accessibility to health services.
    The commodification of health is the starting point. This concept highlights the scenario in which the
administrators of the EPS system (Health Promotion Entity) do not transfer the resources corresponding


                                                                                                         153
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                                           A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




to the IPS (Health Providing Institutions) for the procedures carried out. Consequently, IPS does not
pay workers and therefore some services stop working, with patients being directly affected. This fact
is a clear contradiction of the Constitution because the economic factor cannot be an excuse, especially
when in Colombia health is a fundamental right reiterated by the Constitutional Court and reaffirmed
with law 1751 of 2015 (Colombia, 2015). Health cannot be conditioned by economic factors and the
state must guarantee it.
    Under this premise, throughout this section the main barriers that users expose when accessing health
system services will be listed.

2.1 Lack of Solvency

According to (Restrepo-Zea et al., 2014), the lack of solvency among some users, despite being insured,
is why many times, to access services or medicines, additional payments or indirect costs must be made,
especially in medicines or so-called high-cost diseases. Consequently, this lack of ability to pay delays
care because covering the costs of carrying out diagnostic tests, travel or treatment becomes impossible.
In this scenario there is only one way out, to assert your right through legal means, most times by filing
protection actions.

2.2 Geographical Barriers

Geographic barriers depend on multiple factors, they can be urban or rural and are directly affected by the
fragmentation of the network that the EPS Health Promotion Entity has (Restrepo-Zea et al., 2014). This
situation forces users to make trips, in some cases excessive, both within the city and in rural areas. And
in extreme cases, between municipalities. Without considering such important factors as great distances,
the transportation difficulties presented by cities, towns, municipalities, the patient’s health conditions
and the lack of economic resources. Here a gigantic structural problem of the system becomes vitally
important and that is that the best human and technological resources are found in the large capitals,
this makes the problem of access to health in the country’s villages and towns extremely difficult and
much more difficult. when the illness suffered by the patient is terminal or wrongly called high cost.
    Latin America is one of the most inequitable regions in the world, where Colombia holds second
place only surpassed by Brazil. The member countries of the Organization for Economic Cooperation
and Development -OECD- gave Colombia a Gini coefficient (coefficient that measures the inequality
of countries) of 0.53 in 2019 before the pandemic began. Post-pandemic impact has further increased
inequality, pushing the Gini coefficient up to 0.54 in 2020 and dragging around 3.6 million more people
into poverty.
    It is important to understand that these inequalities have a lot to do with the existence of 11 million
Colombians who live in rural areas according to the National Department of Statistics -DANE- (DANE,
2022a). This becomes a major problem when it is observed that the Health system’s delivery chain is
developed fundamentally in urban centers concentrated in the 5 large cities of the country (Bogotá,
Medellín, Cali, Barranquilla and Cartagena). However, in small municipalities, in departments such as
Amazonas, Vaupés or the Caribbean, there is not the same infrastructure. Not to mention that in large
economically developed urban centers, it can be mixed with the business of complementary plans and
prepaid medicines.




154
﻿
A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




    The most socioeconomically vulnerable populations have less accessibility to health services and
were the ones who had the highest deaths from coronavirus, as shown by the Economic Commission
for Latin America and the Caribbean -ECLAC- in its study Mortality from COVID-19 and inequalities
by level socioeconomic and by territory (Cruz & Henrique, 2021). In Colombia, the -DANE- figures
regarding monetary poverty are 39.3% (DANE, 2022b), an eloquent figure, without mentioning that
22.6% of Colombians could only have two meals a day (Portafolio, 2022).
    In a biodiverse country, with enough fertile lands to achieve food sovereignty, more than 10 million
people are tolerating what should not be tolerated. These inequities are reproduced by the health system,
the best services are available in large cities., the same as the Intensive Care Units -ICU- and highly
complex services.

2.3 Labor Barriers

In Colombia, the underground economy is an harm that is increasing; in March 2023, the proportion
of underground employment was 58.2%, according to data from the National Department of Statistics
(DANE, 2023). The lack of legal employment in one form or another has become a barrier to accessing
services for several citizens. Although the subsidized regime meets this need, the other side of the coin
is that person who has a stable job, is diagnosed with a serious illness and after a while ends up quitting
their job to avoid difficulties with their employers and colleagues due to the number issues.

2.4 Administrative Barriers

These arise from the insurer, the provider, the contract they have and the service plan. These include
the insufficient hiring of personnel, suppliers, providers, the procedures to access the service and the
cancellation of contracts with providing institutions without immediately meeting the need. In the latter
case, it is the patients who are severely affected, as they do not receive treatments due to lack of contract
with an IPS, a clinical laboratory, a diagnostic aid center or a medication supplier (Restrepo-Zea et al.,
2014). The system should prohibit the transfer of administrative problems between system actors to
patients or users.
    Another element that must be studied is the job insecurity of the health sector; many health workers
do not know what social benefits are. 71.3% of health workers are outsourced under “precarious” forms
of contracting according to information provided by the Ministry of Health and the National Attorney’s
Office collected by the media outlet el tiempo in 2021 (El Tiempo, 2021). Outsourced contracts, indirect
links, by union, by event and even verbal agreements are what abounds in the sector. The largest staff
in the health sector is nursing. Fundamentally, they are women, with low incomes, hired to provide
services ranging from $1,500,000 to $3,000,000 depending on whether it is a professional or technical
level, this according to the talent.com portal (it should be noted that social benefits are not received for
the provision of services).

2.5 Benefit Plan Administration Companies (EAPB)
or Health Promotion Entities (EPS)

In Colombia, the issue of EPS has been very difficult and highly controversial. These private companies
are the ones who manage public resources, contract with service providers, pay for the adequate provision


                                                                                                         155
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                                            A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




of health services to Health Providing Institutions (IPS) and manage the risk of getting sick. However,
there are multiple complaints of denial-of-service provision, authorization of third and fourth level pro-
cedures, and delivery of high-cost medications. For this reason, an approach that has been carried out
recurrently is the definitive establishment of the direct line. Let the state be the one to directly transfer
the health money to the different IPS of the country, as the resources and health money (which are public
resources that come from taxes and contributions) are required in the pockets of the workers. They are
the human capital that supports health. And in the IPS, which are the centers that will generate user at-
tention, however, much of this income ends up in the accounts of the administrators (EPS-EAPB). The
amounts of money accumulated by the Benefit Plan Administration Companies -EAPB-, previously called
Health Promotion Companies -EPS- were quite large during the Covid-19 pandemic. The EPS obtained
an indisputable profitability. For example, the well-known “New EPS” generated income in 2020 of 9.9
billion pesos with an increase of 25.7% compared to 2019, Sura generated income of 5.9 billion with an
increase of 26.2% compared to 2019, Sanitas generated income of 4 billion and Total Health generated
income of 3.9 billion (Portfolio, 2021). While some hospitals and clinics lost economic resources with
the closure of some services and outpatient care, for this reason the question arises regarding the need
for these Health System Administrators.
    Multiple proposals have been put forward to try to solve this problem, among them, 1) leave only
the EPS that are working well and have fewer complaints from users, liquidate the others, 2) leave a
single EPS that manages all public resources, 3) that it is the territorial entities (state organizations of
the municipal or departmental order) who manage public money, taking away the management of the
money from the EPS, 4) their definitive disappearance has even been proposed.
    It is worth understanding that in a world where economic globalization is expanding and becoming
more tangible every day, and where the prevailing economic system is capitalist, a balance between the
humanistic and the monetary factor is inevitably needed to function better. It is not possible to provide
high quality health if the workers in the sector do not have a high humanization component, are well
remunerated financially, have contracts that provide them with job stability but at the same time the Sys-
tem is economically balanced and the public health money It is reaching where it needs to reach, to the
workers in the sector and to the Institutions that provide Health services. Thus, users will most likely be
able to receive the care they seek within their diagnostic particularities. However, today we have a health
system with a fiscal gap of 10 billion and there are no real solutions in this regard (La República, 2019).
    As a personal approach, the solution cannot be to hand over the total management of health resources
to the Colombian state; our political history has led us in the last 5 decades to reveal decadent scandals
regarding corruption, clientelism, bureaucracy and power ambitions. generated by officials and public
servants elected by popular vote, appointed in administrative careers, or chosen to hold positions of free
appointment and removal, for this reason, this proposal generates great concern for us.
    Clearly there are some problems of form and substance that still need to be reviewed and corrected.
The administration and management of health organizations has been demanding various challenges to
guarantee the fundamental right to health and adaptation in the Colombian regulatory context. Situations
such as the closure of several EPS or EAPB (benefit plan management companies) and the transfer of
said users to other EPS or EAPB, due, among many other causes, to the financing or loss of economic
resources, generate a quite complex panorama. for system administration. However, it is paradoxical
that in the latest survey published by the Colombian Ministry of Health and Social Protection, 64% of
the people surveyed rate the service provided by the EPS in Colombia as good (Colombian Association




156
﻿
A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




of Integral Medicine Companies, 2022). For this reason, it is important to carry out a detailed study on
the situations that currently raise alerts in the Colombian health sector.
    On the other hand, the achievements that the Colombian Health system has had throughout these 30
years of operation cannot be denied. We can highlight that the country has one of the lowest out-of-pocket
health expenses in Latin America, it is the only one below 4% according to (ConsultorSalud, 2019).
This information is very relevant, since household finances are not affected by possible health problems
that any of its members may have. Likewise (ConsultorSalud, 2019) reports that per capita spending on
health is the lowest in the entire area, since it is only US$358; while in the rest of the countries in the
region it exceeds US$1,000, such as Chile (US$1,244), Brazil (US$1,114), Argentina (US$1,071); and
according to the study, even in Mexico (US$505) and Venezuela (US$384) more is spent on health than
in Colombia (ConsultorSalud, 2019).
    Another achievement to highlight is access to health, which is understood in the country as coverage
or insurance, according to the Ministry of Health and Social Protection, by June 2022 coverage was
almost universal, 99.6% of the The Colombian population has EPS insurance, which leads us to almost
universal insurance, with a distribution of 24,399,839 people in the Contributory Regime and 24,745,934
in the Subsidized Regime (Minsalud, 2022). This is an achievement because before Law 100, social
security coverage was only 23%, while 13% attended private clinics, 40% in the official sector, and 25%
of the total population was unprotected (Vélez, 2016). However, it is important to clarify that before Law
100, any Colombian could attend public hospitals regardless of their ability to pay and without being
restricted from using x or y healthcare center or hospital (Vélez, 2016).
    According to (OPS, 2022), Colombia has improved in almost all public health indicators. For example,
between 2000 and 2020, infant mortality decreased from 25.8 to 16.8 per 1,000 live births, which implies
a decrease of 34.9%. The percentage of low-birth-weight births (less than 2500 g) increased from 7.6%
to 9.5% between 2002 and 2019, while adherence to exclusive breastfeeding in the infant population up
to 6 months represented 25.8% in 2000 and 46.8% in 2005. (OPS, 2022)
    Regarding the immunization strategy, in 2019 measles vaccination coverage was 95%, which repre-
sents an increase of seven percentage points compared to coverage in 2000 (OPS, 2022).
    The maternal mortality ratio for 2017 was estimated at 83 per 100,000 live births, which represents a
reduction of 11.7% compared to the value estimated for 2000. In relation to fertility, it is estimated that,
in 2017, Women had an average of 1.8 children throughout their reproductive lives. In the specific case
of adolescent fertility, a reduction of 36.1% is observed, if the rate of 98.8 live births per 1,000 women
aged 15 to 19 in 2000 is compared with the figure of 63.1 in 2021. Regarding prenatal care, between
2000 and 2019 there is an increase in the percentage of pregnant people who attended four or more
prenatal care consultations during pregnancy, since this went from 72.6% to 83 .9%. On the other hand,
98.5% of births were attended by trained personnel in 2019 (OPS, 2022).
    In 2019, according to (PAHO, 2022), the adjusted rate of potentially avoidable premature mortality in
Colombia was 183.7 deaths per 100,000 inhabitants, which is equivalent to a decrease of 46.1% compared
to the rate of 340. 9 registered in 2000. In this sense, the country presented a rate in 2019 that was 19%
lower than that reported for the Region of the Americas. Of potentially avoidable premature deaths, the
rate due to preventable causes in 2019 was 113.2 per 100,000 inhabitants, a figure 17.4% lower than
the regional rate, while the rate related to treatable causes rose to 70.5 per 100,000 inhabitants. 100,000
inhabitants, compared to a regional average of 89.6 (OPS, 2022).




                                                                                                        157
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                                            A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




    Regarding the general mortality rate adjusted by age, according to (OPS, 2022), in 2019 it was 4.4
per 1,000 inhabitants, which implies a decrease of 31.2% compared to 2000 (6.4 deaths per 1000 inhab-
itants) (PAHO, 2022).
    For this reason, public health indicators confirm efforts that are reflected in a decrease in infant
mortality, low birth weight, breastfeeding, immunization, maternal mortality, adolescent fertility, and
potentially avoidable premature deaths, among others, clearly not You can ignore an effort that shows
tangible results, although the system has flaws (mentioned above), ignoring its progress would not be
responsible.
    Added to this whole panorama is the global crisis that occurred with the SARS-Cov-2 pandemic,
stimulating an intense debate between academia, social sectors, political actors, and professionals linked
to the health sector. The health problem has been worsening due to the fiscal gap that has been growing
year after year for several decades and amounts to more than 10 billion pesos (La República, 2019) and
with the social outbreak of April 2022, health It became part of the National political agenda due to the
file of reform 010 that was being processed in the Congress of the Republic, but when the president
changed in 2022, the new administration presented a bill to carry out a structural reform to the Health
System. Colombian, for this reason the big question arises: What are the challenges and trends facing
the Colombian Health system?


3. IMPROVEMENT AREAS IN THE COLOMBIAN HEALTHCARE SYSTEM

Clearly, the system must improve, or must be reformed, preserving and enhancing the achievements that
have been achieved in these 30 years, but some fundamental structural factors persist that must be solved.
Firstly, the current strong economic deficit, no government has addressed this problem in a profound
way, momentary solutions have been given, but until now the fiscal gap persists, and the liquidation of
EPS is a constant. Secondly, the barriers to accessibility to health services are continuous, especially
in populations that are far from large cities, which we call deep Colombia. And, thirdly, information
systems. Technology must be seen as a means, not as an end; databases must have adequate informa-
tion, seeking an information system that is integrable and interoperable. It must be seen as an issue of
digital transformation; an adequate technological system must be worked on through Internet access
and coverage. It is about bringing the internet to citizens, not just to the home, so that organizations can
use support networks with complete data, providing information on how the population group is doing
in terms of health. Throughout the following subsections, four important components in the possible
reform of the health system are presented.

3.1 Public Health

The reform should contemplate the element of public health in more depth, delve into and articulate with
the ten-year public health plan, understanding the importance of these components in a robust health
system that is in line with international guidelines.
    Any reform must have health as a fundamental right as a precept, health authorities should evaluate
health indicators, develop public policies to strengthen health systems and have social determinants
as a fundamental principle, work on them and attack them, this inevitably leads us to strengthening of
intersectoral articulation.


158
﻿
A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




    There are various formulas to work on this aspect. From the provision of the service (individual
actions), actions with the health authority (collective interventions) (public health policies), and in the
superstructure (at the national level), thus more impact and results are generated. The individual approach
gives results in quality if real access is achieved, not just potential access in large urban centers, but it is
necessary to clearly articulate it with the collective intervention plan that already exists. We have been
working on this for a long time, but it must be improved because the real articulation with the different
actors is not potentially being seen in the community, more so in the communities that are far from the
large urban centers.
    Articulating and connecting all this is the central focus, health systems must be based on primary
care. Primary care is not a minor issue, it must be seen as a main issue, for this purpose, even the acad-
emy becomes a main actor to instill this change in thinking. Universal access and coverage is proposed,
something that Colombia is well advanced in. Resilient health systems, understanding the importance
of strengthening the capacity of the first levels of complexity.
    The structures of the system must be rethought to find the point of synergy between functions, re-
sources, and forms, understanding that the essential function is the formulation of public policies, but
the fragmentation between individual and collective provision continues to be a complex problem.

3.2 Primary Healthcare Strategy: APS

By analyzing the strategy, we seek to understand that APS is an approach to health that includes the
entire society and that aims to guarantee the greatest health based on the needs of the people, as close
as possible. to the people.
    The reform says that the APS will be developed by the state through the public and state IPS -ISES- for
this reason responsibility is being taken away from the territorial entity. There is a dissociation between
public health and institutions providing health services; it is important to understand that territorial
entities must be strengthened and not take away their functions, considering the differential approach.
    The provision of services is differential, we need to clearly identify the rural population from the
urban population. It is necessary to collect manual and technical information, the manual collection of
information cannot be left aside, it is important to register people to have current and relevant information.
    The PHC is carried out in the places of greatest risk (assemblies, jobs, schools, sports centers), where
the individuals are, this is different with people in rural areas, a differential approach.
    Community participation is of vital importance even for the delivery of medicines in rural areas and
their different conglomerates.

3.3 Comprehensive and Integrated Networks

Networks do not work in the best way and are claimed as a necessity. All levels complain about integra-
tion and feedback. Comprehensive RIIS networks, understood as the set of organizations that provide
coordinated and efficient health services with family and community orientation, should present clinical
results by health status to the population they serve.
    First situation, integrated networks and comprehensive networks are two different things, the differ-
ences come from the WHO, law 1438 and 1441 of 2016.
    Great concentration of networks in urban areas and less concentration in rural areas, with a large
presence of the private sector in urban areas, but very little presence in rural areas.


                                                                                                            159
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                                            A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




    They are necessary for the organization of networks:

•     Technical elements: the determining criteria of the networks that have to do with assistance, gov-
      ernance, risk management, knowledge management.
•     Assistance: Risk management, knowledge management
•     Organization and management: trust through agreements between all those involved.
•     Assignment of incentives: For network members who meet global goals.
•     In the proposal for Comprehensive Resolving Care Centers -Capris-: leave the formation of extra-
      mural teams and transportation options well established.
•     Technical and operational regulation of telemedicine as a great help within the sector.
•     Information technologies: single public health information system.

   Resolving capacity of the sectors, relocating personnel, continuous training strategies, according to
the model and the needs of the population.

3.4 Information Systems

Unique comprehensive online health information system, two fundamental questions: why is it needed?
And do you have the conditions and infrastructure for a great information system? Technology should be
seen as a means, not an end. Databases must have adequate information, seeking an information system
that is integrable and interoperable. Working as a digital transformation issue, an adequate technologi-
cal system must be worked on through Internet access and coverage. It is about bringing the internet
to citizens, not just to the home, so that organizations can use support networks with complete data,
providing information on how the population group is doing in terms of health.
    Second, the governance of the data by analyzing it, managing it and allowing its quick and acces-
sible consultation, always thinking about the data model that must be generated. In terms of health to
have the articulation, after this you can see the information system with two conditions: integrable and
interoperable, in which the system is going to be developed, what are the technological environments,
guidelines must be given from the technological part, digital government, digital transformation, smart
cities, are the necessary policies to have adequate technological development.
    Privacy cannot be left aside, especially if we talk about patients in the health system, a digital trans-
formation must be carried out, not an information system.
    Data sources must be exact, complete, and coherent, the quality of the data is very important for
their respective analysis.
    Technology is a means, but the primary asset is built through accurate and clear data, the source,
quality, and security must be harmonized, digital security has only recently been guaranteed through
public policy in Colombia. The data model is important to have a clear and functional information system.
    The concept of public health has always been a broad and structured concept. However, those who
are not technicians or experts in this field are unable to demonstrate the limits of Public Health and the
need for it within a health model that meets the expectations of a multicultural and pluriform society.
    For Héctor Abad Gómez (Abad, 2012), public health, understood as it has traditionally been done, a
discipline that prevents and treats collective diseases (biological or traumatic fundamentally), expands
towards a much more social, more comprehensive vision or discipline., we call this polyatry (from polis
= city-state and iatry = study, treatment, cure). What makes public health a totally new profession, not


160
﻿
A Review of the Colombian Healthcare System




only multidisciplinary but transdisciplinary, seeking to harmonize the other disciplines or professions
that have emerged until now in the course of the history of human culture: medicine, public health,
epidemiology, anthropology, sociology, social psychology, economics, politics (Abad, 2012). For this
reason, the public health worker tends to be a political actor that seeks the vindication of the most dis-
advantaged subjects in society.
   We cannot see social security and public health solely as health promotion, health maintenance, care
and rehabilitation, because there are multiple circumstances that directly influence the appearance of
the disease. These range from decent housing, adequate food (food sovereignty), quality education and
stable employment to, even, the political situation of the country, for this reason it is very important to
understand that the coordination between the actors will allow clearer positive effects in society.


4. CONCLUSION

Clearly, there are many points that must be evaluated to understand the complex scenario of a healthcare
systema like the Colombian one. Colombia brings together highly relevant heterogeneous factors, almost
like no other country in Latin America. A complex geography, infrastructure that must be renewed or
updated in several remote regions. But not only health infrastructure, secondary and tertiary roads rep-
resent a major mobility problem for the inhabitants of the areas that are popularly identified as “deep
Colombia.” Also, interculturality, the specific particularities of some populations, unemployment, and
the consequent social inequalities are some of the most important challenges to be faced.
    For these reasons, a government that is committed to improving the healthcare system must be in
total synergy with a change that entails a deep understanding of the theoretical model of the social
determinants of health. But at the same time, it should guarantee the economic resources to achieve
this systematic and gradual change of all these components that affect the health-disease process of the
residents. If we do not have the economic resource guaranteed, the purpose of change will be registered
as a profound social discourse that did not materialize in the reality of the Colombian who lives in a
difficult environment for whom the minimum for a dignified life is not guaranteed.
    The current general social security healthcare system has had important achievements that should not
be omitted. We cannot retrocede to the old subsidy scheme nor to the health supply without any control. At
the same time, we should not take away functions from territorial organizations and a mechanism should
be sought so that current administrators improve the provision of services and administrative barriers
are reduced. The current system has very important achievements, ignoring them does not contribute to
reflection. The union of institutional and personal strengths with the opportunities that the outside offers
us is key to creating a strategic roadmap. Colombia must have well identified the strengths of the current
healthcare system, but also understand the opportunities that could arise from a reform of the system.
All that remains is to relate the internal and external strengths, so the country can grow and provide a
better quality of life to citizens. This is why we consider it very important to support and enhance the
achievements so that the healthcare reform that Colombia requires can be built.
