                                                     Chapter 10
         Cross-Sector Partnerships
        and Relationship Marketing
              in Healthcare:
           The Example of Fairy Tale Therapy
            in Children’s Hospitals in Poland

                                                        Agnieszka Żak
                                             https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1602-1757
                                           Cracow University of Economics, Poland

                                                        Kakhaber Djakeli
                                                   University of Georgia, Georgia

                                                  Edyta Bielińska-Dusza
                                           Cracow University of Economics, Poland


ABSTRACT
The aim of this chapter is to assess the potential of fairy tale therapy as a comprehensive relationship
marketing tool in the context of cross-sectoral partnerships. The chapter enriches the existing literature
on the increasing importance of cross-sectoral collaboration in addressing important social problems,
with an emphasis on the use of fairy tale therapy. It also contributes to the debate on the role of fairy tale
therapy in building relationships in the health sector. The chapter characterises the issues of relationship
marketing, cross-sector partnerships, and one method of bibliotherapy such as fairy tale therapy. Among
the factors influencing intersectoral partnerships, those that foster them and those that hinder collabora-
tion will be identified and analysed. The publication uses the case study method as a research method.
The chapter will present and analyse a cross-sector partnership between hospitals, businesses, and an
NGO that created a fairy-tale therapy programme in children’s wards of selected hospitals in Poland.




DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch010



       Copyright © 2024, IGI Global. Copying or distributing in print or electronic forms without written permission of IGI Global is prohibited.
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




The assumption that a company’s true capital is its good relationships with its environment is the basis
of collaboration based on relationship marketing (Morgan & Hunt, 1994). Today, in healthcare market-
ing, relationships take on a new and special significance. They are the foundation for trust, loyalty and
success in the long term (Yildirim, Amarat & Akbolat, 2022). Relationships put the patient at the centre
and enable the comprehensive satisfaction of their needs, which is crucial in the context of health and
medical care (Molina-Mula & Gallo-Estrada, 2020). Relationships in healthcare today are becoming
more interactive, personalised, trust-based (Sutherland et al. 2022), and require adaptation to a changing
environment (Menatti, Bich & Saborido, 2022). Social and digital marketing offers significant oppor-
tunities for organizations through lower costs, better brand awareness and increased sales (Dwivedi et
al., 2021). Adapting marketing strategies to the demands of the digital world can help build lasting and
rewarding relationships with patients (Chauhan, 2015). Strong relationships with healthcare providers
enable better coordination of care and continuity of treatment at different stages. With digital data and
analytics tools, communications and offerings can be tailored to individual patient needs and preferences.
Personalisation, in turn, allows stronger relationships to be built as patients feel understood and valued.
Thus, digital technology provides new tools and communication channels (Pascucci, Savelli & Gistri,
2023), but it is the relationship that remains the key element in building lasting and valuable connections
with patients (Molina-Mula & Gallo-Estrada, 2020).
    Relationship building is probably the most demanding and time-consuming challenge in the process
of developing partnerships (Lasker et al. 2001). It remains crucial, however, because the essence of
successful collaboration is the quality of the personal and professional relationships between the people
involved (Kerka, 1997). The best collaboration requires extensive connections between many people
at many levels and is possible when organizations develop structures, processes and skills to overcome
organizational and interpersonal differences.
    Relationship marketing in healthcare focuses on meeting patient needs, wants and desires by listening,
responding, providing and adapting (Dwivedi et al., 2021). Adopting a relationship marketing approach
in healthcare institutions and assessing the importance of its dimensions will benefit the effectiveness
of marketing strategies (Ersoy & Tehci, 2023). Fairy tale therapy implemented in children’s hospitals
can be considered as an example of modern healthcare marketing. Fairy tale therapy can play an impor-
tant role during therapy and recovery, especially among children who are in hospitals for long periods
of time and away from home. Cross-sector partnerships and relationship marketing can also play an
important role in the promotion and implementation of innovative therapeutic approaches. Children’s
hospitals have the opportunity to establish partnerships with community organizations, foundations or
other entities interested in supporting children in their recovery. These types of partnerships can provide
access to funding, expertise, volunteers or other resources needed to deliver story therapy. They can
also help to raise the profile and visibility of the fairy tale therapy program through joint campaigns and
promotional activities. However, it is worth noting that the implementation of projects in cross-sector
partnerships can be strengthened or weakened by a number of factors (Zhu et al., 2019), as described
in relation to the case study.
    In the available literature, there is a lack of perception of fairy tale therapy delivered through cross-
sector partnerships as a multi-faceted healthcare relationship marketing instrument. This innovative
approach to healthcare can differentiate a hospital and attract the attention of patients and their families,
while building the image of the hospital as a place where innovative methods are used for the benefit of
patients. This chapter aims to fill this gap.




                                                                                                         165
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                     Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




    The chapter is structured as follows. The first section presents a general overview of the literature
related to relationship marketing, cross-sector partnerships and fairy tale therapy as a method to support
the healing process. Then, in order to justify our study, it is explained why fairy tale therapy can be seen
as an example of modern healthcare marketing. This section is followed by a description of the research
design, starting with a presentation of the research method and then the means of data analysis. The
description of the research project includes the characteristics of the partners involved in the collabora-
tion of the fairy tale therapy program implemented since 2017, including the results and impact of the
partnership from the beneficiaries’ perspective. The second part of the chapter identifies and analyses
the factors affecting cross-sector partnerships, both facilitating and hindering collaboration. Finally, the
implications and limitations of the research conducted are discussed.


LITERATURE REVIEW

Relationship Marketing

To understand contemporary marketing, it is very important to study business exchange relationships
from a relational and sustainable perspective (Möller & Halinen 2000). Many authors recognise that the
new paradigm of marketing is relationship marketing (see Sheth & Parvatiyar, 1995; Möller & Halinen
2000; Veloutsou, Saren & Tzokas, 2002; Grönroos, 2000), which is considered to be at the forefront of
marketing practices for services (Astuti & Nagase, 2014), due to the close interactions with customers
(Sin et al., 2006).
    Relationship marketing (RM) is a concept of market action whose effectiveness depends on establish-
ing bonds with market participants. It is a dialogue-based process aimed at building mutually beneficial
long-term relationships between a company and its customers and other stakeholder groups (Astuti &
Nagase, 2014), in such a way as to achieve the objectives of the partners involved (Grönroos, 1994). RM
definitions primarily emphasise the importance of building as well as consolidating and strengthening
mutually positive and mutually beneficial partnerships. The development of long-term relationships is
made possible by creating an atmosphere of mutual trust, which is built primarily through communica-
tion processes and sustained cooperation between partners (Grönroos, 2000; Morgan & Hunt, 1994).
Essentially, therefore, relationship marketing is based on relationships, networks and interactions, focused
on ‘win-win’ relationships with individual customers and where value is created together (Gummesson,
2002). The type of relationship depends on the object of the relationship and the actors who form it. In
the literature, the concept of RM has been described by six components: trust, bonding, communication,
shared value, empathy, and reciprocity (see Ersoy & Tehci, 2023; Sin et al., 2002).
    The originator of the relationship marketing concept is considered to be Berry (1995), who defined
relationship marketing as creating, maintaining and enriching a relationship with a customer. A company
can build consumer trust in three ways: through the communication process, through the guarantee of its
services and by ensuring the highest possible standards of its actions (Berry, 1995). The main features of
relationship marketing include an emphasis on the quality of customer service and a focus on customer
retention, a long-term scale of action, constant communication and monitoring of the situation with
the customer, creating new value for the customer or helping to create partnerships both internally and
externally – with suppliers, intermediaries, to create the value expected by the customer (Gordon, 1998).




166
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




    The premise of the RM concept is to build relationships based on loyalty (Gilaninia et al., 2011).
Underlying this perspective is the recognition that customer retention is as important as customer ac-
quisition (Berry 2002); consequently, particularly in service industries, this has led to the original 4-P
marketing mix approach being extended to include people, processes and physical evidence (Booms and
Bitner 1981). Yildirim and team (2022) argue that a number of factors can be identified that are effec-
tive in ensuring patient loyalty. These include, for example, healthcare institutions taking into account
patient complaints, service quality and patient satisfaction, corporate image, hospital reputation, trust
and doctors’ reputation (Yildirim et al, 2022).
    Relationship marketing has a significant potential in the context of public healthcare, based on its
advocacy of building relationships between providers and customers and between providers and their
suppliers. It is important to transform public health care from a primarily provider- and product-based
service to a truly relationship-oriented service (Wright & Taylor, 2005). Building relationships with pa-
tients is critical to the success of many healthcare organizations (Wagner et al., 1994). As a determinant
of relationship quality, trust is fundamental to patient satisfaction (Han & Hyun, 2015). In turn, effective
communication is crucial for empathy in care and understanding of the patient in relation to healthcare
quality (Ersoy & Tehci, 2023). Relationship marketing practices should contribute to patients leaving
the healthcare facility satisfied. It is believed that feeling special and valuable, in addition to social ben-
efits and trust, will positively influence patient loyalty (Yildirim, Amarat & Akbolat, 2022). Ensuring
patient satisfaction can encourage patients to continue to use the same institution. For these reasons,
for shaping, enhancing and maintaining patient loyalty, understanding relationship marketing – which
aims to earn trust, ensure loyalty and communicate with customers in a timely, reliable and proactive
manner – occupies an important place in the service sector (Taleghani et al., 2011).

Cross-Culture Partnerships

Relationship marketing is well suited to establishing and maintaining successful, long-term relational
exchanges (Barroso-Méndez et al., 2014). This, in turn, translates into the success of cross-sector part-
nerships, which are a key tool in building and maintaining long-term, valuable relationships between
organizations operating in different sectors (Bryson, Crosby & Stone, 2006). Companies are increas-
ingly collaborating across the boundaries of their sectors, and these collaborations are taking place in a
variety of configurations. This results in a situation where it is particularly important to properly match
partners and take care of the entire process of cooperation implementation, which should result in a long
and effective partnership (Bielinska - Dusza, 2021).
   Cross-sector partnerships (CSPs) are relatively intensive, voluntary and long-term interactions be-
tween organizations from two or more sectors (business, government and/or civil society) to address
social or environmental problems (Clark & Crane, 2018; Bryson & Crosby, 2015), such as healthcare,
economic development or environmental sustainability (Waddock, 1991). These relationships can vary
widely in scope and length, as well as in the number of partners (Austin & Seitanidi, 2012; Selsky &
Parker, 2005). Cross-sector alliances can often have three or more partners, due to the need for diversity
of goals, business strategies and expertise (Arya & Salk, 2006).
   A key feature of cross-sectoral partnerships is the diverse nature of the partners and the focus mainly
on social rather than business issues. Partners’ objectives and approaches to solving a social problem
often vary due to being influenced by distinctly different stakeholder groups (Selsky & Parker, 2005).




                                                                                                           167
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                     Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




The differences relate to the nature and scope of the problem they are expected to contribute to and the
means by which they do so.
    The main benefits for partners can be considered in multiple contexts (see Lakin & Scheubel 2010;
Parmigiani & Rivera-Santos, 2011). Companies may be motivated to improve their image or legitimacy
or attract better employees, not-for-profit organizations may seek to influence social change while im-
proving their own status, and government agencies may respond to increasing demands for efficiency
and accountability. These varying motivations often make this form of partnership difficult to create and
manage. Power imbalances can also make this form of relationship challenging (Parmigiani & Rivera-
Santos, 2011). Despite this, cross-sector partnerships may become the preferred form of collaboration
in the 21st century (Vestergaard et al., 2021). Their prevalence and practice is even expected to increase
exponentially (Porter & Kramer, 2011).
    In addition to the benefits achieved individually by the partners involved, it is worth highlighting
the benefits achieved by two specific groups of beneficiaries. These are the benefits for employees and
project partners (e.g. society). This aspect is highlighted by Lakin and Scheubel (2010), according to
whom employee involvement is an integral part of corporate social engagement strategies. The personal
involvement of employees in the communities where they work and live makes being active in com-
munity life a reality. When informal networks receive formal support from management, participation
reaches a significant intensity. Employee engagement can be seen as a ‘triple win’– for employees,
communities and the company. Communities receive valuable support, as well as access to a range of
skills and resources from the private sector. Employees gain personal satisfaction from contributing to
the community, experience themselves and others in different roles, and gain new skills and experience.
Such activities also motivate employees in their daily work and enhance the image and reputation of the
company (Lakin and Scheubel, 2010).
    There is a need in the literature for partnerships to promote health and wellbeing (Towe et al. 2016).
As Noh and co-authors (2022) believe, today’s changing healthcare environment requires hospitals to
address population health issues and commit to working with their communities. Population health at the
community level depends on a wide range of determinants, such as clinical, genetic, behavioural, social
and environmental factors (Noh et al., 2022). To address the broad spectrum of determinants beyond
clinical factors and achieve the goal of improving population health, hospitals should collaborate with
various community sectors and stakeholders, rather than taking sole responsibility for population health
(Mac McCullough et al., 2019).
    Bringing different sectors and organizations together in healthcare has many benefits (Buse & Walt,
2000). For example, it influences the creation of lasting and valuable relationships with patients, their
families and the community. Collaboration between different sectors, such as hospitals, charities, busi-
nesses or public institutions, also enables access to different resources, knowledge and experiences (Cankar
& Petkovsek, 2013). This is extremely important in developing and promoting innovative approaches
to care for, for example, sick children. These partnerships can help to obtain funding, human resources
and community support, which is crucial for the success of such projects.
    Participation in cross-sector partnerships can therefore be an important element of hospitals’ offer-
ings because, by working with other organizations, it enables them to expand their services and deliver
comprehensive care to patients. This care can include not only the medical aspect, but also social support,
education and other services related to health and well-being (Towe et al., 2016). This builds patient
loyalty and influences their perception of the hospital as a provider of valuable services. The hospital
can promote the partners in its marketing communications, and the partners can recommend the hospital


168
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




to their customers and community members. In this way, both parties can support each other and build a
positive image. Cross-sector partnerships also allow the hospital to reach new target groups (Vestergaard
et al., 2021). By collaborating with other organizations, a hospital can reach new customers, patients and
communities that may be interested in its services. This in turn opens up new market opportunities and
potential sources of growth. Cross-sector partnerships can also help to strengthen community engage-
ment around the hospital (Maiolini et al., 2022). Joint projects, community events or charitable initiatives
organised in collaboration with partners attract the attention and involvement of the local community.
This builds social connectedness and a positive image of the hospital as an institution committed to the
well-being of the local community (Cronin et al., 2022).

Fairy Tale Therapy

Although health-related sectors (such as health care and public health) play a key role in health promo-
tion, they cannot solve many societal issues affecting health alone. The opportunity to align the resources
and contributions of different sectors to address these issues is offered by cross-sector collaboration
(Zhu et al., 2019). Intersectoral partnerships implemented under the concept of relationship marketing
in health care can play an important role in the promotion and implementation of innovative therapeutic
approaches, such as fairy tale therapy in children’s hospitals.
    Numerous empirical studies have shown that children manifest anxieties and fears related to illness
and hospitalisation, which are very difficult experiences for children and their families (Haristiani, Wa-
hyuningsih & Susaldi, 2022; Lamani et al., 2021). There are many methods to reduce anxiety among
hospitalised children (Ouzts, 1991). For example, Margherita, Martino, Recano and Camera (2014) list
a large number of tools available to support children’s and/or family’s difficult experiences described in
the literature: clown therapy, music therapy, pet therapy, book therapy, art therapy and virtual mentor-
ing, play and online group support.
    The above points to the possibility of applying, among other things, bibliotherapy to this type of
therapy. Defining the term bibliotherapy (book therapy) is challenging (McMillen & Pehrsson, 2004).
The term, proposed by Samuel Crothers in 1916, is a combination of the Greek words for therapy and
books (Crothers, 1916 after McMillen & Pehrsson, 2004). Already the ancient Greeks were aware of
the therapeutic power of books by placing the phrase ‘Healing place of the soul’ above the door of the
ancient library at Thebes. But older than literacy is the sharing of stories. Before written language,
bards and story-tellers wove the beauty of language into individual and collective experience, giving
voice to pain and activating the healing process (Berns, 2004). The foundations for the scientific treat-
ment of bibliotherapy were laid by Bruno Bettelheim (1976), who, based on numerous clinical studies,
argued that literature was able to play an important role in child development. In general, bibliotherapy
is a process of dynamic interaction between the reader’s personality and literature under the guidance
of a trained helper (Lucas & Soares, 2013). Bibliotherapy, therefore, is the therapeutic use of literature
(Cohen, 1993) to reduce anxiety among hospitalised children (Naina & Pooja, 2023). It can also be
considered as an adjunct to other therapies (see e.g. McMillen & Pehrsson, 2004; Pardeck & Pardeck,
1998; Riordan & Wilson, 1989).
    While bibliotherapy usually uses classical and lyrical works of high artistic merit, fairy tales, fables
and stories that have a happy ending, are distinguished by a clear axiology (good triumphs over evil),
are instructive and show how the characters deal with their own fears are of value in fairy tale therapy.
Fairy tale therapy is a specific type of bibliotherapy that consists of a system of instruments and proce-


                                                                                                        169
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                     Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




dures aimed at promoting development and improving health (Kuciapinski, 2014). It includes narrative
methods and reading passages based on fantasy and involves a narrator and people, animals, objects and
even plants that come to life. Telling imaginary stories, which can include not only fairy tales but also
fables, myths, legends and other folklore stories, which are very well suited to address the topic, is very
valuable from a pedagogical and psychological point of view. For the fairy tale has a specific function
in the natural life of the individual (Bettelheim 1976) and offers new dimensions to the child’s imagina-
tion. Emotional aspects can enter his consciousness and are processed by his imagination, so that these
elements gradually become tolerated (Margherita et al., 2014). The fairy tale thus provides a road map
that helps children find their way and meanings (Tsitsani et al. 2011). The suggestive story serves as
a springboard for discussion and dialogue (Berns, 2004). According to Betteelheim (1976), fairy tale
therapy influences the child psyche through identification with a positive protagonist, shapes the child’s
personality, inspires the child to overcome difficulties encountered and familiarises the children with
previously unknown concepts, feelings and phenomena. The method helps to solve personal dilemmas
and is also a kind of support in the process of achieving security. It can also be a way to meet children’s
needs (Kuciapinski, 2014).
    Story time therapy is an educational tool that builds bonding and shared understanding, addresses
emotional needs, supports the child’s psychological development and strengthens his or her sense of
self-worth, and helps with problem solving. This three-step process to support a child’s emotional de-
velopment includes reading, structured conversation and educational play. Conscious storytelling and
educational play shape the children’s personality and imagination. At the same time, the little one learns
to communicate his or her own emotions and take on new challenges. Since children tend to model the
characters they identify with, story therapy becomes useful in identifying the thoughts and feelings
of the characters so that children can identify, explore and express their own thoughts, behaviour and
feelings (Gladding & Gladding, 1991; Prater et al., 2006). This method also helps to increase empathic
understanding of others as it promotes interpersonal skills and emotional maturity. It also enhances
self-expression and self-esteem. Additionally, by raising awareness that others have similar problems or
situations, and by providing children with factual information about the problems they are experiencing,
it promotes a sense of belonging and reduces conflict. Emotions such as stress, anxiety and loneliness can
be reduced. Bibliotherapy also improves problem-solving and coping skills (Mitchell-Kamalie, 2002).
    Fairy tale therapy can be particularly valuable in helping children who are unable to cope with the
emotional distress associated with real-life situations. This is because it helps tame emotions (especially
anxiety), relaxes, calms, develops communication skills and supports children to concentrate. The ratio-
nale for using books as a coping mechanism for emotional and behavioural problems in children, or as a
teaching tool, is based on the assumption that people identify with similar characters (Lucas & Soares,
2013). Children often have difficulty identifying and communicating their feelings. In contrast, children’s
engagement in reading allows them to empathise with the characters portrayed in the book, thus gain-
ing insight into a variety of life issues they may face or experience, such as illness, separation, death,
poverty, disability, alienation and others (Lucas & Soares, 2013). This identification and insight leads to
emotional release, new goal-directed behaviours and new ways of interacting with others (Gladding &
Gladding, 1991). Research suggests that bibliotherapy reduces anxiety (Prater et al., 2006), influences
both behaviour and attitudes of students (Cook et al., 2006), and imparts social and developmental skills
(Prater et al., 2006). Research shows that the regular practice of bibliotherapy during hospitalisation
can produce the desired reduction in anxiety among children (Lamani et al., 2021; Ouzts, 1991; Yati et
al., 2017). Adamo et al. (2008) studied fairy tale therapy workshops in children’s oncology wards. In


170
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




all cases, the researchers noted that the use of fairy tale therapy reduced anxiety and fear in participants
and increased reported and observed coping skills and self-efficacy.
    As Borecka (2001) writes, by using bibliotherapy it is possible to influence certain spheres of a per-
son’s life (mental, intellectual, social, emotional) and to some extent to interfere with their behaviour.
Bibliotherapy is an effective therapeutic method used among people of all ages, cultures and backgrounds
(Pehrsson & McMillen, 2005). Importantly, it must be conducted by a trained person (such as a psycholo-
gist, a teacher or a librarian). Bibliotherapy can therefore support the healing process in hospital wards,
sanatoriums, social care facilities or schools, among others (Borecka, 2001).
    Bibliotherapy as a tool can be used in many ways, independently or together with other intervention
methods. It can be used in occupational therapy as an adjunctive form, i.e. as a tool in the hands of a
professional therapist. This is the original meaning of the term, which is strongly determined by the
second term with medical overtones (therapy). At the same time, fairy tale therapy has gone beyond the
framework of psychotherapy and acquired a parallel meaning - a special contact with the child, a method
of solving his or her problems, assisting in his or her development. It is then used not by psychologist-
therapists, but also by parents, carers or teachers.
    Although fairy tale therapy has many advantages, its effectiveness may be limited by various factors
(e.g. availability of books, lack of motivation to read or children’s withdrawal). Some of these limita-
tions can be overcome through regular implementation and use of group discussions and other activities
(Gladding & Gladding, 1991). Other limitations can be identified if the facilitator has limited knowledge
of human development and developmental issues and insufficient knowledge of relevant literature.
Therefore, facilitators need to be adequately trained.
    Fairy tale therapy brings with it a number of additional values. The most important of these are the
promotion of reading and social education. In their work, Selin and Graube (2017), on the other hand,
strongly emphasise another aspect of fairy tale therapy conducted in children’s hospitals. They see it as
a community building tool. Developing as a reader is part of a child’s becoming an individual – when
you can find out what you like, love, when you can discuss it with another person. Being moved by
literature is about experience. The moment a child is introduced to a story, they gain inner strength and
understand how literature and life works. Focusing, recognising and grasping details, imagining faces
and following the events in a book helps to understand society and the interactions between people. A
children’s hospital may be a small and often forgotten place, but it is essential to building a strong and
healthy society. Engaging with the book promotes healthy thinking and attitudes that allow children
to take their own practical experiences with them when they leave hospital. Focusing on patients’ and
parents’ reactions to bibliotherapy activities shows what reading projects can do for children and for
society (Selin & Graube, 2017).

Fairy Tale Therapy as an Example of Modern Healthcare Marketing

The planning and implementation of marketing activities for hospitals is a very important part of their
operation and development. It is worth emphasising at this point that the most important goal of any
healthcare facility should be to act in the service of the patient (Greene et al., 2012). Fairy tale therapy
in children’s hospitals can be considered as an example of modern healthcare marketing. Among other
things, it represents an innovative approach to healthcare that differentiates the hospital, attracts the at-
tention of patients and their families as well as the media and builds the image of the hospital as a place
where innovative methods are used for the benefit of patients.


                                                                                                         171
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                      Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




    Some of the most important objectives of healthcare marketing activities include: creating and main-
taining a positive corporate image, communicating with potential patients, attracting new patients, gaining
the trust of current patients who become ‘ambassadors’ of the healthcare facility in their community or
offering and communicating about new services and treatments, the purchase of innovative equipment
or new medical staff members (see e.g. Berkowitz, 2021; Balogun & Ogunnaike, 2017; Jenkinson et al,
2002; Otani et al., 2010; Waters et al., 2016; Wu, 2011). Fairy tale therapy can help to create a positive
image of the hospital as an institution that cares not only for the physical health but also for the emotional
wellbeing of patients. By promoting this form of therapy and its benefits, the hospital can influence
public awareness of children’s mental health and the need for therapeutic support. This type of therapy
engages children emotionally and creates positive experiences. This, in turn, can help build personalised
emotional connections with patients and their families, which are important in relationship marketing.
Fairy tale therapy can also be considered as a unique service offering of the hospital, setting it apart from
other medical facilities. Allowing it to build the trust of patients and their families and influencing their
loyalty to the hospital. Strengthening its reputation, creating a positive image and enabling it to achieve
a competitive advantage on the healthcare market. In addition, positive feedback and recommendations
from patients and their families can be a valuable communication and promotional tool for the hospital
(Berger et al., 2020; Castro et al., 2016). At the same time, informing the wider community about the
use of a unique form of therapy can attract the attention of the media, patients and their families, as well
as potential donors and business partners.


MATERIALS AND METHODS

Qualitative research is used to study a variety of phenomena related to individuals, groups and organi-
zations (Ding & Ferràs Hernández, 2023) and is an important methodological basis for many scientific
disciplines. Therefore, during the course of the research, it was decided to use the case study as the
main research method, involving the detailed study of a single case, usually in the context of a real phe-
nomenon or problem (Heale & Twycross, 2018). Case study is a research methodology, typically seen
in social and life sciences (Heale & Twycross, 2018). Researchers describe how case studies explore
complex phenomena in a natural setting to increase understanding (Hamel et al., 1993; Yin, 2009). This
method examines a real, contemporary, limited system (case) or multiple limited systems (cases) over
time, through detailed, in-depth data collection involving multiple sources of information, and reports
a case description and case themes (Creswell & Creswell, 2017; Thomas, 2021). It can also be used
to explore complex and multidimensional issues, understand context, examine processes and analyse
interactions between different factors, in this case between different partners (Heale & Twycross, 2018).
Compared to other research methods, the case study offers the richest range of techniques and tools for
data acquisition and analysis. Data sources can include observations, interviews, company documents,
newspaper articles, surveys, and databases maintained by various institutions.
    In the course of the research, it was decided to augment the case study method with a free interview
technique, supported by email interviewing (James, 2007; Opdenakker, 2006), as well as participant
observation. In addition, secondary data were also used for the analysis: documents (reports), informa-
tion on websites and publications (see Brzustewicz et al., 2022). It is also worth quoting Sofaer (1999),
who believes that qualitative methods have been and can be used in health services and health policy
research. Qualitative research methods are valuable in providing descriptions of complex phenomena;


172
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




tracing unique events or illuminating the experiences and interpretations of events by actors with dif-
ferent roles (Sofaer, 1999).
    The analysis of the literature on the subject, together with the secondary materials collected, allowed
the formulation of four main research questions to which the study responds. These are as follows:

Q.1. How is the process of fairy tale therapy implemented within the framework of a tripartite cross-
     sector partnership between hospitals, companies and an NGO whose volunteers conduct fairy tale
     therapy in children’s wards of selected hospitals in Poland?
Q.2. What factors are conducive to the implementation of a fairy-tale therapy program in the cross-sector
     partnership under study?
Q.3. What factors hinder the cooperation of partners involved in the implementation of fairy tale therapy
     in children’s hospital wards?
Q.4. Can the fairy tale therapy method implemented in a cross-sector partnership be considered as a
     multi-faceted relationship marketing instrument?

   In order to find an answer to the research questions posed in this way, interviews were conducted
with representatives of both the Foundation (CEO, a volunteer personally involved in the project), the
company (Sustainability and Internal Communication Expert, a volunteer personally involved in the
project) and the hospital (Promotion and Marketing Department). All interviews were conducted in July
2023. Secondary data were also analysed: documents (social reports published by the Company and
data on fairy tale therapy collected between 2017 and 2022 by the Foundation), information on websites
and publications (see Brzustewicz et al., 2022), which were also relevant to this project. It should be
emphasised that this multidimensional approach allows for a holistic and integrated understanding of
the problem under study.


RESULTS

It was pointed out above that the primary research method is the case study. The choice of method was
dictated by the fact of a limited population and the fact that this method allows for a detailed and in-depth
understanding of a specific phenomenon in its real-life context. In this case, the focus is on presenting
a project for the use of hospital-based fairy tale therapy implemented in a cross-sector partnership (see
Tab.1) as a multi-faceted relationship marketing instrument. The aim of this chapter is to assess the po-
tential of fairy tale therapy as a comprehensive relationship marketing tool in the context of cross-sector
partnerships. This partnership has been implemented since 2017 between an NGO (a foundation), a loan
company and a children’s hospital from one of Poland’s large cities1 . The main aim of the cross-sector
collaboration is to improve the wellbeing of sick children in hospital wards, including helping them cope
with their emotions through the use of story therapy. This is in line with both the NGO’s mission, the
company’s social goals and the hospital’s strategic objectives.
