Partners

NGO Partner (The Foundation)

The Foundation was founded in 2013. The Foundation’s mission is to support children and young people
in hospitals and to promote reading. It uses the book to build quality relationships – from relationships
with oneself, to relationships in one’s immediate environment, to relationships between entire communi-
ties and institutions. The Foundation involves hundreds of people acting together to inspire and support.
Its activities bring together various communities, from actors, singers and journalists to politicians and
government employees, as well as patients, students and parents.
    The Foundation – in cooperation with companies and institutions – runs social support programs
through which it pursues two Sustainable Development Goals: good quality education (Goal 4) and good
health and quality of life (Goal 3).




174
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




    Thanks to the social movement, it has more than 450 hospitals under its wing. The Foundation helps
its beneficiaries by setting up libraries and therapy rooms, as well as by implementing a bibliotherapy
program. Social Campaigns carried out by the Foundation include: The Great Book Collection, Benches
(in the shape of open books) - Imagination Trail. Since its inception, it has been guided by the slogan
– Give away your Book – Create a Library – Inspire Others. The Foundation’s activities are supported
by people well-known from the world of media, culture, art and literature. Thanks to continuously cul-
tivated relationships, unconventional (extraordinary) promotional activities can be carried out together.
    Through its activities, the Foundation can be proud of the following achievements: 1.8 mil collected
books, over 1000 opened libraries, nearly 500 (465) trained fairytales-tellers, over 50 hours of fairytale
therapy per week, 77 cooperating cities and 54 benches on city streets.
    The slogan Give Away Your Books is being implemented through the Great Book Collection. It is a
nationwide campaign, that begins with a press conference attended by the Foundation’s Ambassadors,
representatives of ministries and the media. For one month, a public collection of books is being held in
Poland’s largest cities, which, after sorting, will go to libraries in hospitals and care and aid institutions.
    Along with thousands of collection points, books are being collected by, among others, the Presiden-
tial Palace, the Office of the Children’s Rights Ombudsman, The Parliament, the Ministry of National
Education, the presidents of patronage cities, theatres, museums, municipal stadiums, kindergartens,
schools and the inhabitants of Poland. The Great Book Collection is supported by a nationwide media
campaign (including on Polish Television, Polish Radio and numerous local media). Promotion in the
media is accompanied by an extensive digital and outdoor campaign. As part of the Great Book Collec-
tion, the Foundation organizes numerous reading events in public spaces, such as conferences, readings
in urban and hospital spaces, as well as promotional stands at the Book Fair.
    The essence of PR and communication activities is not just to inform, but to strive for change. Effective
communication is not an end in itself, but only a path to the goal of changing behaviour or attitudes. From
a marketing point of view, it is worth emphasising that the Great Book Collection is a social campaign
that every year brings not only media effects, but above all social effects.
    Activities under the slogan Create a library are a response to the expectations of Polish hospitals.
The Foundation conducted a survey of the needs of public hospitals under the patronage of the Ministry
of Health. Based on the results, a group of beneficiaries was selected and an appropriate set of activities
was created for each group. New Libraries for Small Patients and Libraries for Adults are being opened.
The Foundation has hundreds of hospitals under its permanent care - patients can use the library without
hindrance and even take home a favourite book. The libraries are established thanks to business and
institutional partners who allocate part of their budgets to community activities. The libraries are opened
together with the Foundation’s ambassadors in the presence of the media, representatives of public in-
stitutions, business partners and the beneficiaries themselves. The events are accompanied by fairy tale
therapy workshops, fairytale therapy workshops and interactive theatre plays.
    What inspires people living in Polish cities are the benches. These are urban furniture in the shape
of open books that decorate public spaces. The benches provide residents with inspiring artworks and
free access to audio books. Each bench has a unique design and an artistic ambassador. Well-known
artists, journalists, singers, actors and illustrators were invited to create inspiring bench designs. The
benches are placed in specially dedicated locations in urban spaces to promote reading, as well as other
cultures and arts.
    A non-governmental organization pursues its objectives, inter alia, through cooperation with state
and local government institutions, health care institutions, other non-governmental organizations and


                                                                                                           175
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                     Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




individuals. Such cooperation may take the form of organizational support, partial or total financing
of an undertaking or assistance in obtaining the necessary funds from other sources. Examples of such
activities include the creation of libraries, employee volunteer fairy-tale therapy, preparation of edu-
cational publications (e.g. therapeutic fairy tales for very young oncology patients) and making them
available free of charge (in the form of eBooks and audiobooks). The Foundation also organizes in-
house training in fairy tales and bibliotherapy for company employees and then coordinates a program
in which volunteers hold regular meetings with children or seniors in institutions. The program can also
be conducted online or by telephone.
    The events of recent years (the pandemic, war in Ukraine) have also put the Foundation’s activities on
a different track. In response to the social isolation (pandemic lockdown) during the prevailing outbreak
of the COVID-19 virus, the Foundation’s activities were enriched by an online fairy tale therapy program,
taking place via the Internet. These were book encounters conducted live via LIVE reports on Instagram
by 20 ambassadors – people well-known in the media world. During the pandemic, the Foundation also
ran the “Books - for health!” campaign, which supported hospital patients and wards of care and support
institutions with literature. The campaign was intended to draw attention to the problems resulting from
long-term isolation – disrupted interpersonal relationships, a sense of destabilization, heightened anxiety
or long-term loneliness. These activities complemented traditional treatment methods and supported the
mental sphere, which is of great importance in the healing process.
    In 2022, waves of refugees, especially children, were in need of support due to the war in Ukraine.
Polish and Ukrainian publishers, educators, NGOs and others responded immediately by making content
available for free on their websites. As the Foundation’s mission is to promote reading, social education
and relationship building, links to the most interesting materials were published on the website: e-books
with fairy tales, audiobooks, coloring books and educational materials.

The Business Partner

One of the Foundation’s partners in social activities is a leader on the Polish lending market, a company
that is part of an international financial group based in the UK. The group operates in Central and Eastern
Europe, Mexico and Australia. It has been operating in Poland for more than 25 years and its services
have been used by more than 4 million Poles to date. The company has been repeatedly awarded the titles
of Ethical Company, Top Employer award. “Top Brand” and “Winner European Customer Centricity
Award 2020, and in 2022 it received the “Fair To Women” award.
    The company actively supports campaigns that improve the lives of local communities and smaller
groups in need of help. It is an organisation committed to partnerships and cooperation with other
business and social entities. It has implemented more than 500 social projects, passing on to the social
environment the commitment of company volunteers as well as significant project funding. Since 2006,
it has been implementing an employee volunteering program, caring for local communities, running
financial education programs and planting trees. Many initiatives are long-term programs with the same
beneficiary. The diverse projects allow a large number of employees to be selected and involved, thereby
making a positive impact on the environment in which the company operates. For example, in 2021,
275 employees and client advisors carried out 29 projects, contributing 1,350 hours of volunteering.
Assistance went to more than 770,000 beneficiaries. Employees are given time off on a selected day of
the year to carry out volunteering activities.




176
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




    In 2020-2021, during the COVID-19 pandemic, the company supported local hospitals with finan-
cial and in-kind assistance. In 2022, in turn, the company rented a house for Ukrainian women with
young children who had to flee their country because of the war, and company volunteers renovated and
equipped it within three weeks. To this day, Ukrainian women with children find shelter and psychologi-
cal support in the ‘Mother’s Home’.
    The company has been working with the Foundation since 2017 and has carried out many valuable,
long-term activities for the benefit of many thousands of recipients, e.g. in 2021 the cooperation pro-
gram with the Foundation helped 100,000 beneficiaries. Books were collected for the Foundation at the
company’s facilities. Together, dozens of hospital libraries were opened. The foundations of a volunteer
fairy tale therapy structure were established in a dozen locations. Office space was made available for
staff training in fairy-tale therapy. Trained company volunteers visit young patients in hospitals and
conduct fairy tale therapy activities for them – in addition to reading, they discuss readings, conduct
exercises, play games and spend time with the patients. Many of the company’s employees, including
members of the board of directors, are involved in supporting young patients during difficult times in
hospital. Every year before Christmas, they visit children staying in the wards of the pediatric hospital,
give them presents and read books. In pandemic, a telephone bibliotherapy session was held jointly with
the Foundation for senior citizens from a nursing home.
    As emphasized during the interview, the benefits are probably different for each volunteer, but the
strengthening of interpersonal and communication skills and openness were repeated in the interviews.
For the interviewee, who also visited the young patients, the most important thing was the feeling that
the visit helped, and took their minds off their everyday hospital life. Encounters on hospital wards made
her once again aware of the hierarchy of life values and what is most important in life. The benefits
for employees carry over into benefits for the company. The volunteer experience has strengthened the
commitment of the team. The courage to take on new challenges. A good example is the aforementioned
Mother’s Home. It is a place of refuge for pregnant women and women with young children who had to
flee from war-torn Ukraine. The idea for its creation was born three days after Russia’s attack on Ukraine.
It took less than a month to find the facility and refurbish it with the efforts of company volunteers. Had
it not been for previous experience with, among other things, fairy-tale therapy, it would probably have
been more difficult for the employees to achieve such a spectacular effect.

The Hospital Partner

The Regional Specialized Children’s Hospital is one of the oldest pediatric hospitals in Poland, having
been in operation since 1876. It currently consists of 10 wards, 22 specialist clinics and has 198 beds.
Every day, more than 500 employees care for the smallest patients, in the spirit of the motto: “We fight
for the life, health and smile of the child”. Every year, the hospital provides 77,000 consultations, and
hospitalizes 8,000 patients.
    The hospital’s mission is to promote health and treat all children in need living in Kraków and the
Małopolska voivodship, as well as neighboring voivodships, with particular emphasis on psychiatry,
rheumatology, neurology, allergology, gastrology, endocrinology and pulmonology. It fulfils this mission
through specialized diagnostics and therapy within the framework of inpatient treatment and specialist
outpatient clinics, constantly improving the quality of services, improving the skills of the staff, adapting
to the needs and expectations of patients, improving the conditions of patients’ hospitalization and the
work of the staff. Among the hospital’s strategic objectives is the expansion of cooperation in the field


                                                                                                         177
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                     Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




of health promotion and protection. The treatment offer is enriched by non-standard forms of therapy in
which patients using the services of the hospital and the ward participate, including: dogotherapy, fairy
tale therapy or horticultural therapy (garden therapy).
    One of the hospital’s departments is the Clinical Psychiatry Ward for Children. It is a child psychiatry
centre that provides continuous, comprehensive, all-round care in many forms, including: inpatient, day
treatment, outpatient, individual, group, family or therapeutic community therapy. A strong emphasis
is placed on the psychiatry of the young child. If necessary, short inpatient stays of the child with the
mother are possible. The unit is very experienced in the diagnosis and treatment of holistic developmen-
tal (including autism), behavioural and nutritional disorders. It strives to make the patient’s treatment a
process and to have a permanent team of therapists working with the patient wherever possible. Speech
therapy care is also provided as an ongoing element to support patient development.
    According to Dalton and co-authors (2020), public health emergencies such as pandemic take a toll
on both physical and mental health. Children are particularly vulnerable to such situations, due to their
limited understanding of events. They may not be able to communicate their feelings in the same way as
adults. Children, due to their inability to escape physical and psychological harm, have limited coping
strategies. Conversely, school closures and separation from friends in younger and older children can
cause stress and anxiety. Psychological suffering can also be exacerbated by media coverage of crisis
events and unverified information circulating on social media (Dalton et al., 2020). The authors of ‘The
State of the World’s Children 2021’ report call on national governments and public and private sector
partners to commit to improving the mental health of all children and protecting those in need. Among
the demands are investments in prevention efforts, programs for parents and caregivers or tackling the
taboo on mental illness (Keeley, 2021). Imran and colleagues (2020) also postulate that prioritising
mental health, including child and adolescent mental health, should be an essential component of any
universal, community-led response to the COVID-19 pandemic (Imran et al., 2020). Concern for the
mental health of young children and the sense of responsibility of the volunteers involved meant that
one of the first hospital wards to which the fairy tale therapy program returned after the pandemic was
the Psychiatry Ward for Children in Regional Specialised Children’s Hospital.

Overview of the Partnership: Fairy Tale Therapy
Program in Children’s Hospitals

The fairy tale therapy program, implemented in Polish hospitals, is a CSR program created for a busi-
ness entity by a non-governmental organisation (the Foundation). The program was planned in such a
way as to use not only the financial potential, but also the personal, organizational and content-related
potential of the business partner. The implementation of the project was preceded by an analysis and
consideration of the needs of beneficiaries and volunteers. Importantly, at its core was the compatibility
of the partners’ organizational goals, which influenced the course of the partnership. This is a rare model
of cooperation between a large financial institution and an NGO, in which both entities are partners,
supporting each other with their resources and achieving tangible social results – far from greenwashing
and sham activities.
    Fairy-tale education means reading books, discussing and playing together with children. Volunteers
visit hospitals and children’s homes, carrying out story-education meetings with the foundation’s youngest
beneficiaries. Their aim is to show the children’s guardians (parents, educators, teachers) a concrete tool
through which it is possible to build a deep relationship with the child, to identify and name emotions


178
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




together with him or her, and to support him or her in difficult experiences. In this case, developmen-
tal bibliotherapy is implemented, which can be used by teachers and other helpers to facilitate normal
development and self-actualisation in an essentially healthy population, as opposed to clinical biblio-
therapy, which is used by trained professionals dealing with serious emotional or behavioural problems
(McMillen & Pehrsson, 2004).
    After opening a hundred libraries, the Foundation looked for a scientifically researched tool to use
books as psychological support. This is how a program of first fairy tale therapy and then bibliotherapy
was developed, both involving the use of the therapeutic function of literature. This is of particular rel-
evance in hospital centres as well as in aid and care institutions.
    Initially, the Foundation made contact with the establishments itself. The person responsible for
contacting the beneficiaries agreed on the details and a cooperation agreement was signed. Suggestions
from local coordinators were also used. There was never any trouble with the establishments, they were
very willing to cooperate, seeing many benefits, already described in the context of fairy tale therapy.
Contracts were always renewed, demonstrating a desire to continue the program.
    The program has over 200 volunteers and 26 coordinators. Each volunteer, prior to the start of the
fairytale therapy meetings, undergoes a comprehensive training in fairytale therapy. During the work-
shop, volunteers gain knowledge and skills on fairy tale therapy and identifying their own emotions.
Volunteers are supervised by local coordinators who accompany them on their first visits to the centres.
    In the period 2017 - 2020 (before the COVID-19 pandemic), fairy tale therapy took place periodically,
up to 28 times a week, for nearly 60 hours, in institutions located in 13 Polish cities. It was conducted
in many different paediatric wards – cardiology, oncology, haematology, orthopaedics, psychiatry or
internal medicine. Weekly meetings are listed on the schedule (see tab. 2).


Table 2. Weekly fairy tale therapy meetings

                                                                             Number of Cities (on   Number of Hours (on a
    Day of the Week                  Location (on a Given Day)
                                                                                a Given Day)            Given Day)
       Mondays                       hospitals, children’s homes                      2                      5
       Tuesdays                       hospitals, care institution                     8                     21,5
                                      hospitals, children’s home                      8                     16,5
       Thursdays              hospitals, children’s home, care institution            8                     15,5
 Source: own preparation based on materials from The Foundation



   Among the most important benefits resulting from the mutual cooperation of the actors involved
were the increased success of all parties’ undertakings thanks to the use of synergies, mutual inspiration
or gaining insights from a different perspective and the exchange of experiences and skills between the
partners, as well as the value for the local community and increased public trust in all actors. During
the implementation of the project, between 2017 and 2022, several hundred thousand children were the
beneficiaries. It is worth noting that, according to interviewees from the Foundation, Parents also benefit
from the project. They often listen or ask what the fairy tale therapy method is all about and this is also
a very important aspect of the fairy tale activities. From the company’s perspective, the participation
of selected employees in the project as volunteers has raised the social awareness of other employees



                                                                                                                        179
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                     Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




and increased interest in volunteering within the company. Company volunteers emphasised that the
project had an impact on all aspects of their lives, both inside and outside the organisation. It improved
relationships between employees and made them rely on each other more, in a way that they were ready
to help each other in their private lives as well. Participation in the project increased employees’ aware-
ness of the social problems around them and, as a result, their willingness to help others increased. At an
organizational level, positive relationships between employees increased their satisfaction and fostered
greater commitment to their work. In turn, increased employee engagement encouraged the company
to increase support for the implementation of future editions of the project (Brzustewicz et al., 2022).
    The fairy tale therapy program, in a modified form, also took place during the pandemic when all
hospitals were closed. Two programs were launched in 2020. The first involved evening fairy tale therapy
meetings led by ambassadors (40 meetings, 26 ambassadors involved). The second program, on the
other hand, was online story time with volunteers (31 story times) who met with children in hospital
wards before the pandemic. In 2021, on the other hand, a special tele-library therapy program for senior
citizens was launched, an online fairy tale therapy program was introduced and 113 fairy tale therapy
educators were trained (also online). After the lockdown was lifted, fairy tale therapy was reactivated
in two cities. The Foundation has recently received grants to develop this program in children’s hospital
wards, which gives hope for thriving activities in the years to come.

Factors Facilitating Cross-Sectoral Collaboration During the
Implementation of a Fairy Tale Therapy Program in Hospitals

Zhu and co-authors (2019) list among the factors affecting cross-sector partnerships both those that fos-
ter them and those that hinder collaboration. Based on this typology, seven facilitating factors and four
inhibiting factors can be identified, based on information gleaned from interviews and conversations,
analyses of reports and the authors’ own observations (one of the co-authors participates as a volunteer
in a fairy tale therapy program).
    Thus, among the facilitating factors for mobilising cross-sector collaborations are:

1.    Shared values – at an organizational level, for all partners, meeting the needs of beneficiaries
      was paramount from the outset. The alignment of the organisation’s strategic goals, the sharing
      of complementary resources and the high level of commitment of top management enabled the
      partnership to work together. The action of the partners on the basis of shared values led to positive
      changes in the social awareness of employees and strengthened the commitment of the company
      and the Foundation to CSR goals, which, as Brzustewicz and her team (2022) note, indicates the
      emergence of social impact.
2.    Durability of cooperation – cooperation was facilitated by effective communication between
      partners and an atmosphere of mutual trust. In the case in question, the comprehensiveness and
      longevity of the activities initiated together are also characteristic. These elements allowed for
      the development of a long-term trilateral relationship. Reflecting on the experience of partner-
      ship, as well as on individual values and emotions, is an important source of learning that helps
      to understand and appreciate the differences encountered (Battisti, 2009). The Foundation highly
      values the partnership with the company and claims that the collaborative model created together
      can be a model of action for cross-sector partnerships. The partnership with the Foundation is also
      rated very highly by the Company. Its cooperation arouses a great deal of positive emotion among


180
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




     employees, advisors, customers and also cooperating companies. Thanks to the cooperation with
     the Foundation, the company is sure that the needed help goes to the right places. During visits to
     the hospital ward by the volunteers conducting fairy tale therapy, the little patients often ask about
     the next visit and expect it to continue, suggesting that fairy tale therapy is a meaningful element
     for children during their hospital stay.
3.   Commitment of employees and volunteers – a successful collaboration would not be possible
     without the commitment of employees. The company’s volunteers are part of the Foundation’s
     team and work together to achieve the mission. In addition to the donated funds, they are realisti-
     cally involved in the Foundation’s activities – they donate books, receive training in story therapy,
     attend library openings and regularly visit young patients in paediatric wards. Participation in
     the project has been a source of great emotional commitment and satisfaction for the volunteers,
     which ultimately motivates them to continue their involvement in the project. As a result, the value
     offered to the beneficiaries is unique and provides an opportunity to create wider social change.
     Satisfaction with the introduction of this form of activation into the employee volunteering offer
     is rated very highly by the company. The company conducts satisfaction surveys on the activities
     offered after successive editions of its volunteering programs, including fairy tale therapy con-
     ducted in paediatric wards. The conclusions of the regular surveys are very satisfying. Volunteers
     appreciate that volunteering has given them the chance to do something good and to strengthen
     their skills. They also appreciate that the company is involved in such activities both financially and
     organisationally. From the company’s perspective, the participation of selected employees in the
     project as volunteers has increased the social awareness of other employees and increased interest
     in volunteering within the company. Involvement in the fairy tale therapy process was shown not
     only by the volunteers and beneficiaries (little patients), but also by their families and medical staff.
     This is important, as the health service needs service-oriented and customer-focused staff who
     develop informed relationships with clients. The RM approach is extremely important, especially
     because of the critical role of the human factor in health service delivery (Ersoy & Tehci, 2023).
4.   Hospitals openness – the analysed hospital joined the project in 2017. The introduction of this
     form of activation into the department’s offer, as well as the cooperation with partners, are rated
     very highly by the hospital. There are a number of benefits that the hospital sees from this form
     of contact with patients (both for the hospital, staff and patients). The hospital is building its posi-
     tive image in the province. As bibliotherapy is not intended as a treatment but as a complement
     to it (Caldin, 2009), it provides an opportunity to broaden and diversify children’s activities and
     allows them to be observed in new situations. The activities help children to calm down, exercise
     concentration and imagination, and invite them to reflect and talk about important topics. Hospital
     staff, recognising the range of benefits, have been very open and supportive of the implementation
     of this program.
5.   Health-promoting context – a collaboration between different sectors allows access to different
     resources, knowledge and experiences. This is extremely important in developing and promoting
     innovative approaches to working with, for example, sick children. In the case study, shared values,
     activism and cooperation are promoted. The need for mental and emotional support for children and
     young people, especially after the pandemic, is indisputable and recognised by many communities.
     Here, cross-sector partnerships focus on improving the wellbeing of children on hospital wards and
     their emotional support. Partners help to secure funding, human resources and community support,
     which is crucial to the success of such projects.


                                                                                                          181
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                      Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




6.    Cross-sector leadership and governance – it is important to emphasise that on the part of each
      partner, board members and middle managers are very much involved in the project. Through
      this activity, they inspire their employees and others to take action. The culture of volunteering
      is very much entrenched in the company, as an employee volunteering program has been in place
      since 2006. Not only rank-and-file employees but also directors and board members get involved
      in projects. When the company started working with an NGO in 2017, employees, in addition to
      setting up shared libraries or collecting books, also joined in the fairy tale therapy program. This
      approach allowed the company to actively use the potential of its volunteers and involve them in
      another new type of assistance – competence volunteering. Company volunteers trained by the
      Foundation’s experts in fairy tale therapy use the knowledge they gain at meetings in hospitals.
      Senior management is also involved in the action of reading to children who are in paediatric wards.
      The company’s CEO and members of the management team visit children in the paediatric hospital
      on the occasion of Santa Claus.
7.    Supporting activities – social campaigns, such as the creation of libraries, the Great Book Collection,
      Benches (in the shape of open books) or inviting people known from the world of media, culture,
      art and literature to take part in promotional activities – these initiatives facilitate partner recogni-
      tion and partnership building, which translates into easier acquisition of sponsorship and funding.

   On the other hand, the following factors stand out among the challenges inhibiting cross-sectoral
cooperation when implementing a fairy tale therapy program in hospitals

1.    The mental load on volunteers – the fairy tale therapy is challenging. It is working with the emo-
      tions of both children and volunteers. Therefore, it is a good idea to prepare well for the activities.
      Before each visit, the volunteer should answer the question whether he or she is ready for the meet-
      ing today, whether he or she has the energy and strength to share it with others. After the meeting,
      he or she should leave himself or herself space and time to reflect, to talk to a more experienced
      person. Peer support is extremely important, especially when volunteers visit ‘difficult’ hospital
      wards, e.g. paediatric cardiology, oncology, haematology or psychiatry.
2.    Moral and organizational constraints – in the opinion of the interviewee from the Company,
      fairy tale therapy classes are a special kind of volunteering. In story therapy, the most important
      thing is to follow the child and his/her needs. The number of pages read or the precise adherence
      to the lesson plan is not important. Sometimes, just being willing to do the activities is enough.
      Sometimes the fairy tale therapy is not carried out due to the children’s bad mood. It is therefore
      important to be open to the little patient, but also to his or her loved ones, who sometimes accom-
      pany him or her to the hospital. It is also important to be able to withdraw if the patient is tired or
      unwell. In some cases, especially for children with trauma or difficult experiences, the use of some
      elements of story therapy may need to be used with caution to avoid triggering unwanted emotional
      reactions.

    In contrast, the limitations identified by the hospital associated with the provision of fairy tale therapy
are the difficulties in delivering the program in larger groups of patients. As Pardeck & Pardeck (1993)
suggest: “the ideal group size is between six and eight participants. The group should be homogeneous.
The members of the group should be of a similar age or be more or less at the same developmental level,
so that the book chosen is suitable for all members of the group”. Due to the variety of patients staying


182
﻿
Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




in the same ward at any given time and place, it is sometimes very difficult to fulfil these conditions.
Another issue is also that sometimes children may seem uninterested or distracted (Lucas & Soares, 2013).
However, it is worth bearing in mind that some children can absorb information even when their backs
are turned to the storyteller. This suggests individual differences in learning styles, but also indicates
that the beneficial effects of bibliotherapy may only become apparent after some time (Berns, 2004).
    There are also limited opportunities to use fairy tale therapy in a wider context as a valuable external
communication and promotional tool for the hospital. It is very important to respect the intimacy and
dignity of young patients and to protect their image and sensitive data. In some situations, for example
in a psychiatry or haematology department, it is forbidden or undesirable to capture images of children
participating in fairy tale therapy on any audiovisual media.

3.   Financial and staffing constraints – constraints of this kind can inhibit the creation, operation
     and maintenance of cross-sectoral cooperation. Limited external sources of funding (such as grants)
     and reliance largely on funds raised from sponsors and partners, limit opportunities to engage in
     new initiatives or expand existing ones. In addition, difficulties in recruiting volunteers for some
     programs can also be a threat, undermining their sustainability. The effectiveness of bibliotherapy
     may also be limited by the availability of books or materials or resources. Others are related to
     insufficient knowledge of the person conducting the fairy tale therapy. This may relate, for example,
     to developmental problems of children and adolescents as well as the selection of appropriate
     literature. Hence, the demand for proper preparation and training of all volunteers is extremely
     important.
4.   Geographical dispersion - the structure of cross-sector partnerships is influenced by geographical
     location, with which certain cultural factors or material resources are associated (Awad, 2023). In
     addition, the dispersion of beneficiaries creates logistical challenges and influences the choice of
     facility in a given city. In addition to the needs of the hospital, the ability of volunteers to commute,
     coming from different parts of the city and even suburban areas, is also taken into account. Among
     other things, the public transport network and connections to other parts of the city are important.
     Access is also facilitated by existing cycle paths and the number of parking spaces in the hospital
     car park. It is also worth emphasising that our study refers to the presentation of the application of
     fairy tale therapy in one hospital in Poland, while the fairy tale therapy program is implemented
     in 13 Polish cities. This has the effect of limiting, among other things, the possibility of frequent
     interpersonal contacts between partners, coordinators and volunteers.


DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION

This study extends the existing literature on the growing use of intersectoral collaboration in addressing
important social problems through the use of story-telling. An existing three-way cross-sector partnership
between hospitals, businesses and an NGO is taking action to improve the mental health of hospitalised
children. The fairy tale therapy program conducted in selected children’s hospitals can at the same time
be seen as a marketing instrument to support relationship building in health care.
   When analysing the partnership, one can see the interconnectedness of interests between the part-
ners. The results show that each partner brings complementary resources that are relevant to achieving
the partnership’s mission. The relatively close cooperation between partners leads to interaction value


                                                                                                          183
                                                                                                        ﻿
                                       Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




from the point of view of all organizations involved. Cross-sector partnership refers to a collaboration in
which all participants were fully and equally involved. This is in line with the work of Morgan & Hunt
(1994), who argue that the development of long-term relationships is possible by creating an atmosphere
of mutual trust built primarily through processes of communication and sustained collaboration between
partners. The previously cited factors of trust, bonding, communication, shared value, empathy, and
reciprocity (Sin et al., 2002) influence the relationship between partners. The research conducted also
allowed for the identification and analysis of factors that foster and constrain cross-sectoral coopera-
tion. Their analysis in the context of the implementation of the current or similar programs will enable
positive factors to be strengthened and negative factors to be counteracted.
    Fairy tale therapy can be integrated into children’s hospital provision in a way that is not only effec-
tive, but also strategic. By involving partners from different sectors, it can create strong synergies that
benefit both the children and the organizations involved in the therapy. Children’s hospitals can establish
partnerships with community organizations, foundations or other stakeholders interested in supporting
children in their recovery. They can also provide access to funding, expertise, volunteers or other resources
needed to deliver story therapy. These partnerships can also help to raise the profile and visibility of
the fairy tale therapy program through joint campaigns and promotional activities. Fairy tale therapy
can also be a tool for working with the local community. Collaborating with community organizations,
schools or foundations that promote children’s health and well-being can strengthen relationships with
local stakeholders and build the hospital’s reputation as a place where patients are treated holistically.
However, it is important to note that despite the marketing aspects, fairy tale therapy in children’s hospi-
tals is primarily aimed at providing therapeutic support and improving the wellbeing of young patients.
Marketing here is a tool to promote and reach the right people, but it should not override the primary
goal, which is the health and well-being of patients.
    Our analysis has several limitations. To generalise the results, more similar community projects of-
fered in other locations should be analysed. Although they may be similar, their complexity and unique
characteristics may affect the formation and implementation of cross-sector partnerships and the outcomes
of the byway program. The local context can significantly influence the types of collaborations and the
factors contributing to their success. Our findings may not reflect the diversity of experiences in other
areas. Secondly, our data on cross-sectoral activities was reported by key informants, no larger scale
research was conducted. Thirdly: children were not included in the interviews, even though they are the
largest and most important group of beneficiaries. Based on the ethical principle of respect for persons,
parental consent and child consent function together to protect the child and promote self-determination
(Roth-Cline & Nelson, 2013). Parents or legal guardians must give their consent (written or verbal) to the
implementation of research involving the child, and in many cases must be present during the research.
This rule should absolutely apply to any type of research involving children (whether medical or market-
ing). It is not in every case possible for a parent to accompany a child during a hospital stay, and hence,
for this group of beneficiaries, participant observation has been limited. Due to the turnover of patients
in hospital wards, it is also difficult to assess unequivocally its effectiveness in relation to a specific child.
    In an era of disruptive technology, revolutionary change, pervasive digitisation and artificial intel-
ligence, healthcare systems around the world are transforming and changing. The goal of these changes
is to make healthcare more affordable, accessible, acceptable, collaborative and positive. The advance-
ment of digital technology and its impact on every area of life is happening continuously on a global
scale. However, it will not replace real, deep relationships and artificial intelligence will not teach how
to deal with emotions.

KEY TERMS AND DEFINITIONS

   Bibliotherapy: A therapeutic approach using books and other forms of literature used to support a
patient’s mental health.
   Case Study: One of the qualitative research methods, the main aim of which is to illustrate a certain
case as well as possible. It provides an in-depth analysis of a specific phenomenon. It includes a detailed
analysis of the case, goals, objectives, motives, and actions.
   Cross-Sector Collaboration: A process where organizations from different sectors come together to
focus their expertise and resources on a complex issue important to the community they want to support.




                                                                                                         191
                                                                                                       ﻿
                                      Cross-Sector Partnerships and Relationship Marketing in Healthcare




    Fairy Tale Therapy: An important method of support and therapeutic influence for children, which
can help them to deal effectively with emotions or difficult situations (e.g., illness, pain, frustration).
    Partnerships: A voluntary, cooperative relationship between participants (partners) from different
sectors, established to achieve a common objective or undertake a specific task and share the risks,
responsibilities, resources, competences, and benefits.
    Relationship Marketing: Is a relationship management strategy that emphasises customer, patient,
etc. satisfaction and loyalty.
    Relationships: Interrelationships with other actors (individuals, groups, organizations) in the inter-
personal space.
