ABSTRACT
The work aims to characterize and analyze the economic policy of healthcare development in Georgia
and, in particular, the policy of digitalization of healthcare. The healthcare system in Georgia has been
characterized as a mixture of Bismarck and Beveridge models, with the government’s universal healthcare
program recently expanding, the goal of which is to increase the affordability of health services for the
population. The work describes the goals, tools, institutions, and problems of Georgia’s development
policy and digitalization of healthcare. In conclusion, the authors consider those areas that should be
maximally strengthened in the policy for digitalizing healthcare in Georgia to achieve the goals successfully.



INTRODUCTION

The territory of Georgia is 69,700 square kilometers. According to the 2014 census, the population is
3.7 million. After the collapse of the Soviet Union, namely in 1991, Georgian statehood was restored. It
should also be noted that the unfavorable economic situation, the low solvency of newly created (based
on privatization) private enterprises, and the lack of a unified database of payers have always been
severe obstacles to developing healthcare and social insurance. Informal out-of-pocket payments have
become widespread due to limited government funding for the health care system. The private insurance
companies created during this period were less interested in the healthcare market.
   With the help of the United States and EU countries, Georgia overcame the most difficult challenges
and problems that generally arose in all countries during such a period. Gross domestic product per capita
increased from US$3,233 to US$6,571 between 2010 and 2023 (World Ometers, n.d.).


DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch013



       Copyright © 2024, IGI Global. Copying or distributing in print or electronic forms without written permission of IGI Global is prohibited.
                                                                                                           ﻿
                                                Policy for Digitalization of the Georgian Healthcare System




   Economic growth has had a significant impact on improving the population’s health status, which has
a positive impact on the process of achieving health-related sustainable development goals. As of 2020,
the average life expectancy from birth for men is 68.8 years, and for women it is 77.8 years. In Georgia,
women live on average nine years longer than men. (World Life Expectancy, n.d.).


GEORGIAN HEALTHCARE SYSTEM

Until 1995, the Soviet healthcare model existed in Georgia, which then virtually completely excluded
(due to the lack of financial resources at the disposal of the state) the implementation of the interests
of poor citizens.
    According to Article 37 of the 1995 Constitution of Georgia (adopted on August 24, 1995), the state
declared for the first time that the burden of responsibility for health care was distributed among various
state entities, and health care services were no longer accessible. What was new to the country’s citizens
was that the state’s responsibility was no longer comprehensive, but that obligations were determined
by state health care programs and industry management (regulatory) mechanisms. The public still had
the right to access medical services provided by government healthcare programs. In reality, due to the
highly meager healthcare funding from the state, the population had nothing (Verulava, 2022).
    The financing of the health care system was initially carried out according to the social insurance
model (Bismarck model). With the introduction of the social insurance system, the funding party (the
insurance company or the Ministry of Health) and the providers were separated. The financed party had
the right to choose a medical institution at its discretion, which increased competition between them and,
therefore, positively impacted the quality of service.
    Reforms in the healthcare sector began in 2004, but actual steps were taken in Resolution N11 of
the Georgian government of 2007, which adopted the “Master Plan for the Development of Hospitals.”
This meant the creation of 100 multidisciplinary hospitals (with 7,800 beds) in the country, but there
were no financial resources in the regions, and the plan remained unrealized.
    Since 2007, the transition to a tax model (Beveridge model) has begun because, for developing coun-
tries, the tax system has an advantage over the social insurance model. Particularly in those developing
countries where the informal sector is more developed, the Beveridge model has the potential to spread
more rapidly. However, the tax system burdens the state budget. In addition, there is uneven funding as
it may result in subsidization of the self-employed, but not necessarily the poor. Thus, for Georgia, as a
country with a small, open, and developing market, a system of financing health care through general
taxes (the Beveridge model) was then appropriate.
    Until 2013, the low level of economic development (with a meager state budget) hampered the imple-
mentation of set goals in the healthcare sector, and a significant part of them turned out to be unattainable.
    Since 2013, in parallel with the Beveridge model, a state program of universal healthcare was launched,
which aims to increase the financial accessibility of medical services for the population. All citizens
of Georgia are provided with essential medical services under public and private insurance programs.
For example, in 2017, compared to 2012, the volume of government allocations for the health sector
doubled and increased from 365 million GEL to 800 million GEL. (Ministry of Health, n.d.a) We share
the views of health policy researchers who believe that income growth increases the costs of health care
and the costs of managing this process. (Hagenaars et al., 2017)) At the same time, it should be noted
that the introduction of telemedicine and artificial intelligence technologies should reduce management


226
﻿
Policy for Digitalization of the Georgian Healthcare System




costs and increase the efficiency of the healthcare system. However, this also has tools and institutions
for the implementation of economic health policy in Georgia.
    The main goal of the state health policy is to improve the health status of the population of Georgia
through universal access to health services, eliminating inequalities and equitable sharing of financial
burdens.
    The goals subordinated to the primary goal of health protection are:

1.     Improving health sector management;
2.     Increasing the fairness and efficiency of the health care financing system;
3.     Development of human resources in the healthcare sector;
4.     Ensuring access to high-quality, effective, and safe medicines and medical products;
5.     Strengthening the health management information system;
6.     Improving the quality of medical services;
7.     Strengthening the health system to improve preparedness and response to threats.

     The central government institutions implementing health policy in Georgia are:

•      Ministry of Internally Displaced Persons from the Occupied Territories, Labour, Health and
       Social Affairs of Georgia; (Ministry of Health, n.d.b)
•      LLC National Center for Disease Control and Public Health named after L. Sakvarelidze;
•      NHA – National Health Agency (NHA);
•      LSI – Medical and Pharmaceutical Regulatory Agency
•      SSI – Information Technology Agency;
•      LSI – Center for Emergency Response and Assistance Coordination.

     Important tools for implementing health policy on the territory of the state and in the legal space are:

•      Law of Georgia “On Health Protection” 1997;
•      Law of Georgia “On Health Insurance” of 1997, which was declared invalid as of July 1, 2007;
•      Law of Georgia on the rights of patients. Accepted 05/05/2000;
•      Law of Georgia “On Medical Activities,” adopted on June 8, 2001; And
•      The Law of Georgia on HIV/AIDS was adopted on November 17, 2009.

    It should also be noted that these tools are constantly being improved.
    “Georgia Solidarity Fund” (founded in June 2014) aims to support children diagnosed with cancer
and young people under the age of 22, which includes financing diagnostics and treatment in Georgia and
abroad. In 2013, the Georgian government, in a socially oriented budget, has unprecedentedly increased
(almost doubled) the health sector’s budget. However, several expensive medical services remain that
available government resources cannot fully cover. Based on international experience, the organization
mobilizes financial resources based on voluntary donations from citizens and the public and private sec-
tors. The funds raised are used for the treatment of the beneficiaries of the fund. Treatment is financed
in Georgia (in the amount of the share remaining outside the state program) and abroad.
    At this stage, the fund collects financial resources from the following sources: stable voluntary micro-
donations from the salaries of civil servants; similar voluntary donations from citizens working in the


                                                                                                         227
                                                                                                          ﻿
                                               Policy for Digitalization of the Georgian Healthcare System




private sector; one-time donations; Integration of a charitable component into a product by companies;
charity events and auctions; Patronage (the company fully covers the treatment costs of one beneficiary).
100% of the funds mobilized in the fund are used to finance the treatment of beneficiaries. For maximum
transparency of activities, the Solidarity Fund is managed by the Supervisory Board and the Standing
Commission, which, along with the public sector, includes representatives of the non-governmental and
private sectors, as well as the UN permanent coordinator in Georgia. The state covers the administrative
costs of the fund.
    The state concept of the Georgian healthcare system – “Universal healthcare and quality management
to protect patients’ rights” – was adopted on December 29, 2014. The mentioned state concept covered
the period 2014-2020.
    The state concept of the healthcare system identified ten main priorities:

1.    Health in all policies - a common state multi-sectoral approach - several multi-sectoral strate-
      gies have been approved, and their implementation has begun with the participation of various
      ministries: Georgian State Tobacco Control Strategy (2013); National Strategy for the Prevention
      and Control of HIV/AIDS for 2016–2018 (2016); National et al. 2016–2020 (2016); Georgian et
      al. for Promoting Maternal and Newborn Health for 2017–2030 (2017); National strategy for the
      prevention and control of non-communicable diseases in Georgia for 2017-2020 (from 2023 the
      state will fully finance liver transplantation for GEL 120,000) (Ministry of Health, 2023)

    National Strategy for Eliminating Hepatitis C in Georgia for 2016–2020 (2016). It should be noted
here that organ transplant recipients could not exercise the right to determine disability status, for which
appropriate changes were introduced in January of this year, and liver, heart, or kidney transplantation
is the basis for determining significant disability status.

2.    Development of health sector management. Developing a strategy and action plan for the develop-
      ment of the Georgian healthcare system has begun and will be completed soon. One of the most
      critical tasks is the harmonization of rules in the field of pharmaceuticals, safe blood, human organs
      and tissues, tobacco, and alcohol with the current legislation of the European Union.
3.    Improving the health care financing system - work was divided into the introduction of strategic
      procurement mechanisms, payment based on results, and various cost containment methods (selec-
      tive contracting, DRG).
4.    Development of the quality of medical services - a document on the classification of medical in-
      stitutions has been prepared, permitting conditions for inpatient institutions to be clarified. By the
      year’s end, a strategy for developing hospital and primary health care was developed.
5.    Development of human resources in the healthcare sector - since 2014, funding has begun for the
      postgraduate education of medical applicants for their employment in mountainous and border
      regions. Since 2016, with the support of the US Threat Reduction Agency (DTRA), the imple-
      mentation of the British Medical Journal (BMJ) online platform has begun in Georgia. Since 2017,
      participation in the continuing medical education system has become mandatory for persons work-
      ing in the perinatal service.
6.    Development of health care management information systems. In 2014, an electronic register of
      patients in hospitals was introduced. Since 2015, an electronic register of diseases registered in
      institutions providing outpatient services and a new system for recording malignant neoplasms


228
﻿
Policy for Digitalization of the Georgian Healthcare System




     (population cancer register) have been implemented. Since 2016, a mobile and tablet application
     of the National Preventive Vaccination Calendar and an “electronic module for monitoring the
     health of pregnant women and newborns” (birth register) have been introduced. Table 1. presents
     the objectives, tools, and challenges of strengthening the health management information system.
7.   Strengthening the health of mothers and children - the perinatal regionalization project started in
     2015 and ended in 2017, which involves determining the levels of institutions providing perinatal
     services, their roles, and responsibilities. In 2016, specific treatment for pregnant women with
     sexually transmitted diseases (syphilis) was started. Since 2018, the state has funded eight prenatal
     visits.

   Improving the prevention and treatment of priority infectious diseases - the Hepatitis C Elimination
Program was launched in 2015. Since 2017, the Samegrelo-Upper Svaneti region began integrating the
detection of HIV, tuberculosis, and hepatitis C into the PJD component. A transition plan for funding
programs to combat AIDS and tuberculosis was developed. In order to prevent drug-resistant forms of
tuberculosis, the dispensary service for patients has been strengthened.

9.  Improving the prevention and control of priority non-communicable diseases - the regulatory
    framework for controlling tobacco use has been introduced since May 2018. A drug program for the
    treatment of chronic diseases was launched, and a population-based cancer registry was launched.
    Since 2015, the development of community-based mental health services began.
10. Development of the public health system. Since 2014, Georgia has actively supported the imple-
    mentation of the global health security initiative, which is based on the aspects of preventing,
    detecting, and responding to risks caused by infectious diseases. Of the 11 action packages under
    the initiative, Georgia places particular emphasis on the “real-time epidemic surveillance” action
    package and supports the “zoonotic diseases” and “national laboratory system” action packages
    (Ministry of Health, n.d.c).

    Even partial implementation of these priorities has significantly improved the healthcare situation in
Georgia. The mortality rate is decreasing throughout the world, and so is Georgia. The reason for this is,
first of all, the development of the healthcare sector. New technologies and treatments being introduced
into healthcare are increasing the ability to detect and control diseases at an early stage. As a result,
despite population growth, the aging process continues, and the proportion of the elderly population
increases, ultimately reducing the mortality rate. (Verulava & Jorbenadze, 2021)
    The Georgian National Health Strategy for 2022-2030 was approved by the Government of Georgia
Resolution N230 in 2022. This document is the main instrument of health policy. It includes Georgia’s
obligations in the European Association, which imply cooperation in protecting public health, improving
security, and protecting human rights. The document is also a key component of sustainable develop-
ment and economic growth.
    Regular monitoring and evidence-based evaluation will be carried out annually, with a report prepared
within 60 calendar days after the end of the reporting year and a progress report every six months. The
assessment of the National Health Strategy will be carried out in two stages. The interim assessment of
the strategy will take place in the first half of 2026, when the necessary data on the indicators presented
in the strategy’s logical framework are fully available, and the final assessment will take place at the last
stage of policy implementation, but no later than the first half of 2031 (Legislative Herald of Georgia, n.d.).

.pdf



   Among the strategy’s priorities, the fifth goal is strengthening health management information sys-
tems (Ministry of Health, n.d.d)
   Health information systems (HMIS) play an increasingly important role in the healthcare system; they
are designed to manage health-related data and are used by various stakeholders, including healthcare
providers, patients, and government decision-makers.
   Further strengthening the health management information system in Georgia (Goal 5) includes the
implementation of the following three sub-goals: improving the national structure of e-health, digital
health, and governance; strengthening the architecture and services of the electronic health care system
by improving the electronic system itself and the quality of data exchange; and strengthening digital
health technologies and telemedicine services.
   The indicator for achieving the fifth goal is the health information system productivity index. Its base
indicator for 2020 is 24, and in 2030, we should reach 30.
   Subgoal 5.1 – Strengthening digital health governance. The indicator reflects the proportion of ser-
vice providers in the electronic health reporting system (eHealth). The 2020 baseline is 93%, and the
2030 target is 100%. The main problems of the policy for the development of healthcare management
information systems are:

1.     Georgia does not yet have a national e-health strategy and a corresponding action plan;
2.     There is no effective implementation of the electronic health system policy based on the need to
       strengthen the management capacity of IT services;
3.     more analytical tools must be used to assess the effectiveness of government health programs.




230
﻿
Policy for Digitalization of the Georgian Healthcare System




    Subordinate goal 5.2. Strengthening the architecture and services of e-health, improving the electronic
system, and the quality of data exchange. Outcome indicator for achieving the goal: the proportion of
medical institutions that have “Brief information about the patient,” electronic medical records, electronic
prescriptions, and in connection with a single electronic platform. The baseline for 2020 is 82%, with
100% to be achieved in 2030.
    The objectives of this secondary goal are the need to improve the architecture and maintenance of
the electronic health system, the availability of a single repository of health data (including medical
records and national registries), and low use of electronic health solutions (e-health solutions), such as
electronic prescriptions.
    Subordinate goal 5.3. Promoting the implementation of digital health technologies and telemedicine
services. Outcome indicator: Proportion of primary centers in rural areas using digital e-health technolo-
gies in their daily activities. The base rate for 2020 was 6.5%, the availability of 50 primary healthcare
centers, and the target rate for 2030 was 100%. A significant challenge in achieving success is the limited
use of mobile and digital technologies in healthcare.
    Telemedicine has played an essential role in combating the spread of the coronavirus pandemic. It
promoted physical distancing and remote treatment of illnesses at the primary healthcare level. Telemedi-
cine platforms have proven ideal for combating infectious disease outbreaks. However, with their help,
medical personnel, when communicating remotely with a patient, only in some instances determine in
which case the patient’s condition can be remotely managed and when he needs to visit a medical facility.
    REDMED is the first innovative telemedicine platform in Georgia, with which you can choose the
desired doctor based on his rating and ratings of other patients. The company unites 300 doctors and 20
medical areas. Provides telephone and video consultation with a doctor or a visit to the apartment and
the purchase of necessary pharmaceutical products through courier delivery at the PSP pharmacy. The
doctor issues the card during a home visit to the patient after issuing a prescription. Within 24 hours
from the date of the visit, to check the information, the patient can use the application to ask questions
to the doctor and receive timely feedback (REDMED, n.d.).
    It is equally important to support the creation of creative products related to digitalization in the
Georgian higher education system. DigitalMed is the first Georgian system based on artificial intelli-
gence for the medical field, which speaks to you in Georgian and has the ability to process and analyze
the information received and, as a result, make a primary diagnosis, recommend the patient to visit
the clinic and the appropriate doctor. The product, created with the help of specially developed unique
algorithms, is exceptional and replaces diagnostic procedures and doctor consultations with new ap-
proaches. (DigitalMed project, n.d.) It was built on the medical portal journal. ge, which offers the use
of the product to all site visitors.
    Fifty outpatient clinics in Georgian villages are already equipped with digital equipment, and the
introduction of digital technologies in rural areas is expanding (based on a UN project and financial
assistance from the European Union).
    Mobile health services and health applications have great potential in the development of health-
related information and services. Mobile medical services for vaccination, intelligent monitoring, and
other services are already operational. To support existing activities, economic policies and legislation
related to health care are already being developed, as well as the development of appropriately qualified
personnel, technical support, and coordination. Significant efforts are needed to reduce inequalities in
access to e-health services in rural areas, urban settlements, and various demographic and social groups
(refugees, ethnic minorities, socially vulnerable people, and the LGBTQ group).


                                                                                                        231
                                                                                                          ﻿
                                               Policy for Digitalization of the Georgian Healthcare System




    With technical support from the World Bank, an e-health strategy is being developed to create an
appropriate, secure digital environment. At the same time, work is underway to make fiscal decisions
to limit existing infrastructure and financing challenges.
    Georgia has actively begun work to improve the capacity of medical personnel, digital technologies,
and e-health. These activities should be implemented by developing and implementing eHealth-focused
training programs. It is obvious that the use of media plays a vital role in increasing the efficiency of
processes associated with the digitalization of the healthcare system. The provision of services by medical
personnel, healthcare organizations, and medical associations must have comprehensive characteristics.
In this regard, policymakers must always consider the behavioral culture of economic interest groups
and the population.
    Following the development of telemedicine and artificial intelligence in Georgia, the following
problems and risks have been identified:

1.    In most mountainous regions and villages of Georgia, there is a problem with access to high-speed
      Internet;
2.    Georgian legislation does not regulate telemedicine services and mainly considers telemedicine
      a teleconsultation between two doctors. During the pandemic, the provision of telemedicine care
      directly between the doctor and the patient has increased, so in some instances, some rules have
      often had to be relaxed;
3.    It is essential to develop guidelines for telehealth services (for example, minimum standard of pa-
      tient screening), develop quality management indicators, and reflect them in healthcare programs.
      To date, this work is progressing, but very slowly;
4.    A decision was made to introduce a number of telemedicine services, including preventive and
      door-to-door telemonitoring, cost-effective services, and products adapted abroad from the point
      of view of their integration into insurance packages. Although the decision has been made, it has
      not yet been implemented;
5.    In the digital health development policy, it is necessary to take into account the problem of digital
      inequality - the healthcare of those individuals who, at this stage, cannot be covered by telemedicine
      services due to infrastructural or technological limitations;
6.    Against the backdrop of the spread of electronic histories and electronic insurance, millions of
      personal data (results of examinations, analyses, and consultations) are posted on the portal of
      the Ministry of Health and the servers of various providers in the virtual space. This is a potential
      target for cyberhackers who can cause financial, reputational, or political damage. At this stage
      of development of the economic digitalization policy in Georgia, there is no special legislation
      that government agencies could use to regulate software services containing artificial intelligence.
      However, in some other areas, the Constitution of Georgia and other legislative acts establish sev-
      eral requirements for regulatory services that government agencies apply to the use of artificial
      intelligence. To eliminate the problems and risks associated with the development of telemedicine
      and artificial intelligence in healthcare, the following measures must be taken:
      ◦◦     Access to broadband (high-speed) Internet needs to be expanded to facilitate accelerated de-
             velopment of e-health. Coordination of high-speed (broadband) Internet and affordable tariff
             policy for the population in remote, mountainous, and border areas must be implemented.
             It is also essential to promote the availability of technological equipment necessary for the
             implementation of telemedicine in primary healthcare centers and hospitals in these regions;


232
﻿
Policy for Digitalization of the Georgian Healthcare System




     ◦◦   Existing electronic programs, electronic records, insurance platforms, and communication
          systems in Georgia should implement the following:
          a) assessing the level of confidentiality and security of patient data and making appropriate
                 changes based on the creation of unified mechanisms for protecting information systems;
          b) consideration of modern approaches to cybersecurity and reflection of this problem in
                 the strategic plan for national cybersecurity;
          c) ensuring high standards of data protection, privacy, and cybersecurity in the healthcare
                 sector in Georgia;
          d) preparation and implementation of a strategic plan for the development of e-health,
                 mHealth, and telemedicine and related regulatory changes (note: during the pandemic, it
                 is desirable to maintain a less restrictive regulatory regime in order to achieve maximum
                 effect from services, the main thing is to ensure the security of data and networks.);
     ◦◦   It is strengthening cooperation with the European Union in e-health, mobile health, and tele-
          medicine. This needs to be done by assessing the improvement and effectiveness of e-health
          applications and systems in combating the pandemic. Also, the main directions that have
          already been established in the documents of the European Commission can be considered
          components of the regulation and route plan of the designated territory for Georgia. In par-
          ticular, the electronic platform www.geocase.ge should become (international interoperabil-
          ity of healthcare systems, harmonization of electronic history standards, quality standards,
          reliability, and data security, use of big data in public health, epidemiological surveillance
          and contact tracing applications, mobile implementation of telemedicine strategies and tele-
          monitoring, effective and safe use of personal electronic medical records, networks and re-
          sources of remote psychiatric care, artificial intelligence, etc.) as the basis for the most ac-
          celerated development of patient-centric integrated e-health through digital innovation.
     ◦◦   Expansion of joint projects of modern applications and innovative technologies that contrib-
          ute to health security in the future long-term scientific framework of the European Union -
          Horizon Europe - a program based on considering network cooperation in long-term plans.
          The global pandemic health crisis has provided an unexpected opportunity to unlock the
          power of health systems, both by testing good practices and identifying vulnerabilities and
          problems. Georgia needs to make the most of this chance;
     ◦◦   Georgia is a country with a small open economy and a developing market, where up to 0.5
          million citizens of Georgia and foreign countries live as refugees. Among them are many
          cyber criminals who have already tried to commit crimes on Georgian territory. We hope that
          the Georgian government (as appropriate) will develop capabilities to combat and prevent
          cybercrime in the healthcare system at an accelerated pace with the help of artificial intel-
          ligence technologies.


CONCLUSION

Over the past ten years, due to reforms to the health system, overall health expenditure and government
budget allocations have increased significantly. However, the risk of impoverishment due to healthcare
costs remains high for citizens.




                                                                                                      233
                                                                                                         ﻿
                                              Policy for Digitalization of the Georgian Healthcare System




    In order to increase the economic efficiency of the healthcare system, modern quality control and
compensation systems for medical institutions have been introduced, including the “Payment for Re-
sults” model. The development of the primary healthcare system and the expansion of its functions are
paramount for the financial sustainability of the healthcare system and the control of rising costs.
    In preventive services, it is desirable to invest more financial resources to strengthen primary, sec-
ondary, and tertiary prevention of diseases with the highest burden of morbidity and mortality. This will
protect populations with chronic diseases from very high healthcare costs.
    Simultaneously with the development of a market economy in Georgia, mass privatization of medical
institutions was carried out, which lagged behind the development of their regulatory framework. The
legal mechanisms for the digitalization of the healthcare system also need to catch up to the require-
ments for regulating the results of projects and reforms we face in relations with the European Union and
other international organizations. Problems arise for patients in obtaining safe and high-quality medical
services and for doctors regarding their rights to protection, which exist in the context of systematic
analysis and management of medical errors.
    The problem is the implementation of generally accepted methods for ensuring the quality of medical
care. Regarding infrastructure and human resources, existing quality and safety mechanisms (permits,
licenses, and certification systems) need to be improved, expanded, and continuously improved moni-
toring mechanisms.
    Assessing the quality of care and implementing evidence-based practices at the level of medical
institutions could be better. Problems associated with qualified personnel (resources) and their uneven
geographical distribution represent severe obstacles to implementing quality health services. There are
enough qualified doctors in Georgia, but very few highly qualified specialists are among them. Today, a
highly qualified specialist accepts an offer from abroad and leaves Georgia. Digitalization of the Geor-
gian healthcare system will slow down this process, as doctors will be allowed to work on outsourcing
based on the introduction of telemedicine.
    It is crucial to constantly develop evidence-based systems to support clinical practice and adapt them
to modern requirements. It should include the continuous development and improvement of mechanisms
for standardization, financing, and monitoring of both education and medical services.
    Particular attention should be paid (through economic policy instruments such as taxes, national
qualification standards, etc.) to the smooth operation of statistical and surveillance systems. There
should be a uniform commitment, monitoring, and evaluation policy for public and private institutions
in public health programs, excluding any benefits. Where we are dealing with people’s health, lobbying
is not allowed.

                                                     Chapter 14
  Strategic and Positive Business
  Communication for Healthcare
                                                    Lela Abdushelishvili
                                         International Black Sea University, Georgia


ABSTRACT
The healthcare field is full of various challenges, such as implementing new technologies, not enough
skilled workforce, stress, and anxiety. Patients’ ability to communicate effectively may also be affected
by their condition, medication, pain, and/or anxiety. Therefore, what is needed for the healthcare profes-
sionals is good strategic communication skills and maintaining positivity and simplicity to ensure patient
safety, wellbeing, and the overall efficiency of busy healthcare organizations. Besides, serious problems
may arise from poor quality communication. If professional communication skills vary in quality, then
a patient’s understanding of their health status may lack context, completeness, and accuracy – often
causing confusion and potential harm. Communication failures in hospitals and medical practices are
responsible for malpractice claims, resulting in deaths and high costs.



1. INTRODUCTION

Healthcare industry went through serious changes over the past two decades: COVID-19 pandemic,
overloaded hospitals, last-minute changing circumstances and a lot of anxiety. For the first time in history,
a health crisis has shut down the entire global economy. This made healthcare institutions and profes-
sionals move toward value-based care, improve overall wellness of population and decrease illnesses
and complications. Besides, there were reported cases of information overload, unclearly structured
messages and statements which confused people and let them feel stressed and frustrated. So, it became
extremely essential to clearly, positively and strategically communicate and pass messages with the view
of leading to win-win solutions and outcomes as well as develop positive attitudes and be able to handle
critical situations effectively in both face-to-face and online/digital communication.
    COVID-19 pandemic significantly contributed to the change of the healthcare environment: proce-
dures were quickly altered, hospitals found themselves confronted with the shortage of staff, there was
high demand on quick information analysis and making timely and critical decisions. During 2020, the

DOI: 10.4018/979-8-3693-0679-6.ch014

﻿
Strategic and Positive Business Communication for Healthcare




pandemic has become a major health concern in the whole world. In fact, the number of those who died
of this infection amounts to two million.
    Additional challenge came up as a result of big technological innovations: strong digital platforms
became extremely necessary, a lot of data accumulated and its privacy issues arose, deep fake content
creation was reported, too much focus on automation was made. Besides, patients demanded doctors
to meet their expectations and even serve as heroes, experts, creators of positive patient experience and
just close friends of theirs in order to ensure positivity and calm atmosphere. The added challenge was
clinical communication practices changed by this infection. In other words, the regular face-to-face com-
munication changed into remote and distance practices. This led to certain barriers to communication as
almost all consultations became video based and the use of virtual assistants emerged.
    Notably, COVID-19 created such challenges that the field of healthcare has never experienced before
and some big changes for the better emerged. One of them is developing resilience and innovation. In
other words, use of telehealth emerged. If before video healthcare visits were not regarded useful, now
they proved to be otherwise. Besides, the amount of requests for such virtual visits required technology
update and simplification of the process to meet the needs. Therefore, online consultations were used
intensively and, in fact, they proved to be successful which helped healthcare establishments to move
towards a good balance of face-to-face and distance appointments and, thus, provide even more effec-
tive consultations.
    Because of the increased need, people began working in joint cooperation and attempted to defeat
the crisis which led to breaking down hierarchy and promoted more intense sense of cooperation and
collaboration.
    One negative outcome of the pandemic was the issue with mental health. In other
words, people were afraid of becoming ill and they had practically no interaction which in-
creased anxiety and boredom. As a result, negative emotions accumulated inside them and
the need for sharing their stories arose. As John Bonamo, MD, Chief Medical and Qual-
ity Officer at RWJ Barnabas Health (https://www.rwjbh.org/blog/2022/january/how-covid-19-is-resh
aping-healthcare/) stated, “Everybody has a story and it is important for us to take the time to listen and,
if necessary, become an advocate or a resource for the patient”. Indeed, listening to the patient and his/
her story, demonstrating to him/her that they are cared for and listened to makes significant impact on
his/her mental health and, thus, sets him/her for positivity.
    COVID-19 advised medical field representatives to be flexible. What this meant was that old regula-
tions and rules no more were applied and the so-called functional flexibility came in which meant giving
different roles to different employees. This also is linked to job rotation which encompasses workers
taking various tasks related to their shift working schemes or special requirements and demands of the
medical institution.
    Thus, taking into consideration all the above-stated, anxiety rose, many last-minute changing cir-
cumstances arose, psychological state of individuals got critical and, therefore, effective strategic com-
munication skills and maintaining positivity and simplicity proved to be extremely significant to ensure
patient wellbeing, calm and quiet interaction between colleagues and provide timely and quality service.
    Undoubtedly, communication lies at the heart of effective healthcare. In a fast-paced and complex
industry, the art of communication is extremely significant for healthcare leaders to ensure engagement,
make their teams more empowered and activate a culture of transparency.
    It is due to poor communication that the potential for medical errors is increased and a negative work-
ing environment is created. This leads to a lower quality of patient care which has a negative effect on


                                                                                                        237
                                                                                                     ﻿
                                          Strategic and Positive Business Communication for Healthcare




clinical outcomes. When health care professionals are not communicating effectively, patient safety is
at risk for several reasons: lack of critical information, misinterpretation of information, unclear orders
over the telephone, and overlooked changes in status. Lack of communication creates situations where
medical errors can occur. Therefore, special strategies and techniques used will make sure the situation
is more or less coordinated and taken care of.


2. STRATEGIC AND POSITIVE BUSINESS COMMUNICATION
FOR HEALTHCARE PROFESSIONALS

Effective communication can have a positive impact on the relationships between the patient and the
provider dynamic. It lets patients know better what the condition of their own health is and, thus, be able
to plan and implement health and wellness strategies. It can also help patients develop a comprehensive
understanding of current care strategies involving their health, including acute health situations.
    Communication can also help develop a sense of trust between the patient and provider, which
might make it easier for patients to adhere to a provider’s recommendations. These elements can lead
to several key potential long-term benefits, such as reduced patient visits, reduced costs and improved
patient outcomes.
    The increased presence of technology makes effective communication even more important in health
care. It can help mitigate the potential impact of health misinformation that could otherwise lead patients
to make poor or improper decisions about their health. It can also help improve communication among
other health care professionals who may need to coordinate care across multiple facilities. This can also
lead to improved opportunities for delivering care.
    Because technology constantly evolves, it is crucial to periodically reinvigorate communication for
health care providers to meet patient expectations. This will help to avoid further misunderstandings
and crisis.
    Besides, patient portals developed by healthcare facilities will allow patients to get access to health
care information. Educational health information can be placed on the patient’s portal that is relevant to
the patient’s specific health needs. Clinical summaries can be made accessible. These summaries can
be a review of the patient’s last appointment or a summary of recent test results with explanations. The
summaries can also include recommendations, such as the need for follow-up appointments.
    Using simple language helps to overcome the challenges. Language is the most powerful tool of com-
munication. Communication of ideas, thoughts, opinions and emotional expressions, social interaction,
using the power of sound help to maintain effective communication in healthcare and ensure positivity.
    When a healthcare worker communicates effectively, s/he makes sure every patient is approached
individually, respectfully and with the view of understanding his/her concerns or worries as well as
thoughts and opinions. In this process such skills as active listening, paying attention, gathering main
points come to the front.
    For nurses, good communication in healthcare means approaching every patient interaction with the
intention to understand the patient’s concerns, experiences, and opinions. This includes using verbal and
nonverbal communication skills, along with active techniques. As a result, interaction is maintained,
engagement is made, active listening is present, complaints are heard, engagement is provided, feedback
is encouraged and the culture of collaboration and empowerment is enhanced.




238
﻿
Strategic and Positive Business Communication for Healthcare




    Communication with patients and their family members enhances patient experience and even leads
to better outcomes. In this process it is essential to emphasize the importance of active listening and use
of simple language to make sure that the information given is understood easily.
    In the era of digital, technological progress and advancement a lot of tools are at the disposal of
healthcare professionals to provide better and professional communication since effective communication
is the main backbone or successful operation in the healthcare field. Therefore, it is essential to engage
and communicate effectively with patients and their families, promote transparency, enhance patient
engagement and lead them to better outcomes. This helps to create a collaborative and patient-centered
environment thus leading to effective communication towards positive development and change.
    Improving communication in healthcare will not only help to ensure the best level of care is provided
to patients and clinical outcomes are improved, effective and efficient communication helps to drive
down the cost of healthcare provision.
    One of the most effective communication strategies to improve patient safety is to structure con-
versations to ensure information is accurately transferred between two people and a useful approach
is SBAR, an acronym for Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation. Michael Leonard,
MD, Physician Leader for Patient Safety, along with colleagues Doug Bonacum and Suzanne Graham
at Kaiser Permanente of Colorado (Evergreen, Colorado, USA) developed this technique. The SBAR
technique has been implemented widely at health systems such as Kaiser Permanente. Please provide
your information to download the SBAR Tool.
    This approach is particularly useful for nurse-nurse communications during shift handovers and for
nurse-physician conversations.
    Conversations should start with Situation – who is calling, to which patient the call refers, and the
reason for the call. Concise Background information is then provided about the patient and his/her con-
dition. Information is then provided about the Assessment of the patient. The caller then provides their
Recommendation or asks for recommendations on the best course of action to take. The person who
initiates the call then asks the receiver to repeat the information to make sure it has been understood.
    Adding structure to the conversation ensures relevant information is rapidly, accurately, and efficiently
transferred. It is also useful getting people to think about what they are about to communicate beforehand
to ensure all the necessary information is passed on.


3. THE STICC AND BATHE PROTOCOLS IN COMMUNICATION
STRATEGIES FOR HEALTHCARE

The STICC protocol has been adopted in several industries for providing structure to briefings and
ensuring critical information has been understood.

•    Situation – Explain the situation and the problem
•    Task – Explain the recommended course of action
•    Intent – Explain why that action should be taken
•    Concern – Explain any issues that may arise and what to look out for
•    Calibrate – Invite the person to say if they do not understand. Ask if there is any reason why an
     action cannot be taken and for the receiver to express any concerns.




                                                                                                         239
                                                                                                      ﻿
                                           Strategic and Positive Business Communication for Healthcare




   Effective communication with patients is essential for passing on essential information and making
sure that information is understood. Good communication also improves the patient experience, which
improves patient satisfaction. There are several communication strategies in healthcare that can be ad-
opted to improve communication with patients.
   The BATHE Protocol-is a useful for establishing a rapport with a patient and to put them at their
ease. The purpose of this communication strategy is not to solve any problems the patient has. Instead
the aim is to give the patient reassurances and to listen to their concerns. This approach has proved to
be effective in outpatient settings at improving the patient experience and patient satisfaction scores.

•     Background – What is going on in your life?
•     Affect – How does that affect you?
•     Troubles – What troubles are you experiencing?
•     Handling – How are you handling them?
•     Empathetic Statement – E.g. That must be very difficult for you.

   The AWARE Approach to Patient Interaction - is a useful protocol for improving the patient experi-
ence. Healthcare professionals should Announce their presence, Welcome the patient and smile, Ask if
there is anything the patient needs, Review the interaction and explain why it was important and when
the patient will need to be seen again, then Exit with kindness.
   Patient teach back is a useful communication strategy in healthcare that nurses and physicians can
adopt to make sure important information has been effectively communicated and understood by the
patient. Patients may say they have understood what they have been told even when they are not totally
clear about what has been said. This approach helps to make sure that important information has been
properly understood. This communication strategy can help to prevent readmissions. After communicat-
ing information to a patient, ask the patient to repeat back what they have been told in their own words.


4. THE IMPORTANCE OF PATIENT SATISFACTION IN HEALTHCARE

The importance of patient satisfaction in healthcare cannot be overstated. When patients have a high
level of satisfaction with the care they receive from a healthcare provider, patient outcomes are likely to
be better. Patients are also more likely to remain with the same healthcare provider. If a patient has a bad
experience, he/she could change providers and share the bad experience on social media, the internet, or
word of mouth. That could easily make it harder for healthcare providers to attract patients.
    Patient satisfaction is a good measure of quality of care, which is why a percentage of reimbursement
for Medicare is linked to patient satisfaction scores from Hospital Consumer Assessment of Healthcare
Providers and Systems (HCAHPS) surveys.

4.1. Take Advantage of New Communication Tools for Patient Satisfaction

These communication strategies in healthcare will help to ensure information is transferred effectively
and efficiently; however, communication strategies in healthcare will only go so far. Healthcare organi-
zations also need to provide modern communication systems to facilitate better communication. Today,
smartphones and other portable electronic devices allow people to communicate quickly, effectively,


240
﻿
Strategic and Positive Business Communication for Healthcare




and confidentially in the outside world. These technologies can also be used to improve communication
in healthcare, although many hospitals are still reliant on one-way communication tools such as pagers
and faxes. A great deal of time is wasted in healthcare playing phone tag, chasing up physicians and
nurses to make sure information has been received.
    HIPAA-compliant text messaging platforms allow all members of a care team to communicate with
each other efficiently and effectively. These platforms allow healthcare professionals to keep all mem-
bers of the care team in the loop and ensure information is communicated with the right people at the
right time. These platforms can support text, audio, and video calls and can be used for quick consults,
to contact any member of the care team, to transfer information accurately at shift changes and other
patient handoffs, and allow healthcare providers to easily provide telehealth services to remote patients.
    Healthcare providers that have replaced pagers and faxes with these advanced healthcare communi-
cations solutions have been able to improve efficiency and productivity, increase staff morale, reduce
medical errors, improve patient satisfaction scores, and cut costs.
    One of the most effective communication strategies to improve patient safety is to structure con-
versations to ensure information is accurately transferred between two people. One of the most useful
approaches is SBAR, an acronym for Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation.
    The key purpose of strategies is to add structure to communications. Consequently, each of these
strategies can be adapted to suit specific circumstances provided the strategy remains consistent to
ensure that handovers, briefings, and consultations follow the same patterns to prevent omissions or
misunderstandings.
    Because of the different nature of the scenarios, there is no one-size-fits-all communication strategy
in healthcare that is suitable for all scenarios and trying to tailor one strategy to address organization-
wide communication issues could create more problems than it solves.
    While some strategies do not provide the opportunity for questions or objections, their purpose is to
communication a course of action, justify it, and ensure the course of action has been understood. Other
strategies are more interactive inasmuch as allow for questions and/or objections. Teams should decide
which strategy (or version of the strategy) is appropriate for specific scenarios.
    With some communication tools, teams can upload templates to ensure each element of the com-
munication is covered and nothing is omitted or overlooked. This can be a very useful feature during
handovers when stressful situations could result in forgetfulness or shortcuts being taken that might
mitigate the effectiveness of the communication strategy.


5. CONCLUSION

The 21st century in the time of active hybrid communications. The physical and digital channels of com-
munication between the doctor and patient already competing with each other (Gigauri & Djakeli, 2021).
Many patients to rationalize the use of the time already turn in the side of virtual e-visits to physicians.
The generational marketing shows us how it is important to know more insides about the patients. For
example, the patients of generation Z, have high willingness of online booking of the visit to the doctor
and digital engagement from the side of health organization. But members of X generation, still continue
to lead in physical, verbal communication with gatekeeper medicine, their doctors, family physicians,
representatives of secondary healthcare. Even if healthcare will be innovatively developed to the new
era of virtual surgeries, and this type of surgery operations will be in guidelines of well developed, high


                                                                                                        241
                                                                                                    ﻿
                                         Strategic and Positive Business Communication for Healthcare




industrial countries (Djakeli, 2012). Still baby boomer and generation x members will continue to wish
all medicine into form of physical communication. That’s why we see that communication has many
problems and barriers. Various types of communication barriers in healthcare are physical and envi-
ronmental, physical or biological, language, personal, emotional, socio-psychological, cultural. They
significantly affect the process and outcome and hinder efficiency. From this perspective, strategic and
positive communication plays a significant role to overcome those barriers and challenges and ensure
positive and flawless environment to provide wellbeing and room for further development.
    Thus, the scientific research about the healthcare communication must be focused on effective ways
of overcoming psychological, behavioral and economic barriers to provide clinics and individuals with
tangible and useful evidence based knowledge on how to become more trustful, mutually understandable,
efficient and effective to achieve the positive long term communication with patient.

About the Contributors




    Kakhaber Djakeli was Fulbright Scholar at Yale University in the School of Public Health dur-
ing 2006-2007. He received the Research Scholarship of German Academic Exchange Service during,
1998, 2004, 2009, 2013 and. 2016 and worked at Leibniz University of Hanover LUH, He delivered
his classes in American and German Universities, during 2003-2020. He is now an associate professor
at University of Georgia, in Georgia and a visiting Professor at Lublin University in Poland. Kakhaber
Djakeli publish his works about Marketing, Health Economy, Health marketing, Healthcare reforming.

    Mamatha A. is working as Assistant Professor in Department of Computer Science and Engineering,

   Pilar Alarcón Urbistondo was born in Malaga on June 20th, 1965. Degree in Economics and Busi-
ness Sciences from the University of Malaga. Doctor in Business Administration and Management from
the University of Malaga and Professor of Marketing and Market Research of the University of Malaga
where she also develops her teaching, research and transfer work. Her main line of work focuses on the
identification and assessment of target markets and the description of the behaviors and perceptions of
their consumers applied fundamentally to tourist destinations, although currently she works on these
aspects in other public fields such as culture (museums) and public heath (hospitals). At the same time,
she extends her work to the identification of vulnerabilities in the offer, B&B relationships and reputation
in order to develope competitive intelligence (tools for obtaining and analyzing information from the
environment) for decision-making by public agencies in their relations with the different stakeholders.
Co-author of more than 15 book chapters, 30 articles in national, international and sectorial fields out
of which 20 of them are included in magazines indexed of maximum level (Ranking JCR: Q1 y Q2) and
35 papers sent to national and international conferences. She participates as a researcher in more than
35 R & D & I international, national and regional research projects and contracts, of which in 25 she
has been Responsible Investigator.

   Mohamad Zreik, a Postdoctoral Fellow at Sun Yat-sen University, is a recognized scholar in Inter-
national Relations, specializing in China’s Arab-region foreign policy. His recent work in soft power
diplomacy compares China’s methods in the Middle East and East Asia. His extensive knowledge spans
Middle Eastern Studies, China-Arab relations, East Asian and Asian Affairs, Eurasian geopolitics, and
Political Economy, providing him a unique viewpoint in his field. Dr. Zreik is a proud recipient of a
PhD from Central China Normal University (Wuhan). He’s written numerous acclaimed papers, many
focusing on China’s Belt and Road Initiative and its Arab-region impact. His groundbreaking research
has established him as a leading expert in his field. Presently, he furthers his research on China’s soft
power diplomacy tactics at Sun Yat-sen University. His significant contributions make him a crucial
figure in understanding contemporary international relations.
